Comment: Japanese bridge being renovated Very disappointing - the bridge was still undergoing renovations which were supposed to have been finished. Not much to see.

Was looking forward for the obligatory photo of this ancient bridge, surprised to turn the corner and see that it’s under renovation and would be for years. Nothing to see as you can pass through, don’t waste your time or expectations.

The bridge is under renovations and is covered by a big shed. You can go up close to see the construction but there is nothing much. Definitely not photo worthy. Would suggest to visit only after all the renovations are done.
It’s fully covered 🥺 … as in under construction. There’s an opening that one can peak what was the Japanese covered bridge.
Completely covered up within a tin shed and under reconstruction. Nothing to see except some explanation posters and a lot of workmen.
It was the symbol of the city, with the Chinese neighborhood on one side and the Japanese neighborhood on the other. It's a shame that it's going to take a while to fix it because it's destroyed.
It’s a construction site, very little of the bridge can be seen and yet tickets are still Required. It’s a waste of time and money so avoid

Bridge under construction I confirm, the bridge is completely under construction and I think it will last for several more years. Also avoid visiting the city during Chinese New Year as all the sites to visit are closed.
This bridge is under construction, it is completely dismantled, and cannot be visited. Instead, you will find scaffolding and barriers.

I have visited Hoi An and the covered bridge many times over the years. This was the worst I have seen it. It was undergoing much needed restoration - this was not the problem. The hoards of tourists swarming around and over it were. It was just symbolic of Hoi An being loved to death and Old Town being choked by too many people. I realise that there is some irony here - as I too am one of them - but it is sad to see little official control of the numbers of tourists who make the streets impassable and have ruined the atmosphere.

Unfortunately the Japanese bridge is under construction, no one had told us about it and we were a little disappointed… fortunately the rest of this city is truly incredible with a unique atmosphere. And these lanterns are splendid!
Please don't go there until you know for sure it has finished refurbishing/renovating/preserving this bridge. Like most things, if you don't care to maintain them, they will fall in disrepair.

Unfortunately, a visit to the Japanese bridge is currently out of the question. The bridge is being completely renovated and there is nothing to see at the moment. Because of the construction site, I have the feeling that this is a long-term project and that it will take years before the bridge can be seen in its full glory again.
Bridge being re-built The bridge in being re-constructed and is entirely enclosed with metal sheeting. You can see the damaged remains of the bridge.

Don't pay to see Rubble What Bridge lol. We went to see it , we were told we had to.pay to see it. So we paid money to look at a pile of rubble. Don't waste your time or money. Unless it gets restored, at this moment it is latterly rubble.

We visited HOI an Sep 23 and Japanese bridge been demolished and is under repair so will take a while to be rebuilt. Did not spoil our visit as still plenty to see and did see the Japanese bridge on prior visit.

Bridge covered under construction as of 6/1/23 As of 6/1/23, the Japanese Bridge was covered under construction (see photos). I went to Hoi An to get a photo of the Japanese Bridge and was disappointed. Just FYI. Check to see if the construction is completed before going.

Everyone goes there just for sake of going as it’s supposed to be a landmark. Nothing here just an ordinary old crappy bridge which is always super crowded. Absolute waste of time, markets surrounding it are worth exploring though

Just a covered bridge After reading all the hype on the Japanese covered bridge it was a disappointment. It’s nothing but an old bridge. Yes it was historical but it was about 10 metres across and that was it. Looked better from the outside to get photos than the inside. If you go at the wrong time of the day they have it guarded and you will need a ticket to cross. We had to buy a ticket to even enter the old town which cost about $6, not of a lot of money but we had been told that we would only need a ticket if we wanted to go to certain buildings or the old Japanese bridge. Not sure what goes on but it is Vietnam and just go with it.
The canal reeks and has garbage floating in it, which doesn't make the already lacking aesthetics of the bridge any more pleasing.

Nothing impressive here The bridge looks better in photos than real life. Dirty water and they charge you to walk across it (about 30 steps and it's done). There's a bridge further towards the river you can walk over for free.

mmmh sorry, it’s just a old bridge and not more. You need a ticket, which coast you $5,00 to use the bridge and enter the old town. The Problem, behind the bridge you only will see a lot of shops, which you also find outside. On the bridge you see, by feeling, 1000 people who need to make photos. Price is definitely to high
Smelly tiny bridge that isn’t that big of a deal. Costs $120,000 dong to cross it during the day one way but is free at night.
Small bridge and very crowded Don’t understand why everyone recommend and what they were talking about
Too crowded. can’t walk. Smelly. Too touristy for our taste. Obnoxious. beautiful at night but definitely geared towards tourist. Watch your bags

We wandered around old Town and just wanted to check out the bridge, which isn't that impressive to begin with, and then we saw that they charged for it. That is absolutely ridiculous, it's such a small bridge and yeah, Japanese made blah whatever, but seriously!? We just turned back and didn't care anymore. We got used to pay for almost anything here in Vietnam, which is annoying already, but that just topped everything.

They expect you to pay to cross an old covered wooden bridge what should be right of way between two public roads. Nothing to see of note inside. Save your money and take your selfies using it as a backdrop.
We had more enjoyment looking at it from another position. You can cross this water in several places. Crossing the bridge at night was better as fewer tourists we found.
It's just a bridge. An old bridge. No need to pay just to cross that bridge. Quite old and very ordinary looking.
Nothing much to say it’s a run down bridge that assists you crossing one side of a river to the other

Short "Japanese" bridge This is a short "Japanese" bridge but an Old Chinese Ancestors Temple was built on/beside this bridge. Not worth to pay for the entrance fee just to see or cross over this bridge although many touristswith VN Guide over-crowded on this short bridge.
I’m not impressed there are so much more to see in Hoi An and this is not a must. Suggest the old town and the board walk at night instead.
This was for sure an overated part of the ancient city. Nothing but tourists taking Instagram photos. Do some research and get out and see the non touristy parts of Hoi An.

Boring but nearby shops are cool. Who buys a ticket to cross a bridge, unless of course there is a troll underneath. It’s a bridge. Sure it’s old but really not very interesting. I actually deleted most of my pictures.
It's nothing, just see it from the outside. And use the free bridge 50 meters from there. There is so many turist
Not really big + you pay to pass a bridge that is 10m long. You can see the interior by standing just outside. No written explanation for this historical place.
You can enter this bridge from one side and pay a small fee or cross the foot bridge 20meters away and enter from the other side for free. Odd but nice looking bridge.

If you haven't already read other reviews with this complaint then from one side of the bridge you have to pay for a ticket. Don't go at night as it is outrageously packed with Chinese and you won't get a quality picture. The guard seemed to stop every European walking through, though the Asians tourists just walking in freely. Our accommodation was on the other side of the bridge and I explained we simply needed to walk through but we were told to go around then loop back in the other entrance (roughly a 10 minute walk). I would just make sure you're entering from the pay free side of the small bridge.
If you enter to cross the bridge without paying noncence money then walk over it from the north entrance. They charge Money Form the south, ans the best pictures to take are from the outside.
Ten meter long bridge where you have to pay for it. Seems like they only asking tickets from tourists, locals are walking pass through without any ticket. So Vietnam!!

Great icon BUT and its a big BUT There is a charge to visit the Bridge but if you are, like us there for a long stay, then being hassled each time we were going to and from our friend's Optical shop was to say the least very annoying. Several times we had to go around or through a restuarant on the river or down a side lane. Tickets should be a LOT better managed and some kind of system /badge/ card for long term visitors.

Get a photo from the outside & move on The bridge has history, I get that. But charging people to walk the very short distance inside?! Come on! You’d be better off getting a picture from the outside & google searching its history!
Really don’t bother visiting. Don’t bother visiting this place. I didn’t find anything special other than market of different stuff

Chances are you will pass this while in old town. If they want to charge you for a ticket just skip it or walk down the alley on either side and cross from the other direction. Only interesting if you are eeally into old wooden bridges and slightly interesting history.
Nice but that's all. Not worth the visit. Whole town visit for a day is enough. Over the day the shops but with high prices and in the evening the night market with food and souvenirs.

Didn't understand the fascination with the bridge personally and officials sometimes refuse to let you pass through without paying even though you have passed through several times before for free. Extremely frustrating when you are trying to escape the crowds by the waterfront and want to go back to your hotel but instead have to walk around.

If you are walking through the city at some time you will pass by this bridge which I do not understand why they charge to visit. The river we pass through is super polluted and smells bad. Only worth the visit if you are not doing anything and with some leftover ticket

I was very nervous when I tried to get in the bridge structure. The guard just stoped me and asked me for the tickets saying that I have to paid for it. However, a bunch of asians were just passing by him and he was not charging them the tickets. When I asked him why, he just answered saying they were Vietnamese! How do you know was my question to him and I just received an impolite head gesture like "it doesn't matter! It is ok to me to pay for tickets to visit the places in te old city but I cannot stand with this irresponsible answer and attitude to make all other non-Asian foreigners to pay for the city maintenance! A bunch of Chinese everywhere enjoying their holidays for new year and just non-asians have to pay? Definitely not! I did not enter there at all! Just enjoyed the structure outside but could not stay that much because all the city wastewater comes to this river below the bridge which brings a very very bad smell! I loved Hoi An, for sure, but not this attitude to stop me while a bunch of other Asian tourist that were not having their nationality checked out!

Overrated, unfriendly ticket controllers Stayed around Old Town for 4 nights. Bought a ticket to view the historic sites. BUT couldn’t believe how rude the ticket takers at the bridge were! One lady even yelled at me “no ticket, no see!” I had already seen the bridge. I was just trying to walk through to get to my hotel! The tickets are supposed to be a donation. But they were very pushy... watched them bully many people about buying a ticket just to walk through.

Stupid guard won't let you pass We tried to go across and the guard wanted to take a ticket just for going across Wich is not ok because the bridge is free. We had to go around and entered the other way of the street and exit across the bridge.
Ok to walk through for free. Do not pay money for this. Just go around. They wanted app $5usd to walk though.
Too crowded place, nothing to experience. Lady made you to buy a ticket even to cross. Useless activity.

Hordes of tourist wait to cross and endlessly looking for their perfect selfie - you really get over the everyone expecting you to wait for them to finish taking their perfect photos... Really not sure what the fuss is about. A small room does have some information explaining about the Chinese and Japanese traders. But other than that, pretty picture but not that you’d visit he town for.

You could give it a miss We walked over the bridge just to get to the other side of town. I had no idea it was a tourist attraction until the woman in front of asked got stopped by what looked like security and told she had to pay. The bridge is pretty, but I wouldn't pay to take a photo on it. Not with the large crowds that push through. We didn't pay, we just kept walking, as it was late in the evening I think we got away with it, not sure about day access.

I didn't pay to walk a 10 meter bridge The history of the bridge is more interesting than the bridge itself. It costs about $5 USD for an entry ticket and there are a number of people checking tickets, though some people I spoke to said they walked it for free from the other side. Trust me its short, run down and passes over very polluted water. Your trip to Hoi An won't be less enjoyable for missing this bridge. You can stand outside the bridge and take a photo, it's almost as if you were on it.
It's worth walking over it, but there's not much info written in English about it and it's usually full of hundreds of tourists off the coaches

We looked for the bridge early morning to take pictures before the sunrise but my heart sank when I saw the bridge I have been looking for 2 days. The river surrounding the bridge was full of garbage. You will not notice it at night when you visit the place but in the morning, it was so visible. I hope the government would do something about the river surrounding ancient town and its nearby ancient structures.
Nothing much to see here, and not worth the "entrance fee" if you happen to be asked to pay for! Too overcrowded too for any decent photo if you ever want one...

Having been to this city 7 times in 5 years for a total of 26 weeks, guess you can tell we love the place. To my dismay as we tried to walk across the bridge on the way to a friends gallery we were aggressively approached by a tourist guard for our ticket. She would not let us pass. So we gave up and decided to walk around. The angry lady just radioed ahead and had two male guards approach us to pay for a ticket and would not let us pass. Meaning we could not get out. As a mid 60’s lady I could not be bothered with their behaviour and I gave up and just Paid for a ticket from yet another hostile collector. If this is the new Hoi an they will fast loose their shine and the tourist dollar. I have never been treated this poorly before. As you would imagine my tourist dollar over 26 weeks would have supported this lovely town quite well. If not for our friends would visit elsewhere.
The story about the bridge is more interesting than the bridge itself. At least thats my opinion. Also we got no ticket and could just walk over it and stuff. But didn't like it much so we proceeded.
Very busy with rubbish in the river the bridge crosses. An entry fee stopped me from crossing it as I could cross over for free 20m down from it.
The only useful thing about this bridge is that every map you get will tell you how far away you are from the Japanese bridge.
A bridge is a bridge is a bridge. And in this case nothing special. Don't look for it. You will pass it anyway when you are here.
Overrated and over dirty water. However, if one is into history, it may be worth your time. Also, much smaller than you think.

Completely didn't meet the expectations This was SUCH A DISSAPOINTMENT. After seeing a couple of pictures online we were expecting a well-maintained, beautiful and charming bridge... How surprised were we... :( First of all, it was barely possible to go through. Too many people, everyone doing in their own direction. Second of all, noone obviously really takes care of it because it is in a really poor condition. And third of all, it is just... ugly.
Waste of time, nothing to see except a rotting bridge. Had to buy tickets to cross the bridge which spans 10m
This is the most famous site in Hoi An but it is quite disappointing to be honest. The fact they make you pay to walk across the bridge (less than 5 metres long) left me feeling ripped off.
It's a bridge, it's covered it's packed with people. You pay admission for some reason. Better to take pictures of the exterior.

What's all the fuss about. Apparently you need a ticket to get in and cover the 5 meter span, all l,could from the outside was a souvenir shop. Save your money, take a few snaps from the outside and move on.

The company which run the monuments is just taking advantage of the tourists. Indeed the brigde is amazing and it's nice to go through. But what is completly forgotten is that it's normally free to go through and tourist normally should just pay to visit the small temple inside.... For the Vietnamese people the terrible people who "take care" of this place will never ask for any fees and let them go through freely while they just try to get money from any foreigners coming there!!!
It cost us $14 dollars to cross the bridge. We thought there would be some sort of attraction on or after the bridge but there was nothing. It was a waste of money.

Nice to look at but really overprotective pitt bull sytle ladies checking tickets...weirdly enough only checking entrance at one end so you can just walk in from the other side. Stupidly busy, couldn't see anything. If they are going to have a ticket system they should only let a certain amount on at once. Typical vietnamese (un)organisation!
We walked from our hotel to here only to find out that you had to pay to get in and of course we didn't take enough money with us - so my recommendation is don't forget to take money

It is an old bridge used to connect two sides of the city. You have to pay to can get inside and sometimes they ask you money just to cross it. Inside there are 5-6 photos of the bridge and the city some years ago. It is always full of tourist and sometimes it's claustrophobic be in. The stories on the bridge talk about the animals statue used like guardians and the different sides of the city, the Japanese's one, Chinese and Vietnamese.

Hoi An is a beautiful town and at night is filled with lanterns making the streets of the old town romantic to walk down. When you google about hoi an before coming you will see pictures of the famous Japanese covered bridge. The bridge is probably the most ugly thing in the old town; a tiny bridge packed with tourists that doesn't even look that nice! Stay away and explore the rest of the town!
I had to pay to go across it which is an outrage. The bridge isn't even anything special it is just the story behind it.
Most overrated thing I saw in Vietnam. Don't get upset if you don't get to see it as you will save yourself 120000 dong per person.

Having read that the average time a person spends at the bridge is 1.5 hours I was intrigued as to what could make it so interesting. Got there to just your average run of the mill bridge and have seen better bridges in Wigan near the stadium. Loved Hoi An, Hated this bridge

Don't bother with this. It will waste 30 seconds of you life that you will never get back & cost you $10 for the privilege. You wouldn't see much anyway as it is constantly swamped by Japanese paparazzi.
Could hardly get a look at it as too many Japanese Tourists taking photos. Also you have to pay to cross over the bridge. Walk 30m down the road and cross for free...

Too many people and too much harassment from vendors. I suggest you book an hotel nearby the rice fields as they have free transport to town all the time and come to visit the town early in the morning as it gets extremely crowded and the vendors do not leave you alone.
Not that exciting a bridge and they make you buy a ticket just to walk across it! Seriously! They charge you (take one of your five tickets) every time you walk across. Total rip-off.

In order to cross the Japanese bridge, you have to buy a ticket which equals around $10 AUD. a total rip off as there is constantly about 50 people on the five metre bridge. Unfortunately our hotel was on the other side of the bridge so the toll was unavoidable. If you dont have to go near it, dont.
Nothing really worth paying a ticket! I mean it's an ok bridge, nothing wowing me.. very short and crammed with tourists
Absolutely nothing to see here. It's a normal bridge in the middle of a city which is flooded by photo-hungry tourists. Skip it.
Nice to look at but the people try to charge you to walk threw, even if it's just to take pics inside the bridge
When we went to Hoi An many people told us to visit the Japanese Covered Bridge. We went to see it at night and it was nothing special.

Charge, Charge, Charge! You set foot in and you're at once addressed with "ticket?!" If you have not purchased one, you're not allowed entry to this nor any other specially designated buildings, which is fine. It just feels a bit grubby, that's all, especially when it's raining balls.

Overated. Don't need to buy ticket or go over it - it's a scam and highly unlikely it goes back into the old town. We went round the whole Thing via a bridge that was 10 meters away from this one. Only good if you specificly want to go over the actual bridge for 120VMD per person
just boring. what is the big deal. Walk over it, learn the history and move on. If you fail to see it, don't worry a minute about it. it looks just like the pics

No where any where in all the brochures does it state that you have to pay 120000 dong to get into the old town, and then you have to show your tickets on one side of the bridge only. Only random people are selected to show tickets.

The bridge was overcrowded with tourists (bus loads). The fact that you have to pay an entrance fee and then can hardly move or see anything makes no sense. The river under the bridge is extremely polluted and smells awful.
They charge money $120,000 dong to cross the tiny bridge into old town, but you can just go a block up and enter on a side street for free.

Don't waste your money Buying the multi ticket is not worth it in hoi an - you are paying to walk across this bridge! The bridge is tiny, not impressive and surrounded by rubbish!! If you really want you can see it from the bridge parallel for free anyway Hoi an is great for shopping and wandering but not for sight seeing
Need to pay to get on the bridge......full of people.......plenty of rubbish on the riverside.......terrible green light spotting on the bridge during the evening
Absolutely packed with people. Just another bridge I'd say. I thought it would be more interesting but nope, not much to see at all.

Ok. Thought there was a lot of hype for this But of waste of time as not much to see and unimpressive. Although pretty much free given you get tickets for the town and this includes the Japanese bridge.

Tiny bridge that they try and Charge you £4 to walk over See title. Enough said. Stupid 'attraction'. Ps. Lane of tacky tourist shops on other side...totally different to rest of old town vibe. Avoid like the plague.
I really wanted to see the bridge but the smell and the terrible state of the water underneath that I can only describe as being like tar was so disappointing.

Don't understand why this is so highly rated. The temple on the bridge needs a ticket to visit, and it is literally a room with a incense pedestal and some old photos. You can still see the bridge from the outside, but it looks in bad need of repair. Could be so much nicer

A bridge if ur into bridges All I can say is it's a boring bridge that's falling apart yes it is ancient but that's probably the best thing about it . Not really worth seeing a photograph speaks a thousand words and that's enough for me to see but that's just my opinion !
Not free as advertised. Best or avoid this area until after 9pm or walk around the bridge and go against the flow for free- tickets only required going one way.

I was surprised that I was on the bridge: small and unassuming. Requires a ticket to cross the bridge. The monkeys on the one end and dogs on the other hand welcomed visitors, bridging the Japanese and Chinese sides. There is a donation box inside the temple.
Not sure why this is an attraction?????? They yell about paying to cross it but not al the time. It's dirty and so is the water. Not sure what the point of it is
Its a bridge get over it (and you have to pay for that) move on Not very interesting at all. At night it lights up. But so does all of Hoi An

The bridge was nice to see but again if you looked at it for too long people would start asking for payment like everywhere in Vietnam. It also costs to walk across the bridge. If you can handle the smell there is a walking bridge down by the river that you can make it to the other part of town.

It's an old bridge. They make you buy a ticket to cross it. There's a new bridge 20 feet away that you can cross hassle free. If you're a aficionado of old bridges then I guess this would be a treat for you. If you're a normal person, this offers about 30 seconds of interesting viewing. It'll get you across the river, I suppose, so A+ for that.

Once through this bridge we lost our bearing of hoian and ended up walking far further than we intended. Once iver the bridge we found ourselves in small streets full oh market type goods - no restaurants or bars just street food.
$ have to pay to cross You Have to pay to cross the bridge Or you can walk around. It was a nice sight but not worth paying money in order to cross in my opinion.

Another daily rip off Went here and straight away had someone pounce on me asking for ticket. I thought it's a bit rude to have to pay to cross a bridge to then go into another small section of the old town only to be ripped of even more with the inflated prices of the mass produced Chinese tat. Wouldn't recommend and seemingly they only started charging people in the last year :(
Overcrowded and Expensive This bridge has at least 40 people on it at anyone time and your charged $6 dollars for the 30 seconds it takes to wander over it!
You can cross without paying, but they try to get you to pay using one of the five tickets they expect you to buy. Don't bother, just walk across.

Entrance fee for a lame bridge? Seriously? At daytime they ask for a fee to cross the bridge, which is quite ridiculous. There's not much to see. At night no one asked for a ticket. There is still not much to see then...

Cute bridge which takes about 2 minutes to view and not worth buying a ticket for. What peeved me was when an old couple wanted to cross to the other side and the attendant dragged them back this side to make them but another ticket..you have to buy a ticket to get into Hoi An anyway. Look..i am all for giving money to see any sites and hope that my money benefits these places but in Vietnam they don't mind ripping off foreigners because they think we are all rich. This behaviour is ripe in Hoi An. Prices should be the prices and not higher for white faced people.
Stood and watched the lady in uniform only asking Caucasian people to pay/produce tickets. no big deal except it's the main access through the area. Not well thought out.
The bridge is nice to see, but it's possible to enjoy it from the outside. Don't use one of your 'old town tickets' to see the inside, because it isn't worth the while.

Not really worth paying for. I don't know, but for me it's just a worn bridge with a ton of selfie-tourists jammed in this 10-meter bridge! (Wear eyeprotection for the selfiesticks waving around! ;) )
I do not understand why I need to pay to cross a bridge of 8m! You can walk around it and its nothing special
It is a bridge, neither more nor less. They charge and can not see anything special. Do not waste time there
We didn't find it very impressive to see. I guess the story behind it is good to know. Nothing exciting to see though. I gess if you're in the area then you should stop by.
They want to charge you 120 kdongs as it this one of few attractions over there just to walk on few squiky wooden planks? Theres plenty of bridges like this in Japan for free.
Visited the bridge and was the main effort was to sell me local produced table cloths . I came to see the bridge
A let down, the bridge has been poorly maintained and is over a river that is being used as a sewer line
Takes one minute to see the bridge. Don't waste a ticket on the attached pagoda. One of the most overrated things I've seen

Nothing special!!! Just nice! The bridge is very short and completely to restore. there is a temple on the bridge that is visitabile only if you by another ticket, different from the one you need to enter old Town

Walking over bridge only is supposed to be free. Even after insisting, the employee demanded and even shove me. I didn't pay and just left. It's not even a great bridge compared to some more ancient and beautiful I've seen in China and Japan.

Not worth time nor money It's just a bridge. If you pay for someone to explain the very few artifacts there for you it might be worth a quick visit I guess but... other than that, I think the houses are more worth it.
This is not at all what i expected, there is pretty much nothing to see and they charge you money even just for crossing on your way to the restaurant.
Getting only 5 tickets for the different booths in Hoi An ancient city - this bridge felt like a waste of a ticket.

You pay money to walk 10m in darkened wooden space? Sketch of likely conversation between Seabon and Vietnamese man collecting tickets at bridge: Bridge man: You pay money to walk 10m in darkened wooden space? Good views of scummy water and other undecorated wooden footbridge just 20m away! Bridgebon: no, you're dumb! There's a perfectly good bridge you can cross for free 20m away, from which you can see the picturesque Japanese bridge.

You do not need a ticket! Ignore the gentleman on the bridge telling you need a ticket you only need a ticket to visit the under whemling temple on the bridge. There are far better things to spend your time doing in Hoi An

polluted canal around the bridge Such a beautiful bridge with smelly surroundings. The canal is polluted with mud, it smells bad. It seems that the local people committee does not care about environnement concern. Thoudands of tourists pour into the old quater, it would be normal to have a clean canal and river.
Several different locals stand out front and attempt to collect money to pass through Covered Bridge. Nothing much to see but more venders.

To even list the Japanese bridge as a sight is really funny if it wasn't so sad. If this bridge is a sight, EVERY bridge, building, etc in Europe that is over 100 years old would have to be mentioned in here as a sight. And in contrast to the Japanese Bridge mentioned monuments in Europa are well restored and kept in shape.
unforgetable experience, old town beautiful!! It was unique walking around the township. Sure, a lot of things are repedative but you will stumble on many, many other things.

Didn't look as if anyone had done anything with this in years, pictures of the bridge looked nice but it hasn't been painted or cleaned in years. The house with it consisted of 2 small rooms with virtually nothing in it, took us less than 2 minutes to look round
This is so over hyped and nothing special. We walk straight by it the first time without seeing it..
Went to see Japanese bridge, I wonder what all the fuss is about. Go a see it but, you have not missed a lot if you do not, and do not pay to walk across it not worth it.

Be careful of the miss understood If someone ask you the ticket at the entrance of the bridge. Just tell theme ( no I just cross the bridge ). They will let you go. But if you want to visit the little (room) in the bridge then you have to give the ticket.

Thanks God they don't have Golden Gate Bridge!!! Ok, it's nice old bridge in downtown....but??? To cross the bridge you have to pay aprox. 2$ fee...and the bridge is 10m long. So please, how much they'll charge for crossing Golden Gate if this bridge is in Hoi An???..500$!!! Ok, GGB is't old as this bridge...but bridges in Prague ,Venice, Paris?!?!......older, more beautiful, and for free!!!...so please don't rob people, that's not fair...all of us working hard for money in life.
Not much to see. Might be more interesting if I knew the story behind it Ok if you are passing it anyway but I wouldn't go and look for it

Hoi An really depends on the tourist industry, yet has failed to restore or keep this bridge in good repair. It's right in the middle of town and can only be used on foot. It could look wonderful with just some paint. I was very disappointed when I came across it.

Nice bridge, nothing special though, we were to we had to pay to cross the bridge (like some trolls out of a fairytale), when we said no they stood in front of us and blocked our path. It turns out the charge is for a gallery / museum leading off the centre of the bridge, not worth the money, better off following the path down stream and you get better views and better to photograph.

Not something really special. You're ticket includes 5 places to see in the old city of Hoi an, so don't waste one of the tickets on this. There is only a little room with no interesting explanations, you can take pictures from the outside and it's more than enough.
Like most of the exhibits in old town Hoi An, this one is overpriced and provides nothing. Stand to the edge of the bridge and look at it for free

Rip off crossing bridges I enjoyed the sights of hoi an and the traders had their charm despite being a bit over friendly and slightly pushy at times. However crossing bridges is a scam. Myself and my girlfriend crossed the bridge to get a drink and there were no signs or people telling us about having to pay to return. Upon coming back to the bridge there were three people waiting demanding 120,000 dong from both myself and my girlfriend. They were aggressive, rude and laughed when we asked for an explanation as to why when we crossed the bridge originally nobody told us about the charge. You can walk further up the river to another bridge to avoid these people and return to the town for free. It was a disappointing aspect to an otherwise enjoyable day in hoi an.

Yes, you do have to pay for crossing the bridge! No, you don't need to cross it, nothing of importance to be seen, just cross beside, but take care! locals try to push you in the muddy water (extremly funny!!!--- for the locals!) , better take another bridge by walking a bit more and not beside the canal. Nice view from outside, kitsch at night. The actual value you get for the "ticket" (many fake tickets! Don't buy from people walking around!) is about 20,000Dong, so you might forget it and spend an afternoon in Hoi An only. Last public bus to Danang is 530pm for 18,000Dong, dont pay more!!! Get off 100 meters from train station, goes every 30 Minutes at least!

You can only cross the bridge if you buy a ticket at a ticket store nearby. Those tickets admit you to 5 "attractions", one of which is the bridge. This ticket is quite expensive (120k dong) and if you dont want to go anywhere else, that is a lot just to cross the bridge (no alternative tickets). The bridge itself is small and I can't see why others rate this with 5 stars.

I've seen plenty of bridges in my time, and this Japanese Covered Bridge rates among the worst. We had walked over the bridge the previous night and thought the decorated exterior was as pretty for what it was. The next day after purchasing our 5 tickets to sights in Hoi An we were knocked back from other "attractions" as they cost extra and wouldn't take our tickets even though it was stated in the pamphlet we were entitled to entry. We then tried our luck in the area of the bridge that we walked over the previous night. Dodging a large tour group we had to give up one of our tickets to cross the bridge. Even though we got to see the room on the side, it lacked interest and was not worthy of being a ticketed attraction. To rub it in almost every second person behind us was not stopped by the ticket official. This bridge is just one of 21 sites that are given the title of a "cultural attractions", but we found most of the other sites do not convey much real significance, but are rather chosen just to be cash cows for tourists.

Crossed this bridge fine previously but then met aggressive local women in official looking uniform demanding money when we tried to cross again. She chased us shouting and physically stopped us from crossing. As far as i was concerned you needed a ticket to view the temple but not to cross the bridge. Like a few things in hoi an and vietnam a complete scam and an aggressive one at that. Really poor.

Scam artists say you need their tickets just to walk near the bridge. Beware, scam artists don't just shout at you to buy a ticket for being on the bridge, but to walk near it, take photos of it and even have the audacity to say they have the "authority" to charge for walking in the surrounding streets. They dress in blazers and have lanyards to look official.There are no signs saying anything of the kind, if there were we would have paid to enter that area, but they actually physically blocked us from LEAVING once we realised they wanted us to pay. It was terrifying and the moment someone threatened to call the police they slunk away. Left us shaking. They refused to tell us what the tickets were for, even though we kept saying we wanted to leave and not walk over the bridge. They are very persistent but you have to continue to resist. Beware all travellers.

The bridge itself is sort of "cute" if you will, but the scam artists that attempt to make you buy a "ticket" for a free bridge are giving Hoi An a bad name, which it clearly does not deserve. The merchants on BOTH sides of this bridge should be beside themselves and angry as they are losing business as we watched people stop and get turned around because they were not aware of the fact that crossing this bridge is FREE -- and rightly so, were not going to buy a "ticket" that is a scam. When will the merchants on both sides of this bridge wake up and realize that they are losing customer? And, when will the Hoi An tourism authorities wake up to the fact that the scam artists on the bridge are putting a black mark on a beautiful town and a wonderful holiday experience. Wake up merchants and tourism authorities and act before it is too late! Do the right thing and fix the problem before the reputation of Hoi An takes a hit for no good reason.
Please just walk over and do NOT use your ticket on here (waste!) The road after the bridge (away from the river) is quaint though and is reflective of the Hoi An culture.

Got abused and assaulted Tonight I had a terrible experience while trying cross the bridge. My partner and I were stopped from crossing the bridge by two aggressive women demanding that we purchase a ticket. It sounded ridiculous so I asked some western tourists who were also crossing the small bridge from the other-side. They advised that they had crossed the bridge at least 4 times for free and had no issue with payment. I then turned to the two women and told them why we were being charge to cross the bridge. The fee was applicable to the entrance of the temple/museum, and while we tried to explain that we only wanted to cross the small bridge, they still demanded a fee. We told them it was a scam and then proceeded to cross. At that point the women grabbed me and tried to pull me back. She was screaming and shouting that I was a stealing. I tell you what, this has completely ruined my stay here in Hoi An. FYI I am a Vietnamese born Australian who can speak fluent Vietnamese. After a 5mins screaming match with the lady who demanded money from unsuspecting tourists I just kept walking. Not only was I physically abused I was verbally abused, where I was accused of not being Vietnamese and I should know better (?????) better that I should know the locals try and scam innocent tourists???

It's a bridge. A small bridge. It'll take you 30 seconds to cross it - double that if you take a photo. Not worth the trip to Hoi An, certainly not worth buying a ticket for. Go on it at night after the ticket collector has gone to bed.

I saw ticket conductor (skinny female in Blue uniform) was shouting at few Asian tourists at the site and it really made me feel about what people often say about Hoian (Friendly town, people). Really wanted to take photo of that woman but worrying that she would give me some trouble. Pls train staff to smile - not with grumpy face. Perhaps those staff think that they have power thus they could do anything they want. Hoian is very nice town thus don't let those people spoil its charms
OK so it's an old bridge, but honestly it's actually quite ugly so it's not even a photo oppurtunity... Definitely not worth going out of your way to look at.
I don't really get why this is an attraction in Hoi An... Go at night, walk across it in about 30 seconds and you're done.

We couldn't wait to leave hoi an. It's just a town built for tourists to shop...... how many tailors does one small town need. Everything is overpriced- even the street food is double the normal price. Charging people to walk across a bridge to another part of town is robbery! The bridge is ok for a photo but the town is one to skip unless you want some new clothes.
Still under construction and apparently, the work has even been stopped following a disagreement between the different restoration players over the exact work to be undertaken.

Demolition and new construction is underway! - finished in 2 years, like in 1594? :) Nothing to see, waste of time - and money!? - it is claimed that tickets are still being sold - don't be fooled. We have visited this nearly 450-year-old bridge several times, as early as 2007. The new one will be a magnificent building! - this alone will be a good reason to come again to this great city and this absolutely fantastic old town.
We are really disappointed. This bridge is being repaired, losing its value. You also have to pay to see it. What a waste of our time.
April and still under construction. I was pretty gutted but just bad timing I guess. Not much anyone can do. Plenty more to do
The bridge was under renovation when we visitied in mid March. What a bad timing. Better if there were pictures about its stories & explaination about its history.
Bridge hidden by scaffolding Nothing to see or very little (see the photo taken on February 17, 2024) because the symbol of the city of Hoi An is being restored
This ancient bridge is no longer accessible to guests except for viewing what remains of it - it is undergoing substantial reconstruction
Under restoration January 2024 We had only one day in Hoi An and were hoping to visit tge Bridge unfortunately it is under restoration and covered.

Bridge is under renovation Japanese Bridge is under renovation and not much to see at the moment. There are informational banners, also you can through walls the renovation process. As its in the middle of the old town you can tale a look anyway on the way to eat for example:)

Bridge under restoration Under restoration. Not much to see. First time visit at night time, totally missed it, thinking we had to walk through construction site, but in the day time realised it was the Japanese bridge but under restoration.

There isn't any "Japanese Covered Bridge" there is only a place where it used to be. There is a metal shed built there and it appears to me that it is so rotten that 95% of it will need to be replaced.
Bridge is currently under repairs - covered in scaffold Don't expect works to be completed till 2024 !
Dismantled and nothing to see except piles of building materials. Unable to get a sense of how it will look when restored.
Disappointed that the bridge is now encased in a structure that prevents you from actually seeing it. Hopefully it’s only temporary.
The history around this bridge is great, however it’s extremely over crowded. There’s even a ticket stall on the bridge itself which just takes away any sentimental value. Very overrated

Tiny, crowded and uninteresting Little wooden bridge!! Lots of people and nothing really of any note here. Maybe I am missing something or really need to be into Japanese/Vietnamese history to understand but nothing here to interest me.
Only viewed from outside Decided to go at night in the hope that it would be all lit up. But it wasnt. No information on the opposite bridge so it was just another dull bridge.

Throngs of tourists slowly taking their carefully posed Insta worthy photos and you have to pay! Nice to look at and read about, but when you get there you have to pay to walk on it, and compete with bus loads of package tour tourists for one second of photo worthy time. No atmosphere for us independent and non blogging and Insta posing tourists. We opted to enjoy the views of it instead.

There's practically nothing much to see. Just listening to the history of the place & taking photos, our group didn't enjoy this part of town. After crossing the bridge, the shops over the other side were much more quiet too

I honestly walked past about ten times before I realised this was the Japanese covered bridge that all the travel guides went on about. I am no architect but this bridge really is nothing special. Worth looking at if you are in the old town but not worth a trip out for. I only noticed it was this bridge from the hordes of tourists surrounding it.

Even being in low season during the night was soooo crowded... And there were someone asking for tickets... But lots of ppl was getting in without ticket... And at the is technically just another street...so I don't fully understand how this works... So we just walked away, took a picture from outside and... Check.
I don't understand the big deal. It's a timber bridge. Sure it has a little history but definitely not worth making a special effort to visit. Luckily we stumbled across it by accident.

Not really anything to see. Look at the above picture. You have now seen it. It is nothing any more special up close. It is incredibly crowded and sometimes (depends on the day), you get charged just to walk across it. Plus, it is getting old and has not been well maintained. Nothing to be impressed about.

Don’t understand what’s the hype about this place This is a historical bridge built around the 17th century in order to connect the 2 parts of the city to enable commerce. The bridge is wooden and contains a small museum. It crosses over a small stream that flows into the main river. I didn’t find it interesting.

We first visited Hoi An in 2005 and had very fond memories of a beautiful town. We were excited to come back here with our kids but the whole experience was a massive let down. The bridge is still a beautiful landmark but when we visited it was inundated with tourists dressed in costumes all clambering over the bridge to try and get the best selfie. We could not even get close. The bridge has survived a long time but unless tourist numbers are limited I doubt it will last...

It was nice to look at for like 2 seconds get a quick picture from the outside and thats it. Can't really see the sunset from this bridge, you'd have to go to the main bridge which is almost impossible to cross as there are hordes of tourists trying to get their selfies as bicycles and motorbikes try to get by.

Not as good as it used to be.. Having seen the Japanese bring 10 yrs earlier I was excited to take my husband to see it. It let us both down as it was commercialised and the river before it was polluted. We are glad there was more to Hoi An than just the bridge..

We should this be something special? We have tried to figure out we this should be so special, but has not been able at all. No special view. Nothing that is not to be seen elsewhere. Just s totally overcrowded bridge where people try to steal your money!!!

Nice looking bridge, but just as nice to look at it from a distance. The bridge itself is not that special to make it the number one attraction in the city. Go and have a look if you’re strolling in the area, but don’t go out of your way to reach it.
We felt like we needed to see this bridge because of the reviews and hype about it. We were disappointed. The main bridge crossing the river is nicer than this bridge.

Do not understand the hype. We did use this bridge a couple of times but simply because it was the only way to get where we were going. Maybe I am missing something but I just do not understand why the bridge is so popular

It’s an old bridge. The temple inside the bridge is of some significance. Other than that. Meh. I thought it was sort of a “must see”. If I didn’t know what it looked like beforehand I would have walked right by it.

Read much about the old town but it was so crowded we quickly decided to take a pass and returned to our hotel just after crossing this bridge. Very much like Disneyworld without the rides! Too many people!
Interesting but over hyped - small , very busy and nothing of great interest. The town is far more atmospheric, especially the moped free zone.
Wouldn’t pay for thid Go and look at it for free from the outside. Wouldn’t pay to go sit inside and battle with the throngs of other tourists.

Gotta pay to pass a 25 meters long bridge completely stuffed with chinese tourists? No thanks. Not aware of the history of the bridge but if local authorities continue to exploit it like this there wont be one to be told for long.

It's a bridge, not worthy of such high reviews - swarming with tourists and not that photogenic This one of the most famous tourist attractions in Ancient Town and can be walked across without showing a ticket, although the first time we visited during the day we didn't even attempt to fight the crowds to cross the bridge, just wasn't worth it. We visited in day time and also at night to take photos. We just weren't very impressed however, even without all the crazy crowds, in my opinion this is just not that great looking a bridge.
Bit of a fizzer. Can’t believe you have to pay money to stand on this bridge! Always crowded with tourists

Tourists were required to pay for walking through the bridge. I was not quite interested in the history and this little bridge and it was not attractive to me. So I decided not to pay and took another path to go to the other side of the bridge.
Nothing too exciting... unable to go on it unless you buy a ticket to go through temples. I wasn’t that interested to pay to go. Able to get photos from the street.

It’s a bridge... and it’s stuffed with tourists It might be an important historical landmark, but you’d never be able to tell with the number of tourists gurning and posing in front of it. Try to admire the architecture if you can blank out the point and shoot posers.
It’s old and therefore historically interesting but be sure to go early in the day before the crowds jam their way in.
We were disapointed- too crowdy place. Local people tryied to cheat us to buy entry ticket. Stallholders in neighborhoods get unpleasant if you buy nothing from them.

The Bridge is too crowded and polluted My group came here and not enjoyed at all . Why? 1/ The water enviroment is polluted and smelly. 2/ Everybody is here and pay no respect to the temple God. 3/ street vendors were selling cards, yelling, ... like a mess. Hope it will be organized better.

For a 200 year old Bridge it is not revered as much as it should. It is the access to a puppet show and you have to have tickets to go in. It is also crowded with Chinese tourists. About 100 were on it when we went past, and I think one day it may collapse. It is very hard to photograph with so many people around. Maybe go very early in the morning.
Not worthing the ticket Very Nice bridge from the outside, it definitly doesn't worth à ticket to cross or visit the inside. If you're in the area you'd better visit thanh toan covered bridge!
Think it's a bit over rated. Something to see.. But really... It's just a bridge that adds some character to the old town... That's about it
Nice example of Japanese Architecture of the era. Filled with tour groups. Skip the admittance and enjoy the view from outside.
You have to go through it to get to much of Hoi An's other attractions and it will cost you one of your five entrance tabs on your Hoi An ticket, but it's not much. Spend 1-2 minutes and move on...
Not really sure what all the fuss is about! Not particularly awe-inspiring during the day or after dark, and during the day they want to use one of your 5 tickets just to cross!

Inconsistent Old Town Tickets I stayed in Hoi An for 5 days and for the first three days, not a single person asked me for a ticket and I crossed over the Japanese Covered bridge multiple times, day and night. Then, suddenly, I was not allowed to pass unless I had a ticket. There was a time where literally hundreds of people were coming and going out of the bridge and they yelled and stopped me for a ticket. Really?!? You’re going to cut my ticket when you can barely move because of the tourist traffic?!? This happened more than once. I was over it and just frustrated at the total inconsistency of the old town ticket system. I’d be all for it if you actually stuck by your system, but they definitely do not.

So many other people are trying to get a photo at the same time. You also have to pay if you wish to go onto the bridge, I’m unsure the cost. Unless you are really keen I wouldn’t go out of my way to see it.
Not a whole lot to see, worth walking past but not paying to cross it. Absolutely packed with tourists, felt like a sardine!

I got out of bed for this? Granted. It’s something else to see a bridge like this if you live in the West. But we were severly underwhelmed. Is this really the main attraction here? I would say ‘not as advertised’. During the day you would walk right past it if it weren’t for the bus loads of Chinese tourists taking selfies, and during the night it is one of the only buildings in Hoi An that ISN’T illuminated. Inside the bridge is a little tempel, but you will need to buy a ticket. Furthermore, the water this bridge crosses is a rat infested open sewer. Sorry if you did like it. I have seen better.
Read that you could cross without purchasing a ticket but when my wife and I tried, the woman on the bridge stopped us and preceded to order us to buy a ticket.
This is a very very very crowned bridge, full of people. Personally I don’t think I will go there twice.

The stench of burnt rubber and decaying organic/plastic matter hits you in the face, like you owe it money, as soon as you get within view of this place. It's obvious why such a smell should assault you when you see that the water beneath it is completely black. It's where monsters are born and die in. The bridge itself requires a fee to enter at certain times, for certain people, on certain sides of the bridge. I would hope they use ticket sales to clean the area around it a bit? I did end up buying a ticket and the bridge is exactly what you would expect from the name and the fact that it only spans a few meters across.
And not that impressive - but you can simply walk through into the city - if you can get through the hordes!

Not important ... you can see the picture enough Small & not interesting ... People are Great + Comfortable + Large .... Market is expesive or v. you must find & negotiate starting from -50%! (also, Da Nang) or don't buy.

Its a beautiful old bridge, but you have to show your entrance ticket which is actually ridiculous because it connects too streets. Your not allowed to pass the bridge without a ticket. Its really the only stupid thing in HoiAn.

I’ve visited other covered bridges in Vietnam and would recommend giving this one a miss. Like much of Hoi An it’s very crowded and choked with tourists. Hard to see anything and I didn’t learn anything special about the destination. If you have a 5 ticket attractions pass (about $6 USD) there are other more serene and more beautiful places to visit in town. Note that I have no pictures from inside the bridge because it was so busy I would have just been photographing people’s backs. This was early in the day, too. Have a look at it from the outside and boom, you’re good.

When you enter the east side of this ancient bridge you will be blocked by official looking people requesting payment to step onto the bridge. Many were turned away. Don't be foolish. Don't pay. If you follow the main river's path for a block and make a U-turn, you may enter the bridge from the west side, for free. The bridge was built by the Japanese in the 1590's and became an icon of Hoi An. It was a link between the Japanese community and the Chinese quarters.
Been through this many times just to get to the other side and find it underwhelming - small covered bridge - there are better things to see in Hoi an than this bridge.

Toll Bridge in middle of town The Japanese Bridge or Monkey Bridge is in the start of Old Town. As we approached, 2 guards were stopping people to sell a 5 attraction ticket to go thru the small, very short covered bridge . Some paid and others turned around. If you walk a short distance there is no cost. Not much in the town.. the 5 attractions on the ticket : bridge , old style house, Chinese Temple, and a couple other things. This town lives only for tourists and the bridge is not worth any price to pay.

Most je hezký jen z jedné strany, z druhé není o co stát. Navíc se za průchod platí což nechápu, evidentně se snaží vydělat úplně na všem. The bridge is nice only from one side, the other is not what it is worth. Moreover, the passage is what I do not understand, obviously trying to make money on everything.
Well it was okay. But they charge to go across. Wasn't very awe-inspiring so if you're there go see otherwise don't bother making a special trip.

You should go see it & check it out. I walked over it without realizing what it was. I've also found it almost unavoiable to have gone to due to its location in town. Take a few pictures at it, by it, inside it. It won't be one of the more memorable experiences of my stay in Hoi An, but it is a unique cultural site that adds to the beauty of the town.

At the entrance we were asked to show purchased ticket. Due to communication problem the lady ticket collect was real rude and shouting for the tickets to be shown and marked. Her mannerism spoilt our day, she must have assumed we did not buy the ticket. We did not even get the apology from her after we shown the tickets purchased earlier. The male assistant has to apologise on her behalf. She should not be there, she has given a negative image of Hoi An !!!!
That is it. Entry to it you'll need to buy. Only the front is beautiful the back is not so nice. But it looks good.

Not what I would call a feature attraction. It costs you one ticket to travel over it one way and zero the other way. However visitor numbers are over whelming so ticket collection is haphazard. The bridge itself is very short with nothing of great interest.
Overcrowded, bit like a touristtrap! But still worth a glance from outside. But not worth a ticket. The surroundings are great.
It is very fine but crowded with tourists. Try to see it on not busy occasions. For shopping the area is good

You don’t need to give over your ticket for this. You can cross the other bridge 10m south for free and get a picture of the bridge. If you do want to cross it, do so at night and sneak into a group of people. They will not check your ticket. There are better bridges around the world and the dragon bridge in Da Nang is cooler. Venice has nicer bridges and Japan has more interesting and Longer bridges in this style.
It was quite pretty but really? You had to pay for walking over it?? So dissappointed on all that money sucking

While the bridge has marginal historical significance, visitors to Hoi An who don't give a hoot about said history, will find an old structure -- a very short covered bridge. I found the bridge to be neither aesthetic nor particularly functional. If you happen to be passing, take a look. Don't make an effort to visit.
It’s just a bridge. It is an old bridge made by Japanese people but it costs money to get into so I would not recommend it.
ordinary bridge can be found in many other palces in VN . It is again a tourist trap very much like the Mona Lisa paining in the Louvre Museum . If you don't have time do not bother

The bridge is nice to take photos of. However for the price to pay to enter the bridge to cross over it wasn't really worth it. We didn't find anything different on the other side of the bridge that we didn't see at the night market.
People stand at the start and end of the bridge and expect you to pay to walk over 10 metres of concreate. Just bypass the bridge and walk around it.

Dull and small, a momentary visit The bridge is featured as one of the main attractions of Hoi An, and as a major item of history, it is a valuable treasure, so you mshould see it, but it's not worth more than a few minutes attention as a simple tourist. You have to buy a ticket to visit inside.
Nothing great here. I’d skip it. It’s a quick walk through with a temple. Unless you are a devout Buddhist who knows the history of this it’s not that great.
Nothing to write home about, but it's nice to see it when in Hoi An I guess. Herds of tourists everywhere you look taking selfies. Good for people watching but nothing special.

The bridge is good - the little Annex less so So the covered bridge has a pair of monkey gods guarding one end a pair of dog gods the other. It's also guarded by ticket collectors. You don't have to give up one of your precious 5 to cross the bridge but that isn't made very plain. You give up the ticket to go into the Annex - well that was a waste of a ticket. Not much info in there and very little of interest. So by all means cross the bridge or better still take a photo of it from the little wooden bridge .... Just don't give up a ticket for it and skip the Annex.

More useful as a thoroughfare than an exciting attraction. Looks nice but really is not exciting. However if you enjoy people standing in your way whilst they take countless selfies then you'll love it.

It's great to see however I certainly wouldn't come to this city just for the bridge. You have to buy a ticket if you want to cross it (have a look one end and the walk around and look from the other, you won't miss anything). The ancient town of Hoi An however is worth flying around the world twice for
Didnt really like the bridge. Maybe because before we arrived here we also went to Japan and saw way better Japanse architecture. Very crowded place.

They charge you to cross this little bridge. The bridge itself looks nice locsted in the ebautiful city centre. However, it is not worth it to actually buy a ticket to cross it. I would advise just walking past while you're walking through the city.
Nothing much to write home about Went to the bridge. It is quaint - I could not find anything remarkable really. Miss it unless you have plenty of time.
Cute bridge, but nothing really special. Although I don't know the history behind the bridge, it feels like a themapark..
It's a bridge with a roof, nothing more. Having said that you can get a lovely view of the rubbish in the river.
Just don't get what the big deal is. Its a 15m covered crossing over a creek. If you can get past all the Japanese taking photos have a look but its nothing special.

Lots of better places in Hoi An than this Its a complete joke this place has 4 stars. It's so small that you can make literally no more than 6 steps, and if you can, because there's so much people here in August, that it's impossible to walk. One tip: Don't come to Hoi An just to see this bridge, it's a normal wooden bridge. Better spent time chilling by the river and walking through it's colourfull and charming streets.

Good for a photo & that's about it , due to the unbelievable pollution in the water, don't worry if you miss this attraction in hoi an , worth a quick photo but that's it 😐 many other things & picturesque places to go to

The architecture is impressive, but the bridge is honestly boring. It's really small bridge and if you want to walk on it, they'll try to charge you. But if you walk from the less touristy side, they just let you walk in on the bridge. Totally a rip off
Whilst I appreciate the bridge is very old this one isn't particularly beautiful or well preserved, best viewed at a distance the other side of the river
We did not pay to cross the small bridge. It looked old and unimpressive from the outside at night (lit by green lights).

You have to be joking This bridge is a ramshackle structure that should be replaced or closed. It may be historically relevant, but is unsightly and unsafe. It has not been maintained and to charge a fee to cross it is an insult.
What's the fuss about? Rammed with buffoons carrying selfie sticks who left their self-awareness at home. You can't miss it on the way into the old town, but keep walking.
Nice looking bridge but crowded and underwhelming. It takes two minutes and is nice to see, but not life changing at all. It is part of the old town ticket, so might as well.

Picturesque bridge, ruined by the dirty river filled with trash flowing underneath it. Bridge was too packed to actually walk on the day I went so timing is everything it seems if you want to get a picture without 100 other tourists 🙂 Clean up your act Hoi An!

Just a covered wooden bridge crossing the river and very small--not even that scenic. Most convenient way of crossing into old town and crammed with tourists trying to get pictures of themselves. Don't pay to cross--just walk!

Nothing much and a let down when you have to pay Appears a highly recommended place to go. But when we reached there, was not as big as we have expected. A wooden structure over a masonry bridge structure. Nothing much to see, and was a let down when you have to pay for crossing over.

I will not paid to cross a bride I didn't pay to cross this bridge, I think doesn't worth it! It was crowded and the surroundings full of trash. I don't mind to pay a fee if at least the do a good manteance of the place

Prior to the trip, I read about this bridge quite a bit everywhere but when I finally saw it, it was literally just a bridge. I saw it both in the day and in the night; Still, it didn't really hype me up at all. But having said that, you don't really have to go out of your way to see this attraction. As long as you are visiting the Ancient Town, you will see it.
We had quite a buildup prior to looking at this bridge but it is certainly nothing special. Very short bridge and there is a fee to walk across it.
Nothing special except for it's historical and iconic status. Visitors to Hoi An ancient town cannot miss this attraction and visit it cos it's there....

This is one of the sights covered on a ticket purchased to visit several historic sights. It is an ancient small wooden bridge. If you are expecting anything, you will be disappointed. Just because they charge to cross this bridge or look around, don’t think it is worth anything. It is a congested area for tourists. I’m not sure why anyone needs to spend more than 5 minutes there. Take your selfie and move on.

Nothing really to get excited about... there is also a rip-off, as a girl is collecting an "entrance fee" for a site that really is nothing really special. The trick, walk around along the river, take the first narrow street to the right,... An you get to the bridge from the other side (and pay nothing. 😝the real sites though are in the streets of the city, with all the lanterns at night. You can forget the bridge...
It's just a bridge...not even that pretty really! Could easily be skipped and not missed. Has some information on the inside but not very informative.

Tickets to cross ... really!? We crossed this bridge 3 times free of charge BUT lots of tourists were not so lucky it seemed to depend on which way you were going and if there was a large tour group crossing ( just go with the glow) it's inconsistent and totally unnecessary to pay to cross such a tiny bridge. Best photos are taken from opposite the bridge anyway
You can not appreciate it because is crowded as hell!!! Actually we walked through and we had to do it very fast because of all the people pushing... couldn't even take a pic :(

People will try to scam you into paying to cross, ignore them. Funnily coming from the other side is fine. Not even worth crossing though, it's quite smelly inside and the view of garbage in the canal wasn't so stunning. Maybe don't even bother looking at it for longer than a few seconds and only if you happen to pass by it, it's just a bridge. There is a really good ice cream place nearby however - head straight towards it on the path that crosses it, then make a right before the entrance and walk for less than a minute.

It is a pretty boring bridge... Not quite sure why this is such a huge attraction in Hoi An but gets a lot of attention from tourists with selfie sticks. Don't bother buying a ticket to go across, not worth it.

The bridge connects to the Japanese section of the area located on top of a river that used to be functional. It was really crowded and the incence in the small room on the bridge were quite overwhelming that we almost missed the animal sculptures of either sides of the bridge, indicating the years (year of the animal), it took to build the bridge from what we were told. Tickets were snipped off at the entrance of the bridge.
You need tickets to cross - and the best restaurant is on the other side! The detour round is long, and the bridge is not that impressive.
Ancient town which is accessed via the bridge offers nothing old town has already. Disappointed to see the same type of tat sold by some vendors. Really could be a wonderful place.

The bridge looks nice especially in the evening but it isn't worth to go there just for the bridge. If u are in the old town and have already bought a ticket u can go there and visit it. Otherwise I wouldn't recommend it.
Tourist trap with whole streets tolled off because they access the bridge. Don't waste the money and go on a boat ride instead for 200K Dong.

The bridge is nice but it is unbearably busy. In fact so busy that I couldn't be bothered to take a photo. Hoi an is amazing and if you get away from the main attractions you will enjoy your trip far better.
all in all just a short covered bridge with decorations. Too much is made of it. also must have a ticket that they check at the bridge

O don't see the point of paying to cross this bridge. It's not that special and if you want photos is better outside the bridge where you can actually take pictures of the bridge itself. In whole Vietnam people are not trained to deal with tourists. The people who ask for tickets or fees everywhere are rude and they yell at you. So unpleasant and unnecessary. They really make you don't want get in in some places. Their loss.
Just a bridge used to turn a buck Interesting due to its age but just a bridge really. Not worth paying the fee to cross.....just go around it.
Its a bridge with a bit of decor on it. No big deal. See it if you are there but not worth a special effort.
Not a lot to,say about this place. We were in and out but bus loads of Japanese tourists with guide may find it of greater significance.
Nothing too impressive. Only a few minutes required to walk across it and admire it. Apart from that, nothing great.

Bridge looks nice, looks better at dark during the night market. Really crowded so don't expect to get a picture on it alone. Definitely not worth paying for so don't buy tickets in advance cause you will be let down that you did. Just make sure to walk past it at nighttime if you want to see it. You can easily check this off your list while enjoying the rest of the night market.
The bridge while lit at night is colorful in the day appears small, crowded and unattractive. Perhaps it is popular for many tourists but not high on the list for me.

A bridge is a bridge...... I must have missed the point but I don't get the hype with this bridge. Extremely popular photo opportunity it seems so go there, grab a photo and leave unless the historical significance of the bridge means more to you than the rest of the photo hungry folks who visit this attraction.
Although very old, it is not the best thing to see in Hoi Han. You have to pay to go through.. Don't bother. You will have a better panoram from the outside.
Photo opportunity, I suppose. It is included on your compulsory book of tickets so worth a visit. Beautiful buildings on both side's so this is a bridge you have to cross!

Not quit sure, why this bridge is on the 20.000 VDN , its a bridge. So.. yeah. Just a old bridge. Maybe the history makes sense why5 its on the 20.000 VDN but yeah. A bridge. But the streetd before and after the japanese bridge are amazing!

Don't know what the fuss is over this bridge, only that the bridge is ancient and holds some history. Avoid going into the temple as there is nothing much to see. Since your ticket only allows you to enter 5 attractions avoid the temple. There are better attractions available. You can cross the bridge for free to another part of old town.

Not sure the poor structure could take the weight of all the tourists scrambling to stand on it an pout and throw up the ritual (v) for victory. The water underneath was stagnant and stunk. You have to pay to cross the bridge I love my culture and history, but this bridge did nothing for me, best advice, go see it, then you will know
This is a bridge that crosses the river. It works well to walk on. It appears to be structurally sound. End of story.
I was not impressed with this attraction as it was often choked and full of tourists. It was often hard to even pass through because of large groups standing in the way.

If there's no one who will tell this is the famous bridge, I will just pass by. This is just an old preserved bridge which you can see all around Asian old cities. I've seen many old, famous, and good architecture bridges and this one is no big deal for me. But if you are someone who interested in history, you may like it.

The bridge is a historic site for Hoi An but to pay to go in is too much. It's only a very short bridge. We went in through the next lane via the small road by accident. Nothing special inside just stores selling souvenirs and clothings. A bit disappointed. Try to go at night it looks better with lights on. Last 2 nights the bridge was very crowded with light show on the bridge.
It really is beautiful and cute in some way but not a worth place to take your time. 시간 내서 갈 만한 곳은 아닌 것 같구 그냥 지나다가 보이면 사진 하나 찍는 정도입니다. 지나가는데 티켓을 사야한다는 말을 듣고 웃겨서 웃었습니다.

Like the rest of the old quarter, there's nothing much to see here. Tons of tourists crowding around the bridge, but it is pretty at night/ evenings when people place candles into the water making wishes.

Rubbish during day. Looks nice I suppose but nothing special. When went whole area was flooded and so bridge was rammed which made it sucks more. At night very though when lit up from other side of river. Don't waste too much time here. Eat!
The view from outside is free! To cross the bridge you will need to purchase a ticket. Not really worth it! Walk along the pathway on the river front instead 😊
Not sure what all the fuss is about this. Not much too it apart from it being out of place amongst the Vietnamese architecture. Then again, what can you expect when it is just a bridge after all!
Unless you are paying the toll to get to some of the restaurants it hardly seems worth crossing. It is picturesque from the outside though.
Yes the bridge is quite charming, but you cross it in 30 seconds. You need a ticket to cross. Don't bother paying to go into little temple thing in the middle. Not much to write about really.

Japanese covered bridge We were looking forward to visiting this bridge. To go inside you need to buy ticket as their is security at both entrance. The bridge is very short. We could see the entire inside whilst standing outside the entrance. Not much to see. We just took a picture of it from outside. That's as much I would recommend.
Just a small bridge that looks run down and they try to make you pay for it as well! Would not spend more than 2 minutes there!
It is ok. Not worth wasting a ticket to walk across though. The ancient town ticket for 5 places was a waste of money and time
What is all the hype? Maybe you need a guide to give you more perspective on the bridge but by itself it's nothing special. Yes its old, yes its Japanese but I wouldn't go out of my way to see it.

Wanted to take refuge here during a heavy downpour and was told I have to go back into the rain and buy a ticket from across the road. I thought the ticket would allow me access to a special area of the old town... except it just literally lets you see this TINY bridge and you can see the other side of it by walking around. It's not expensive so you can contribute to preserving it, I just thought it was very random like taking a toll route when there's a free one right beside.
It is a nice bridge but in my opinion not worthy to pay for! They have only a small room with some photographs and that is all!

I think if I hadn't been there I wouldn't feel I didn't see anything special.also you have to pay a ticket to pass the bridge.and we are talking about a 10 meter bridge.I passed threw a normal bridge next to it.

If you have seen the picture you have seen it all. Don't come here just for it. But if you are in the area then no harm to just drop by I guess. They check for tickets coming in from the old town, but I just went to the other bridge 50m down and cross over to enter from the other side which they don't check. Weird.
It is a very small bridge that you have to pay to walk across. I am sure its very historic but it is just a bridge.
A wreck of a building over a filthy canal This is the symbol of Hoi An? I think not. A sorry wreck surrounded by fields of selfie-stick vendors. Skip it.
Didn't do much for me It's just a bridge, which we got charged to go on, I really did not impress or do much for me to be honest.
Couldn't get too excited about it until they wouldn't let us walk across without paying. Unless your really into this stuff it isn't a big attraction.

Located as the entrance to Hoi An ancient town, i did not see anything great in this bridge.
