A very short covered concrete crowded bridge where below is a stagnant dirty water that seems to be a good place to breed mosquitos and rats. If you love history, this is a must to visit but find out yourself the history of this bridge first before coming to this place, only then you will appreciate it.

The whole experience of travelling to Hoi An is not about this bridge. Very busy, quite dilapidated and goes over a small stretch of water perfect for rats and mosquitoes! I sound such a philistine but we were very underwhelmed. the old town itself is good though if touristy.

Not much there to see really. A nice story and guys asking for tickets. That's it. It is all dirty all around. One nice side and incredibly jut off the bridge it is the end of the world. Not nice to walk around with touts all over
The kind of things that you must see but not more than that. The bridge was built 400 hundred years ago. It well presereved. Nice,no more than that

Just not very interesting Just a bridge over a dirty beck, nothing much spectacular about it. Go at night when it's lit up and slightly more attractive. They also tried to charge me to cross it. What's that about?

the river water has badly polluted to BLACK 我喜歡會安古鎮. 當中的一個著名景點是日本橋. 但當我看到日本穚時, 我驚呆了. 因為它下方的河水是黑色的. 像是被古鎮的家庭廢水給污染似的, 成了一條黑水河. 我無法理解為何沒有乎呢? 一個觀光景點卻有惡臭旳河水, 成了不愉快的經驗. I enjoy the Hoi An old street, but however, it comes to the famous remaining of Japanese bridge, I was disappointed. It seems like local people exhaust house waste water into the river, and had been made the water turned into black....What a pity.

Just like all the other reviews. Ok, nothing else to see quick visit and move on over to the night matkets. Its nice at night but even in september there are so many people. Night markets are more fun.
Just a bridge, prettier at night. You need to pay a small free 120,000 VND per person. Multi-entry. We didnt bother given the exterior is really the feature.

Was looking forward to seeing this bridge. Couldn't believe it when I got there though...horribly packed and run down in my opinion. It could be lovely if they took better care of it. I know a lot of people on here think this place is great, I just don't see it.
Wouldnt recommend anyone to buy a ticket to just cross over the bridge. There are just more shops on the other side.

Avoid this place. I only visited the bridge as the ticket was included in my day trip, however if I had to buy this ticket now, I probably wouldn't. It's packed with tourists, selfie sticks and begging. Way too crowded for a tiny bridge and some lanterns.

The Japanese Covered Bridge is very short and personally I didn't find it worth the money. There's just a little temple that talks a bit about the Chinese zodiac. There was just nothing stand-out, special or that interesting.
The bridge was nothing special. At night the bridge was coloured green making it very tacky. We ancient centre around the bridge is very Nice.
The bridge is nice to visit if you are in the neighbourhood, but don't worry too much if you miss it.
Hoi An Historic town is a charming visit and the bridge helps in that regard. However, it is really only just a bridge.

Everyone makes this bridge sound so great but it's not. The bridge is tiny and not as ornate as I thought. I heard you didn't have to pay to cross the bridge but there are people checking tickets right at the entrance. It makes the whole experience even more touristy than it has to be.
There is a Japanese Covered Bridge... A bit lame, it's only worth seeing if you're in the area. I would not come here to see it especially. It's exactly what you see in the pictures, nothing more.
Nothing special about it. This is an old bridge nut it's not something special that has detailed craftsmanship on it. It's just an old bridge with history of the Japanese.

Does not worth Overpassing by Donating about 4 Pounds to Hoi an heritage town. If they are maintaining Anything like this, Than I really can't understand Why are they Forcefully Making People paying the donation.
From the outside the bridge looks really nice, but you have to buy a ticket to enter/cross the bridge. Totally not worth it since you can see the inside when standing in front of the entrance.

For tourists looking for a photo opportunity No sorry, I think you need to get out more if this is an important attraction. Get a life, make some friends, go somewhere good. My excuse for seeing it - short cut, no fee paid. Thats how good it is.

This is a lovely old bridge but the water, if you can call it that, beneath is black and it stinks. There is a lot of rubbish as well & considering you are charged to enter, when they feel like it, not sure what the money is being used for. It is worth a look but don't get too excited.
Yeah - it's a covered bridge. Covered with TOURISTS. Good luck getting a good photo on the weekend, let alone getting near it. Perhaps try going early morning on a weekday.
A lot of fuss about a very small bridge! Ok for a photo but that's about it. Hoi An has so much more to offer as you walk the streets around the bridge.

We went to walk over one night to be told we needed a ticket to walk about 10 steps. So went went around the block of old town and came across the other way for nothing. Best photos are off the bridge anyway.
jammed solid with people pushing you out the way every night, best time to see it during the day. Have to pay to cross over
recommend you spend the money on hiring a motorbike and then view this from road. Dont pay to go inside.
Nice to take a picture of but I wouldn't pay to cross it or be stuck in a thick crowd when it's that hot. Stand on the pedestrian bridge nearby and photograph it.

It's a bridge, it's old and it's historical. There you go. You wouldn't realise you just stepped through an important piece of history, with all the people walking back and forth. It's sad to see such an important heritage being utilised for everyday use.

A beautiful historic monument that should probably be protected from the tourist hoards because its soo important and precious. Otherwise its pretty and makes a nice photo but thats about it.Iwent for a photo at 5am and had tea with the only street vendor around at the time. Like most if Hoian-a total let down.

What.... I have to pay to cross! I was heading to the night markets and came across the bridge. I went to cross and I was told I needed a ticket, didn't even ask how much. I reckon you would walk no more than 15 steps to get across and I think that exaggerating how short it is. You can easily take photos of it from the side or from another bridge running parallel with it. Anyway to exploit the tourist.

Due to the position of our hotel we were always crossing the bridge to go to shops and restaurants . Otherwise it was a long way around . I was reliably informed you do not need a ticket to cross the bridge but just to go into the small museum on the bridge . As 4 of us are in the family and we crossed it many times a day tickets would have been rediculous so it became a debate each time we crossed . Very annoying

Bought the event tickets or whatever, 5 entrances to sites for 120,000 dong. Used one on this bridge and it was kind of a waste. The bridge is indeed pretty, but you can admire and take pictures from the outside without having to walk through and use a ticket.
The bridge looks interesting and worthy of pictures. We used one of our tokens to enter the bridge. It doesn't have much to look at inside - best pics and view from outside
Tiny old bridge, it didn't do much for me, crossed a polluted river into more shops and restaurants. Wouldn't make a special trip but if your near I guess it's good to have a look.

Don't see a point to pay for it. Yes it's pretty but why would you have to pay to walk through a bridge? You can walk around it so it's not like it's the only way. Also I don't really like the green light castes on the bridge at night it would benefit from more natural light to bring up its beauty.

Underwhelming but worth a glance Not particularly pretty, but probably the biggest 'icon' of Hoi An. Worth a glance as you walk through the town, especially in the morning when there are fewer tourists crowding it. At night for some reason they insist on lighting it with fluorescent green lamps which give it a weirdly ethereal glow. The paltry stream trickling beneath it often smells and has rubbish in it when we visited. Certainly worth visiting but perhaps for a couple of minutes before moving along. Underwhelming.

A Bridge with a history This bridge leads to more shops in the Old Town, the same you would have seen on the side before entering the Japanese bridge, so don't be disappointed when this happens. You will pay to go across the bridge. One would hope that funds collect go towards maintaining the integrity of this structure.
Yes, it's nice to have an old bridge but it is extremely crowded to walk on. It is 'free' now that you pay the entrance fee for the area at the bridges into the ancient town (120 000 vnd pax)

I found the bridge to be a tad overrated. There wasn't really much to it. Don't get me wrong...it's well built and nice to look at from afar. That being said its not worth a unique trip to. If you have 2 minutes and it's included in your tour than do it. Don't make a special trip though!
I understand this is an ancient bridge and has been kept in amazing condition but you just take a snap and walk away. The actual old town has much more to offer than this bridge
It's an old bridge, wow! We walked over this bridge 2 years ago and it looked the same so we passed on this major attraction. - Hoi An has gone commercial so now this is a toll bridge.

Very pretty at night time The Japanese bridge is worth a short visit.... In fact you have to cross it to enter the old town from one of the four entrances. It is a solid structure Nd has been rebuilt in part several times but keeping with the original Japanese design. It is best views at night time from the river side. As it lights up in different colours... Very pretty.

So we wanted to cross the bridge on a rainy day and the lady holding the door (nowhere near as gullible as Gandalf! Nor Hodor!) did not let us pass without buying a "ticket". Of course we had to take a 20 minute detour under pouring rain and nearly ruined our first day in Hoi An. Obviously they were trying to take advantage of the weather, it turns out the ticket was for the pagoda inside and the next 4 days without rain nobody was forced to buy a ticket including us. Shame! (Not as bad as Cersei) Bridge is lovely tho, but that Sparrow looking lady just killed it for us.
People pay to walk across this old decrepit bridge. I know it is old, but the fuss escapes me. It's full , ole nad you have too pay.

Really why would anyone want to buy a ticket to see a old bridge with a roof it's nothing special save your money. At night if you stand on the free walkers bridge you can take a photo of it when it is lit up

Althought the bridge is pretty, is is always packed and officials try an extort money each time one crosees. We were unsure of the lega, ity of this and nevrer paid. I believe it should be a voluntary contribution.
If you are there then take a look but otherwise not worth the visit. It costs money to enter or walk across so we just took the regular bridge next to it. Looks better at night.
Unique but overpriced Unique but overpriced! The bridge is so small that you can see the detail from either side of it. You don't need to cross it.

The covered bridge it ok I suppose. You have to have a paid ticket to cross during the day but at night there is no charge. The bridge itself isn't anything spectacular, in fact most people just used it as a crossing rather than a landmark to behold.

The bridge itself is beautiful, but we wanted to see the shops on the other side of bridge and we're prevented from crossing unless we bought tickets Disappointing because alternative route around to shops was long. So was off putting to visit. We didn't want to cross for pictures, just to get to the other side.
A different price for foreigners Why should foreigners pay a different price to other Vietnamese tourists to see this bridge. It's not worth it at any price.

Nothing special...why pay? There was nothing special about this wooden bridge, besides a few small dragon sculptures on the top. Why bother paying for the ticket? Not worth it. Take a quick night shot from the sidewalk and that's good enough.
I went there around 6:30 pm and it was lit up by horrid colored lights, like middle eastern themed bar mitzvah, or sponsored by Benetton. If you go there during the day, I am sure it looks very nice.
Quite underwhelmed. Lots of tourists looking at an old bridge on the edge of the Old Town. If you have survived all the calls for boat trips, clothes and shoes to buy, reward yourself with a… bridge.

It is really a small bridge that takes barely 20 seconds to cross. There is no distinct feature except for its age and the covered roof. It has grown in popularity only because of the history it represents and being a symbol of Hoi An. If you enter from the direction of Tran Phu St, you will be stopped for a ticket check which is for the 120,000 dong Hoi An old town tourist levy that entitles you to visit 5 attractions. After crossing the bridge, walk along Nguyen Thi Minh Khai St for a pleasant view of a row of shops and eatery.

Nice but just another bridge It's nice to walk over and take some photos at night but it's just another bridge full of people taking selfies. If you were Japanese it may have some more meaningful significance. You can't miss it in the old town so everyone sees it anyway.

This is a tiny little bridge so crowdedall the time that it is almost impossible to know you are actually on it. But as it is in the old quarter of Hoi An you would come across it anyway so you don't have to go out of your way to see it.
Nothing too interesting Honestly not much to see. So crowded with people standing around and people pushing bikes through. In my opinion not really interesting.

Interesting but overrated Nice example of this type of architecture but well worn and preservation is only reasonable. Worth visiting as part of a rip to Hoi An old town but it is not a stand alone attraction.
Compared to the pagodas and houses maybe not really worth getting excited about. It is an old bridge and in the back you can see some historic photos.
They only let you in for free onto the bridge if you cross from West to East and the staff are rude. An average looking bridge.

Sad we now have to pay to walk over it We've walked over it before, but this time had to pay and I can't remember doing that before. I could be wrong on this count. I guess they need the dollars to keep it in good condition, but it just seems like a rip off this time round. We chose to stand outside to look at it. Pity. Seems everything else you have to pay for too. Good old rich tourists. Got fed up with being asked for 450,000 dong for a cheap T-shirt. Bit mind blowing when we finally paid 80,000 dong which is all it was worth given the quality of the cotton. Watch out tourists, Hoi An seems to be ripping you all off these days. Caveat emptor - let the buyer beware!
Nothing to get excited about! This I seen a few times now and can't really get excited about it, although the bridge has been built really well and strong so will be around in many years to come
I don't see the big deal with the bridge. There is always way too many people so you can't exactly appreciate the view. I wouldn't bother checking this out.

I get that Hoi An is a UNESCO sight. I get it that this bridge is really old and holds so much history but I feel like they have commercialized it too much. You actually pay just to cross the bridge and since it is narrow, people tend to crowd at the entrance because they have to stop to pay.
If you didnt know, you probably wouldnt realize you walked across a so called famous bridge because it's really nothing special
Historic stop was really overcrowded and small and not too much to see compared to the other sites I could have used your ticket at.
Did not find this over-crowded bridge to be something special. It is nice, by all means, but not worth a second look.
Very, very small. Literally 2m long. Not very colourful and doesn't particularly stand out. It is convenient to get to the market side however.

You can take a picture there (crossing costs) You have to pay for the bridge -I don't know how much- but I wouldn't cross it. It's just a few meters so.. And there's another bridge 15m away where u can cross the river. But the bridge looks really nice so it's definitely worth a picture :-)
They now charge to just walk over this bridge, it was free three years ago when we were last there. Avoid this by walking over the footbridge by the riverside then cut in to see the shopping street.
It is nothing special about this small bridge except for its historical value. You need to pay just to cross it.

I was walking there by mistake the day before the day I meant to do some sightseeing and didn't really noticed anything beautiful or worth seeing.. On the day I went sightseeing I was required to pay to cross it (!?) and was really underwhelmed by anything having to do with it...
Amazing spot to see amateur Chinese models but other than that, a let down. Even going on the 6 meter bridge will cost you, not much but enough for you to throw up over the railing.

We made our way towards the Japanese Covered Bridge while in Hoi An. We took a couple of cute photos from the outside. When we got up to the bridge they asked for money for a ticket to get on. We looked through to see if it was worth it and there truly wasn't anything else in it. The bridge itself is small so I don't believe it is worth paying to get on. There is some history attached to it-- it's the only known covered bridge with a Buddhist temple attached, but nothing more significant than that, that I've read up on. Overall, it's pretty, but nothing spectacular.

Not sure what the hype is about It's an old bridge, but it's neither pretty nor spectacular in any way. I certainly would not pay money to cross it. You can easily loop around to get to the other side.
It does look amazing but is not worth the hype. We just went and saw it, we didn't pay to walk over it. Really it is just an old bridge.

Run down and unimpressive I walked straight past the Japanese Covered Bridge when first looking for it! That's how distinctive and memorable it is! It is very run down and falls way short of the pictures you see in travel agents and websites advertising it. Like others have said before locals stand at the entrance to the bridge demanding payment to walk on it when you only have to pay to enter the small and unremarkable temple at the end. If you want to say you've been here just take a photo from the outside. It really isn't worth parting with any money to view.
A small bridge that they tried to charge me to walk over. Full of tourists. Went around to small bridge nearby and came back around from other side and crossed free of charge.
Seriously guys, it's small, it's a bit fancier than any other bridge, but it's still just a bridge. And you still have to fend off ppl trying to collect a charge from you
The bridge is very small. Apparently they started collected fees to walk on the street , the other side is the old town, a very small bridge, nothing to shout about.
Really not a big deal Like i said, not a big deal to me. Just a small overcrowded bridge. The rest of the old town is much more interesting.
Go from the other side and you dont get asked for a ticket. And its a nice bridge, however it is only about 10m long and will take you about 1 minute to look at.

Just a bridge... Underwhelming I agree with a lot of the previous reviews.. Its underwhelming and considering its hype (is on one of the bills), i expected a little more. Short bridge, it was packed with people when we got there, sort of looks like it's falling apart...

Interesting but underwhelming Very beautiful from afar and interesting. However upon arrival, a little underwhelming, with staff not interested in explaining what exactly is the history, no signs or pamphlets in English.
Not much here but close to everything else so no big deal to detour. Nothing much on other dude. A few sculptures along the bridge. Better view from the island for photographs

Just not much if anything. Absolutely teaming with tourists at any time of day. We walked over it (with no ticket? Maybe just need that to view the little room inside?) Anyway, nothing special I'm afraid, barely worth a visit.

Worst export product of japan Overpriced bridge which is anything but a landmark. Only the pillars of the bridge are genuine. Funny how hoi an charges the tourists for their highlights as they are almost brand new.
This is a poorly maintained tourist trap. We took the footbridge about 20 feet away rather than pay. It needs refurbishing and is nothing aw some at all

Nice photo, poor staff and inside So when I Hoi An you will get asked to buy local attraction tickets. This is a nominal fee and to be honest I didn't mind paying. However, a lady kept asking each night we visited Hoi An (7 nights) to produce a ticked to cross the bridge! Well actually you only need it to go inside the rooms in the bridge. Someone might want to tell this jumped up staff member! The view of the bridge is great, its an iconic Hoi An photo. Inside is poor. Very little information is given. Loads of people were allowed across without issue, because we looked particularly untanned, e.g. New to the location this woman decided to have a go! Yet no other were asked within 5 minutes we observed.

Don't pay to go across The bridge is old but nothing particularly majestic about it. Can cross the other bridge in the old town area to take a far away shot of the japanese bridge. Not much more to it then the pictures you can already see on the internet!
Worth seeing but from a distance. There are good photo opportunities for the bridge but if you want to walk over it you get charged!! Wouldn't recommend !!

Enough said. We used one of our 5 local sightseeing tickets (you need them for the places to see in the ancient town) to go from the town towards the outskirts but we didn't get asked for the ticket the other way. Go round and come from the "monkeygod" side if you don't want to use one of the tickets for it.
To be honest 20 seconds will cover it, it's a old bridge that has seen better days. Quick photo and move on
It is chargeable to visit this old bridge. Nothing to crowed about. Not a healthy place to visit with the sulphuric rotten egg smell emitting from the stream below. A distant view would be safer.

Very busy, not spectacular. Very small bridge, not very special and allot of people surrounding the bridge. If you don't have tickets you are not allowed to cross the bridge, even if you are not going to visited the little room in the bridge.
Not really worth the visit, very over rated, loads of people jostling and a lot smaller than anticipated

Japanese Bridge maintenance Re visiting the Japanese Bridge at Hoi an after 7 years was quite disappointing. The general upkeep of the bridge was woeful, with the accumulated rubbish in the polluted water an embarassment even as a visitor.
My opinion is that it not a must-see sight in Hoi An and if its not in your way, don't change your plans for it.

Its a bridge...hooray Its small an not especially impressive. Th history of it is kind of cool but i was not exactly blown away. The good news is that you will probably end up seeing it just walking around the town so its kind of a freebie
Nice but nothing special Small bridge..need to pay ticket to enter..the tickets to the old city cost 120000 dong and it includs the ancient buildings

brief history is interesting but actual architecture and size is a disappointment. looks really OK in day light but looks decent in the night with coloured focus lights... overall, you will not miss much if you skip this.

You just cant miss the Japanese bridge if you are in Hoi An old town, because it occupies a prominent space in the layout of the town. But, the bridge per se is nothing exceptional. The only part I liked was crossing over to the other side of the town on the bridge, which I found to be quieter than the traditional part of the old town. You can give this a miss, if you are short of time in Hoi An.
If you are very interested on history, maybe it is worth to visit. But for me i think there is not really special and we have to but ticket to cross the bridge so finally we walk in the old town only

What are you paying for? Bridge is beautiful, but we were asked to pay 240,000VND (approx $16AU for two people) to cross it, there is another bridge and many ways to get to the other side anyway. A donation only option would probably be better, in my opinion .

Not worth paying for! I was a bit surprised to hear about so many people being charged to cross this; having crossed it myself many times without being asked for payment. If a lot of money is being raised from tickets, I'm unsure of where it is being spent, since it is looking more than a little tired and in need of restoration, which is a shame for a building with such historic value. Nice photo opportunity from the outside at night but the bridge itself is often rammed with tourists. Also worth noting is the strong sewage smell at low tide.
Nice to see but not worth a trip down there only for the bridge. Make sure you combine with other activities.
So sad that this bridge has just become a money making scheme like the rest of Hoi An Ancient town. Over rated.

Not worth the ticket, walk a few meters and use wood bridge instead You have to use 1 of 5 tickets to cross the Japanese Bridge from the old town side. Don't waste the ticket as there is nothing worth seeing inside. Instead, as you are facing the entrance to the bridge take the road on the left, walk down and you will see another flat wooden bridge. You can cross this bridge for free. Walk along the river bank for maybe 100m and there is a small alley that will connect you to back to Nguyen Thi Minh Khai street where all the souvenir shops are. Then walk back and cross the Japanese bridge (they don't check ticket from this side).

Don't waste money for the ticket, it is the same look as if you go through the ticket booth (aka the bridge). Unless you want to see some shops over the bridge... (There is no point paying to go shopping there anyway). Great architecture of the bridge btw

Bridge in need of some new paint..... Well worth a visit if you have a spare 45 seconds, but that's about it. Best to visit during the evening as at least it looks slightly better lit up, but beware of people selling you paper lanterns to place into the water which then look terrible the next morning littering up the river banks. As for the tourist ticket at VND 120k is a complete waste of time so don't bother buying them, firstly there are 20+ attractions for which your ticket gives you 5 entries for which one is this bridge, if they persist then simply take the other bridge 20 meters away which at least has a better view if the bridge your trying to cross
nothing special but to be able to see the old houses, you need to buy the ticket at at the ticket office, one of which is right the opposite of the bridge.
Not much to see. There's history you can feel. This was the second time I visited this bridge. Won't revisit again in the future

Pictures make this bridge look pretty, but there is very little to it and it's not pretty at all. The river around it is extremely smelly and unpleasant. Some nice little shops on either side of it, but the bridge itself was very disappointing.

It's a lot smaller than I had expected it to be from looking at photos. There's just not much to it for all the hype it's given! Just a bridge.,,it is pretty though but don't pay to go through it or anything because there's not much to see unfortunately.
To much tourist attention for and old bridge This bridge is like full with people all the time. It's therefore verry crowded but looking good though for it's old structure.
Unfortunately, although this is a large part of the history of Hoi An, it has not been looked after and looks very sad and disreputable.

Looked at a small bridge and we were about to cross but got asked to pay about $100,00 dong ($6aus) so we said no thanks. Although we found out later it would have got us into several other places of interest.
Not really an attraction Worth a picture and a nice bridge into the old town but nothing to get excited about. Certainly not worth waiting around to cross at peak time, just a small covered bridge

The bridge is pretty fine, with a pair of monkeys and dogs on each side. But the river is so dirty! We came here in rainy season, but the river is so shallow and covered by corrupted plants. The smell made me quit the bridge quickly
Okay it's a slightly quaint bridge that is covered but its barely much of an attraction. At most, you will snap a photograph and leave in a minute. The shrine inside is not worth your ticket.

The bridge itself is nice but the sewer, yes the sewer Under the bridge very smelly and dirty and just not pleasant to be around. It's just a shame to the one of the symbols of the city and millions of tourists coming every year to see it. There's nicer bridge like this one in Hue.

the day we went there a wedding had occured, the happy couple were getting photos with the bridge as a back drop, if you thnk back to what tools were available back then it's quite impressive, not big. If you want to dodge the ticket sellers get across further down the street and walk around to come in from nth entry, a donation is expected but not compulsory if you go into the temple on the bridge.
Can really give it a pass. Loved the charm of the surrounding area though. The bridge itself is crammed with tourists and not very well maintained.
The bridge is tiny and squat. If I hadn't had a map and the bridge been swarming with tourists, I literally would have walked right over it looking for the amazing bridge.
I was quite surprised at how only tourists and not locals were being directed to buy tickets to cross this bridge which would take less than 12 steps to walk across.
wouldn't pay to walk over it Being asked to pay was wrong, I think a donation is more appropriate. And being a Christian I wouldn't pay to see a Buddhist Shrine right in the middle of it.

I don't like leaving poor reviews, however me and my girlfriend were confused when we saw the bridge. We literally spent 5 minutes walking across and taking photos and then left. The bridge is a very small with absolutely nothing very impressive. It is free to walk across and right in downtown, yet it still was rather unappealing . I am not very educated in ancient chinese/japanese involvement in Vietnam, I believe it is more of a sight for the locals as it plays a major role in their history and is printed on the Vietnamese 20 000 Dong bill.

Did not let us in the bridge we went in the evening and tried to walk over the bridge but the person in charge of letting people pass just turned us away. Others were aloud to pass ?!? What's up with that!
This is one of those hardly anything to see spots and I don't know why it's an "attraction". This review would have been shorter but for the minimum character requirement.

I get that it's old and I appreciate that, but it is a covered bridge with a temple inside. I visited during Lunar New Year and walked through at night when it was free with my friend who lives in Da Nang when it was packed with people there for the Lunar Festival. It was dark. Maybe that was part of the problem in my ability to really appreciate it. I just think I would have really been disappointed if I'd have paid money to cross/see the inside of this bridge. It only took us about 45 seconds to walk across it. I glanced into the temple as we passed through. As a Christian, it is not something I would have paid to visit. I can't give a fair review of the temple itself because I didn't actually go inside that part. I did, however, take pictures of the outside of the bridge, which is architecturally beautiful.

I know it's old but don't seek it out I know this is an ancient monument, built hundreds of years ago, but you can't actually see much of it now because of all the tourists. Stand and the river and look back at it and take a photo, then move on!

There were much more interesting and meaningful things to see than to squeeze in the packed bridge full of tourist, not even have a corner to turn. I happened to be here during peak hour and waited long but the crowd hardly slows down. If the bridge is not too packed of people, then it will be a nice experience to slowly admire the building itself.

Takes seconds to cross and not much to see or study. To the locals maybe it means a lot for their history of the town but there are many more spectacular bridges in the world to gape at. And as for what's in the stream that flows under it well, that's another story.

Needs re-painting and hawkers everywhere We'd been made aware beforehand that people would try and charge us to cross the bridge, and it did indeed happen on one occasion, so we just told them to leave us alone and kept walking. When you're on the bridge it looks decent enough. however from the street you can see that it needs some work on it & the paintwork has all peeled off, although hopefully they rectify this now and then.

Granted the bridge has historical significance and has a special place in Vietnamese culture, the bridge itself doesn't offer much. It's a small bridge that's overcrowded and other than a small temple in the middle, doesn't have much. You'll probably walk across it if you are in HoiAn but don't set your expectations too high. Avoid buying the ticket for the bridge. You can see it from outside.
Not a big deal if you miss this Japanese Covered Bridge during your visit to Hoi An. Worth a ticket if you bought one already.
This is highly touted, but it is a rather dull attraction. Photos are mediocre no matter what the weather or sunlight. They even charge you to walk across it.

You walk a few metres over a bridge for $5, so what! I'm sure the bridge has some significance which was beyond me. Or, was it a rip off. You didn't accept my initial review for "lack of characters" (100 minimum) but there is nothing to write about. Sorry.

I have travelled all over South East Asia and was looking for another destination. Friends said Vietnam - you will love it. I have been here 2 weeks - I don't. 1 week HMC City to go to the Meekong -agina don't bother - its a river. Then a week here in Hoi An. Next wek Hanoin and Ha Long Bay. There is nothing here. Vietnam must have ben comoletely destroyed in the war and has been rebuilt in concrete. There is no culture, no music, no dancing, no architecture and definitely no cuisine. Hoi An has 2 or 3 old houses, you have to pay to go inside. Every other shop is an expensive bar / restaurant / souvenier shop. It is simply a resort organised for tourism. Behind the pretty silk lights, the people are letting the new buildings decay. They do not knwo how to maintain them. At the beach - a 5 kilometer walk (there is nothin else to do), there are 3 huge new hotel building complexes abandoned. Th Chinese are building several others. Expect there to be a lot of positive marketing of Vietnam in the next few years to collect foreign currenct. Go to Thaland and the islands, or Indonesia / Malaysia / Borneo / Cambodia. When you have done them - go to Hawaii !

Not the best tourist attraction It is a beautiful bridge, but at the end of the day it is a bridge and serves its purpose as a bridge. there was no tour or no explanation to its significance in Hoi An. I preferred the area of Hoi An before the bridge, if you don't travel over it don't feel like you are missing out on anything.
Interesting old bridge with a small shrine in the middle, but not worth using up one of your tickets to visit.

We only saw the bridge because we walked past it without realising. To be honest the bridge is nothing special. I would not go out of my way to see it. If you don't get the opportunity to see this bridge you aren't missing out on much!

Wondering what all the fuss is about. While the covered bridge is interesting and presents a range of options for photographic opportunities, the bridge itself, as a tourist attraction, is somewhat over-rated. I certainly agree that some of the angles for photographic experience of the bridge are great, especially at night when it is illuminated, but overall, I can only say that for me, as a tourist attraction, the bridge really did not appeal to me that much at all.

Literally is just a small bridge Sure it's historic and nice to look at, especially at night, but I'm suprised people paid to go on it. It's just a tiny little wooden bridge. If you just cross to the other side on another bridge it's free and then you can walk back across the bridge for free.

Not very special, see the animals inside when entering the bridge. Mandatory ticket boots for entering the old city. At evening it's crowded the colored lampoons give this city a high Disney World atmosphere.

Nice, but not worth taking the time. This 'attraction' will last you all of 5 minutes. Don't go out of your way to get here. We happened to go when it was free during the full moon event in Hoi An, and we're glad we didn't plan around it. Seriously, it's a 5 minute thing. It's kind of embarrassing they charge tourists.
Do not waste your ticket on this. You can look from the outside. It is just a small room with an alter. nothing to rave on.

They charge to cross the bridge and it's tiny! There's only a small bit of information inside and so it's not really worth it to pay. You're better off just taking photos of the cool design outside then walking over the other bridge only 20 meters away!

Until just now opening trip advisor, we did not realise the small boring bridge we crossed each morning was some sort of attraction. I would have been pretty angry if someone had tried to charge me money for a ticket to cross it (thankfully didn't happen). Maybe if your Japanese you'll appreciate it more or something... But to us it was a small bridge that helped us get across the river?

Meh, just okay. The bridge is small and it's free, don't pay. I thought the bridge would have been more exciting but it's tiny and there's a guy taking tickets to see it, sometimes....basically it was disorganized. It a pretty photo but the rest of town is more exciting and very lovely after dark.

Ok, I get it, it's a historical landmark, and it's very pretty. But there should not be a charge to walk across this bridge. Why is this a thing? In the end, you pay to cross a bridge (fyi, that's not necessary there is a free bridge 15 feet away) which takes you to...more shopping on the other side. Pretty, but don't nickel and dime me!

An old bridge orginally built to connect the Japanese community on one side of the stream to the Chinese quarter on the other. Not much more to say about the bridge. It's good to see from afar, but not worth the commotion.

I'm culturally ignorant so take my review with a pinch of salt I saw very little purpose in this bridge. It was just a bridge. I don't know why it warrants its own page on trip advisor. Took a picture of it because I felt like I should. However it's in town so you'll see it anyway. Some people paid, some didn't. No idea why.

You have to pay to simply walk across it!!! We wandered over from our hotel as we thought we should at least get a glimpse of this bridge. The area itself is really nice, but definitely touristy!! Remember to haggle if you buy things nearby!! Anyway, we were about to wander over this bridge after taking photos of it, when a surly Vietnamese guard told us to produce our tickets. TICKETS!! It's a 3 meter long bridge!! We just walked around it and had a look from the other side... Good to cross off the check list after seeing the assembly hall.
Very boring, nothing special but looks pretty cool, have to pay to cross the bridge, not worth it. The rest of the town is pretty enough.

Granted it was raining but I found the place lacked interest to me. Probably my fault. We had to pay and I still think "what for?" There is very little to see and the place was packed, again probably because it was raining.

Money making crossing The bridge is a bit of a curiosity. It's quite ornate, but not overly so and not very big. But it is very busy and absolutley packed with tourists. You can't even step foot on the bridge during the day without being forced to purchase a ticket - so it's a great money printing exercise for the town. Not that great an attraction either. There are ways around the bridge, but the people trying to sell tickets really don't like it when you walk around the bridge. One of them still tried to demand money when we'd gone around the bridge - don't think he liked it. Not against paying for attractions, just don't see why I should pay when I just want to visit a restaurant on the other side.
Pretty ordinary and an obvious tourist site. Offers very little apart from somewhere to visit during the street walks and tourist shopping. I would not go out of my way to visit. Crowded .

Over rated this bridge, crowded with tourists and not very photogenic, it needs a re-vamp. Marking time there was a small dog statue and snake as well as a small temple, but with all the tourists having o pay ,it could be so nice. A noisy prayer beside it via speakers did not help

I am not sure why the bridge is so popular. There isn't really anything interesting to look at except a small alter/temple mid way for worshiping and offering. There wasn't anybody around to collect the fee when we were there and we just walked across to the other side, not knowing that there was even a charge for crossing, did a bit of shopping for souvenirs and walked back across. The water under the bridge was terribly polluted and even smelled. I am not sure what the fuss is all about this bridge and the area around it. I agree with Baliroz that $6 Aus to cross the bridge is ridiculous. There must be another way to go around it to the other side, if you have to, instead of paying.
Smelled strongly of pollution. Not clear whether you needed a ticket or not. We just walked around and walked across the other way.

The bridge itself is lovely but it is absolutely ridiculous that you have to pay to cross it! You have to purchase a ticket for I think 6 things and that has to be included on the list. This is really silly cause the best view is from the outside anyway which is easily viewable from the river side. Also there is another bridge like 10 meters away and closer to the river so we just crossed that for free. Or you could go one street up from the bridge and walk around! Shame Hoi An is becoming so touristy! Tip: no one is there to man the bridge and ticket checking at night so feel free to cross over anytime after about 6!

we read that this was a must see attraction in Hoi An but were disappointed. I'm not sure what I was expecting but as its just at the end of the ancient town shopping area its not out of the way to go and see for yourself

Readin other reviews i see that you are meant to buy a ticket to visit / cross this bridge, but we didn't realise and we just wandered across with all the other tourists. We didn't even see anyone checking tickets, but there were so many tourists it was hard to see much at all. The bridge is pretty, but it's not really an attraction in its own right (in my view not really something to list on trip advisor) it's not really much different looking from some of the shops in Hoi An with their Japanese style tiled roofs, and they don't get listed. But it is worth a photo or two.
It is in the middle of a total tourist trap. The journey to visit the bridge is a maze of shops. It was minutes from start to finish

sure, get a picture of it, but dont plan a day around it. Walk past, take a photo and continue on. They also asked us to pay just to get to the other side, i took the other foot bridge and came back over the covered bridge for free. Not sure what I would have been paying for...

You are charged an entrance fee to walk across the japanese bridge. The fee also covers entrance to some other historic sites. However they are not well curated and lack detailed information. And you can walk around the the old town and arrive at places that are accessible over the Japanese bridge without paying the entrance fee.
I did not see its significance to Hoi An nor was that pointed out to me. A small Buddhist altar to one side and a red Monkey-God in front. What gives??

JUst ancient old bridge overrated by travellers. in fact there is not much to see. maybe back in its day, it was beautiful. now, it is just a bridge with great story behind it. maybe, if the water is full, then the view will be spectacular at night. but if you visit this site in the afternoon, just use your imagination why this bridge is become famous.

Look at the photos - better than the real thing I love Hoi An and would definitely recommend to anyone staying there. But paying 90000 dong to see the 'sites' is a bit much. The photos of the bridge make it look a lot more than it is. When you walk over to the other side all you get is more shops selling all the same tourist tat as where you started. Personally I do not think it is worth it. Look at it from the riverside for free
Looks very old but is dirty .the surrounding town and restaurants are fantastic
The bridge is nice and obviously very old. It is quite small and just overcrowded with tourists and local people who are selling stuff. We had to wait in line to cross it even.

Below Average "Attraction" One can enjoy the average beauty of the exterior of the bridge from the walks. And one can cross this small bridge for free and look at 4 old carvings of monkeys and the wood support structure. Just don't stop to look as then you become liable for the "looking at the bridge" charge. Strange. Not worth stopping.
Its an old bridge and nice to walk by but nothing memorable.

Good pictures to be taken outside of the bridge. Yet, you will be charged to walk across the bridge. You are charged more as a foreigner than a Vietnamese. We found that you can continue walking and arrive on the other side of the bridge without paying anything. Watch out for the locals. After a day in Hoi An, our impression was that they were liars and thieves. We will not be visiting Hoi An again.
overated oh hum! Anything else is just more than this deserves

A disappointing visit. I expectd Hoi An to be colorful and unique, but it (especially the Japanese Bridge) did not live up to expectations. We were also shifted from the nice Hoi An Hotel to the Hoi An Pacific Hotel, which was the worst one we stayed at in Vietnam (year 2004). Rooms were small and the food terrible.
Found it quite difficult to properly see the interior due to the large crowds, which apparently is the norm. That said it's worth braving the crowds or even just seeing from the outside.
There is nothing to see here. Just get a photo clicked while you wait for your lantern boat ride and that's about it. The bridge does have a small history but nothing interesting as such.

If you’ve got a spare 3 minutes! When in Hoi An it’s hard not to find this ‘covered’ bridge. It’s probably best viewed from across the river. We decided to go inside and view the small museum housed on the inside of the bridge. You need to buy a ticket from a nearby building. The ticket gives you access to three other nearby locations. Armed with our tickets we went into the museum, 3 minutes later we came out. Honestly it’s not worth it.

The bridge itself and great and nice and has a lot of history and nice architecture. But it is. What's pulling down is all the exhausted sellers who go into people and want to sell things to them. And the prices are of course 3 times more than a few hundred meters away. Automatically translated
The Japanese covered bridge was just ok in my opinion. It's a quick view. There are mini shrines there to admire. Took us less than 5 minutes to explore, snap some pictures, and left.

This is an interesting looking bridge but not necessarily some to actively seek out. There is a small temple halfway across the bridge - it's not large, nor is the bridge - that is ticketed. Our tour group had access but I'm not sure it's a must-see.

A bridge to get you over the river A quaint bridge with temple in the middle. Good architecture and nice photo but it's a bridge that joins two sides of the river, albeit perhaps joining two different cultures. You have to see it if you're in the old town but don't expect to do more than take a photo and cross it
We crossed the bridge a few times and it has an exciting content both historically and culturally. Not an ordinary bridge.

Beautiful in the brochures! One of the tourist attractions is the Japanese bridge over the Thu Bon river, which at the time connected the western Japanese district with the Chinese district in the old center. It was built in 1593 as a covered bridge. In 1719, a Vietnamese temple was built on the northern part of the bridge. During our visit she was completely dismantled for thorough restoration. So we will have to make do with a photo on the yard fence or the internet.
A little disappointed they are renovating the bridge at the moment. Would have been nice if the bridge was intact . Maybe next time I come over it will be finished.

Had a hard time locating it…. I had difficult time locating this covered bridge due its restoration projet going on. I passed it several times, not realizing it was the bridge that I was looking for. Noted some diggings on under this bridge, no signs when this proect would be completed. Took several pictures here before moving to my next attractions to visit.
The Japanese bridge was under restoration... As it was covered, there was little of the bridge to be seen... We didn't see much information around it. We climbed a ladder and went down...
The Hoi An Japanese Bridge is completely dismantled for a complete renovation. We don't see much of the construction site.
Currently under renovation..... It is possible, to enter the building that completely covers the section that was once the bridge, which is currently being rebuilt, or return when it is finished
Not available , under refurbishment The bridge is in complete refurbishment , it would be great but for the moment it is not available ………… waiting to see it next visit
Under the renovation as of Aug. 2023 It was under the renovation in August 2023. The whole bridge was covered by the building. You could sneak to see the part of bridge but not worth it.
As of right now the bridge is closed for maintenance. You can't walk over it and can only see parts of the structure from behind the metal fencing.

Bridge under construction Please note that the Bridge is under construction June 2023 (not sure for how long) it cannot be viewed. However it is worth walking toward it for all the shops and restaurants along the way (Chau Kitchen is lovely) and walk through it to the other side more many more shops and food stalls

Wish I could have seen it! For me, this was disappointing as I really wanted to see it. Unfortunately the bridge is covered in scaffolding and metal sheeting at the present time for renovation, so there is little to see. You can still walk across it and visit the temple though.
Great history, but over-rated as an attraction. Nothing exceptional, nor must see. It's currently being renovated, so even less to see.

Not sure what the fuss is with this bridge, although it is under a bit of construction work, but nothing great when we were there. The side view is blocked which is probably the most photographed part. They didnt clip tickets crossing the bridge as I think it was under repairs.
Busy and crowded. Bridge is very old but worth visiting. Just be prepared for lots of people stopping to take photos while you try to cross it.
Japanese Covered Bridage Nice place to visit and is in an extremely busy part of the town was included in my tour and is worth seeing if that type of thing interest you.

nice bridge - noting much inside - crowded the bridge connecting the old city with the Japanese area have some local significant in the area history. But there is not much to see. The shrine inside it is not interesting and the place is overly crowded.

There is really nothing to this attraction. It is a bridge that looks a little different than the architecture around it. It is settled in the center of a large tourist area with plenty to do around it, so you do not need to travel to get to the bridge. It is right in the middle of everything.

This ancient bridge is near one end of the old town and makes for a nice photo opportunity. I wouldn’t necessarily go out of my way just to see this but if your shopping, eating and walking in the old town you might as well check it out.
It's just normal, nothing special. At night, he prefers the lights. But it is the symbol of the city Hoi an.
Over rated and over crowded Tourist site Over rated site. Historic 400 years old bridge good for a few photos opportunities and spend 5 to 10 mins. A lot of people crowding the bridge for photos.

Lovely architecture - don't plan a special trip though As with many tourist-y sights, the bridge is always absolutely swarmed with tourists, it's very small as well and it costs to walk across it. That being said, you can't miss it from the main bridge that goes into Hoi An ancient town central area so you can admire from afar (and of course, actually see more).
Blink and you will miss it, an interesting historical building but in itself not that interesting. You have to view it in the context of the very atmospheric old town
This is a nice looking bridge. It's perhaps enjoyed more from afar on the parallel bridge. I am not sure if you need to pay to use it but it's sort of fun and interesting.
Japanese covered bridge A bit overrated but it was good to see a bit of preserved history. Could do with a proper restoration including a nice coat of paint.
Lovely. Sure it was lite up more at night last time I was here. Nice for photo. Better if more water in river below ....
Can't say it was absolutely amazing but it was an old bridge that was interesting to see. Lots of people milling around taking photos. Photo is view from the bridge

interesting part of the ancient town Wooden Bridge built during the 1500's very different to the surrounding buildings in the area. Suggest an early morning visit to beat the crowds and enjoy the scenerity of the place
This is a place to see but the locals don't treat the river with importance every time I walk by in the morning the river has a milky colour why
A great piece of history, but too crowded It's a beautiful bridge, with a lot of history behind it.Though, it's always crowded with tourists, so getting a photo there isn't so easy.
Very crowded inside so bit overrated. Can't really take a nice photo baut from outside at nite lovely.
Beautiful scenic gem great for taking photos during the day or even at night. The rustic nature and vibrant earth tone colors complements its surroundings.

I don’t understand the big draw to the Japanese Bridge except that it’s 400 years old. Some very old carved monkeys ‘guard’ each end of the bridge. It was packed with tourists. It looks like there is some fee if you enter from the east end. We unknowingly entered from the west end for free.

We visited this bridge for a photo but decided not to cross as they had a fee to pay, and we were going the opposite direction. However we later came across the bridge by accident and crossed the bridge the opposite way and there was no fee which we thought strange. I’m glad we never paid any fee as there is nothing of great interest to see.
사진으로 보아야만 예쁘다.... 가 정확합니다 호이안에서 볼수 있는 많은 것들 중 꼭 머스트 일 필요는 없어요 거리상 다 붙어있으니 스쳐가는 정도로만 This shouldn’t have been the must-go place if you have only have limited time in Hoian.
Small historic bridge Located in the old town are the bridge. It is a small bridge but are always full of people. Can take pictures from the outside

can’t see the bridge from the crowd Even though we were pretty early in the morning already there, the bridge was already crowded, later in the day got even worse. It is nice and a must see while there. Makes you think about the historical significance of Hoi An.
This is a beautiful place but there is too much people taking selfies etc. so you can barely se the bridge.
It was ok I guess for the history buffs. You have to pay and yet there is a parallel bridge next to it
The bridge is one of the main attractions in Hoi An. Still it’s not that impressive. For crossing you need a ticket, viewing it from the riverbank is free.

I didn't even realize this was a thing We were with a guide so I didn't even realize this was one of the things to see lol. It's a covered bridge and it's pretty but it's prettier from the outside than the inside. This is the bridge on one of the nation's bank notes and many people photograph the bridge beside the note for a souvenir.

The bridge it self is worth seeing. I think maybe a visit to the bridge is best in weekdays. Weekend was extremely packed with people. To visit this area you have to buy tickets. I think it cost 120k Dong for 5 tickets per person that you can use to visit locations in the old city. I think they should spend some money on restoring and clean the water running through. Looks very shabby and the water is not water anymore.
The old bridge is beautiful but the experience is destroyed by too many tourists and way too many salesmen at the streets
It's a bridge. It's old. It's interesting to think about who built it and what the place must have been like when it was built. But not something you need to plan your day around.
The place was teeming with tourists so we decided to just walk by,ended up doing that a couple of times.The architecture is spellbinding.
Interesting place, not something that is a must. Looks nice for taking pictures. The smell of the water is just terrible.

Nothing special about a bridge on a drainage The bridge must have been a beauty but in the current shape, it's just a wooden frame hanging on slightly stinking drainage. I couldn't find anything special about the bridge.
While I understand the importance of the bridge for Hoi An for me, having walked over many similar bridges in Asian countries, it is just a nice little bridge. No offence.

It would be excellent if there hadn’t been a thousand people on it. A famous landmark and a tourist destination. Yes, me and a thousand other tourists tramping through this ancient bridge. It is featured on the 20,000 dong note. So it is of cultural significance Don’t go on a Friday or a Saturday night - it is jam packed, shoulder to shoulder. And really I would have loved to take in the history of this old wooden bridge. Maybe a visit early in the morning or very late at night would be best.
Very expensive to view an old run down bridge. Not much to do, not much to see, A lot of fame, not at all impressive. Would not be my first choice for historic buildings.

Cute but nothing spectacular Very nice little bridge over a small river in the centre of old town. Always lots of people there taking foto’s so if you are in Hoi An definitely something you should see. Just don’t expect too much.
Old fashioned bridge made of wood. There is a small temple also inside the bridge. It is very sturdy also
Situated as portal into into old town, it is small and can be crowded. Not very remarkable looking but worth a stroll over.

A small covered wooden bridge crossing a black oozing stream at low tide! Better seen at night. You also had to pay to cross the bridge which at times had hoards of groups of tourists 'enjoying' the pleasure.

Short wooden bridge with entrance ticket needed. Very crowded and nothing great about it. You should only take the picture from outside from the Dog ends (another side is Monkey). Nearby a lot of shop which considered part if old town/quarter.
Stunning old bridge, lots of character and beautiful restored Very good photo opportunity. Easy to avoid the crowds if you are patient.
Quaint but over promoted Worth a look but way too much over-promotion suggesting something bigger and interesting. It is chargeable but ticket desks take up too much of the entrance area
It's a bridge that has survived a long time You'll have to learn about it on your own. They could do better at cleaning up around it. My review is too short so I'll add that it's over a river.

Must see but disappointing Visited Japanese Covered Bridge in September. While not yet the busy season, the area was very busy and difficult to view with ease. The original photo I had seen left me with the image of a quiet bridge in a country area, which in fact was the complete opposite.
Nice bit of history but to be honest I was underwhelmed by it all. Absolutely overrun by tourists aggressive hawkers and large tour groups.
It's just an ancient bridge. It has its charm to see at night but it's nothing special. Too much people there to enjoy it calmly.

To be honest it was a little underwhelming. I would recommend going in the evening when it’s lit up. During the day it’s nothing amazing to look at. Tons of people taking pictures, lots of tour groups. Hoi An town is relatively small so if you’re out in the evening walking around go by and see it.

Lovely bridge to take photos but majority of the time it is filled with tourists - so you might like to stick around for good photos It is part of the hoi an market/ancient town - so no special trip needed to explore the bridge which is very handy
Not worth paying to visit in the day as most beautiful at night when it was free for us to cross. The inside room was nothing special.

Bridge is in near immaculate condition and worth a look. It was good to see this bridge which is of considerable aesthetic beauty and very well maintained. Just a bit of a shame that the canal flowing under it is dirty and smelly - it kind of takes away from the experience somewhat.
If you are in the area nice to stop by but you will not spend hours here. You can take a nice picture.
Made it very convenient to get from one side of the river to the other side of the river.

Too busy. Overcrowded with masses of people pushing and shoving and unaware that mother, father and 3 children holding hands takes the entire width of bridge and do not understand when other people are trying to get by. Not worth it during high tourist time at night with loud music and way too much noise and people. Crazy

Crowed place, lots of European people The place was very crowed, lots of international tourists visited, mostly European, it was a small wooden bridge with 17 Century story. It was a part of UNESCO Ancient City.

Interesting if you can see it A great exhibit to visit but don’t be disappointed if you don’t see much of the bridge. It will be crammed with tourists desperate to get the best photo inside the bridge when in my opinion, the best shots are outside. It has an interesting history and has been well restored. Also the bridge offers a great route to the night markets on the other side of the river.
Absolutely packed with tourists on the bridge near the water (that you stand on to view the Old Bridge), so you'll struggle to get an unobstructed view/picture
Go early in the morning to avoid the ‘Ticket collectors’ it looks pretty and is used on the 20000 dong note. We missed the trick of holding the note up and taking the picture.
For me this is just a nice bridge in Hoi an, but it does have some history. We wnet in the evening to see the bridge with lots of other tourist. Good for photo opportunity.

It’s a nice bridge, clearly loads of history with it being over 400 years old but soooo busy that it almost ruins it. Do make sure to walk along the bridge and don’t just take photos from the side as there is a whole inside section to check out.
A lot of history surrounding this bridge. It would have been nice weren’t it for the masses of people trying to cross it. I recommend going early in the day to skip the crowds
Old and not well maintained bridge The bridge is not well maintained. The house structure between the bridge look different, one side is Japanese style while the other side is Chinese structure.

Historically beautiful but overcrowded The bridge is indeed beautiful but good luck trying to get a photo of it without it crawling with other tourists. The beauty is in the bridge itself WITHOUT the tourists. I suggest to go early in the morning before tourist buses arrive and while most tourists are still doing buffet breakfast. I give it 2 stars in tourist peak hour, 4 stars if you are lucky to see it without the tourists all over it, hence an average 3 stars.
Unfortunately the city center of Old Town is very popular.. Even in the off-season. The bridge is important and impressive but too crowdy...

The area around the Japanese Bridge is the centre point for tourist drop-offs so is always quite crowded. The bridge itself is very small and while quite nice, isn’t very Japanese. The original Japanese bridge has long since gone and the current version is Chinese/Vietnamese in style. It’s worth a look as part of seeing the old town, but would be a shame if you just stopped to see this area.
An interesting place to visit. Unfortunately the let down is that there’s no plaque or any kind of information about it. Even the info online varies vastly.

Interesting Japanese bridge One of the Hoi An sights that is worth seeing. You can go inside and see old photos of the bridge and people. A good example of the merging of Japanese and Vietnamese cultures.

Its a grand 400 year old bridge structure. Its always crowded but still worth seeing. I bought a ticket that allowed me to look inside the old structure and house. You got to hear about the history and the ticket price ensures the old structure will continue to be maintained
Beautiful bridge.. that's all All in all, it's just a bridge. A bridge where you have to pay to cross! So instead of crossing we just went through the restaurants to get to the other side.

It is the must have photo It is a walking bridge that has a side room with a temple inside . Our guide had tickets however later that day we just walked across. It seems as if they just take tickets on one side of the bridge.
It is nice place to visit. A lot of people in morning but in late afternoon all Chinese tours come so too busy. The bridge is nice but water is very bad to see.

To cross the bridge you need a ticket. Same ticket that allows entry to the other buildings. But this bridge is so small I don’t quite get the reason why. You can still see what the bridge looks like from each end.
Saw this bridge a few times while in the old town and for me and it didn't do to much for me. It's ok

Go early so can enjoy the sppecial nature of this old bridge. Wonderful, character architecture. Is the tourist hub of Hoi An though and is a pushy, hot mass of people in this tourist season making it difficult to really appreciate. Selfie city!

Take a look but nothing to get excited about If you visit the old town then walk across the bridge which joined the Japanese and Chinese sides of the old trading port. It’s a nice photo but the lantern bridge is better particularly at night with the floating lanterns and boats

When we first came to Hoi An about ten or so years ago there were a lot less tourists and no entry fees. Now they are trying to charge you $6 a person to walk 20m over the historic Japanese Bridge. Unbelievable. We can understand they need money for restoration works throughout Hoi An but why slug the tourists again. Charge the businesses which must be making a bundle with the hordes of tourists visiting there now on a daily basis .
Interesting piece of architecture but wouldn't be worth taking time to visit it it weren't right next to Ancient Town.
Well it is a short bridge and it is old. Did I like the visit? When you have kicked a few hundred touritst out of the way and taken a few obligatory pictures I don't think you need to go back.
It’s a small concrete bridge in the middle of Hoi An town build by the Japanese. Perhaps a trifle overrated at first glance but then it grows on you..something appealing about it!
Not sure i'd pay to go inside. We got some pretty good pictures just from the street. Seemed like a little tourist trap to me.
Nice to see the Japanese bridge, very crowded. Very old, well preserved. Need to by a ticket to see inside museum.

We paid for two tickets to cross the bridge as there was an official checking tickets at the entrance to the bridge. And then when we returned no one was checking tickets and was a free for all? Amazing bridge and worth a visit but why charge some people and not others?

It’s 400 years old and still looks good We enjoyed our visit to Old town Hoi An, including the Old Japanese bridge. The bridge is only 12mtrs long, however was an important way to get between the two centres It’s difficult to imagine that the bridge is so old and still in use by thousands of people daily

Beautiful but sooo crowded This bridge becomes packed with tourist after the hoards of tourist arrive in hoi An after 12pm. However if you have the opitunity to get here in the morning you will have the tranquil city all to yourself (which id highly recommend). In the evening once a week on a Monday there is a performance near the bridge by the locals depicting the local traditions. You should deffinatly take the time to stop and watch this free production.
The Japanese bridge is at the heart of the old town and is very historic. It is photogenic from any angle but particularly from acroos the opposite bank of the river.

Just standing beside the bridge and admiring the craftsmanship is enough, this aspect of it is probably a 9/10. It’s beautifully made, but spending a ticket to go inside (the room in the bridge) gives very little.

Interesting, but not that great. The bridge is quite short and gets very crowded in the evening and night. There is an alter room with some interesting photos and info posted. Nice enough, and very old so the historical value makes it worthwhile.
A 400 year old bridge over a small creek with temperatures le built into sea be side Small fee to cross: needs maintenance

Perhaps I didn't fully appreciate the bridge, maybe you need a guide you tell you all the history to make this more interesting to gain more stars. It's a very beautiful bridge, very ornate, but there's nothing more to it. There's a small temple inside which a shrine, and a small bit information about it's use but that's about it. It's good that it's very central to the ancient town such thaybwe didn't have to travel far to see it, otherwise I'd have given it less stars. I would usually expect 'top sights in Hoi An' to take more than 5 minutes to look around... Overall, if you have the tickets to see the sights of Hoi An, then it's worth coming to as it's so central, but I wouldn't pay to see it otherwise.
Must see but don’t pay to cross.. Must see but don’t pay to cross..beautiful bridge and very picturesque.... gets way to crowded and people jockeying to take pictures....
A small bridge with some history which probably I cannot appreciate. But nice walk around the town with Ancient Town.

Dusk is perfect time to visit after doing a bit of shopping down the touristy street, although it gets very busy with people offering boat trips and candles. Plenty of restaurants nearby to choose from.

" ancient bridge in ancient town" This is obviously a very old and ancient bridge, that has become a tourist attraction.. Stone and wood blend so well to show its magical charm. Not very big in size but it does leave a large impression on you as you first see it, and the take the minute to stroll over it.. It takes you from one part of the town to another.. Unfortunately, where there are these charming structures, there are the usual tourist trap type scenarios selling their wares next too it.. Worth a look but try going early the morning, say around 6am, when the streets are empty, the birds are singing and you have that very quiet moment to yourself.. Trust me, try it at that time and have look later in the day, the difference is so huge.

It's an old Japanese bridge. You may find one like it in Japan. During the day they will try to charge you a troll for crossing east to west, but if you walk all the way around you may cross from west to east for free. You may also cross the bridge at night for free with no trolls. Mildly interesting to see, but also an attempted tourist trap.
Very busy and touristy! Visit in the morning rather than afternoon. Not only is it cooler but you will miss the coach loads of tourists that arrive in Hoi An in the afternoon.
Filled with people but still a significant piece of architecture. Plenty to do and walk around the adjacent areas

Nice little bridge, but too busy This is a nice bridge to visit if you are already in old town. It is very pretty. The area is so full of sightseers and we visited in low season. It was difficult to cross the bridge let alone take a pic.

Best photographed from a small pedestrian bridge a few meters away. It gets really crowded in the afternoon when tour buses come in droves. The streets on both sides of the bridge are really pretty though. With lots of small art/coffee/clothes shops and museums/temples.

Covered Bridge - A Bridge Too Far Too Expensive Supposedly a popular tourist attraction...a little piece of history for the area... We would agree...a very little piece, for not such a little price... Artist's selling work on the bridge as you walk by...bridge is probably less than 100' long. Here is what we suggest...Google this place...read the history...save the money you would have paid on tickets, and try some spring-rolls from a vendor along the river... Not a bad place to look at, nothing to spend money on...
I came here in 1994 when Hoi An was a small, lazy town. Saturday night here was like Times Square, you had to buy tickets to etter the bridge. I am happy I came in 1994.

The bridge looks very pretty from the outside, to cross it one of the old town tickets is sometimes required. You can buy the ticket for 120.000 VND and choose 5 of the sights you want to visit. To give one for the bridge is not worthwhile in my opinion! It is better to stay at the road and look at the bridge from the outside!
You are better to view the bridge from the water edge as too many people try to cram on the bridge. Try also early morning before the tour groups start.
It's a bridge that you need to cross. I didn't pay to see the extra rooms, not really worth it in my opinion.

This is a nice bridge but I don't think is a must, although is quite popular. You will be requested the city ticket (maybe) but if you are crossing to the other side you just need to tell them where you are going and that should be enough.
It’s just a small bridge that you can view from outside. go early to avoid the crowd . I was there at 5.30am and had the bridge all to myself .
It’s so busy that you can’t really appreciate it The street beyond the bridge is more pleasant and enjoyable

A little disappointed- overcrowded with people, selfie sticks everywhere. Shouted at to buy a ticket. If you walk around the little back street you get to the other side and can enter without a ticket.
Worth a very short visit Not too much to see here - a nice bridge overwhelmed by people, a small side room exhibit, a bit of river to look at. Very popular but not much substance.
Well I guess you have to visit the bridge if you’re in Hoi An ancient town. Nothing great though, extremely packed with tourist and locals. Had to pay entrance fee but we didnt went inside.

To walk across the bridge you have to buy a ticket, which does allow you to visit several other places of interest in Hoi An however on the other side of the bridge, although a lot quieter were no different than the rest of Hoi An town. So I would advise unless you have too, take your photos from the Hoi An side and save your money.

Worth veiwing but not worth paying to enter. Regarded as one of Hoi An's most important attractions, the Japanese covered bridge was built in the 18th century so that the Japanese living in the city, at that time, could reach the Chinese quarter across the water which it spans. You can clearly see it is of Japanese origin by the sculptures that adorn it as well as how colourfully it is painted. To enter the bridge there is a fee or you can just view from the walkway which runs alongside the Hoai River. This is a ‘quick see’ attraction and worth viewing.

Interesting and attractive part of old town With its history and unique architecture, it certainly adds to old town's charms. It's main attraction is it's photogenic nature and as such, it is a very recognisable part of Hoi An's old town. As a destination it is ok, but not memorable.

Unless you're in the city to stay don't push the boat out to see this bridge. Whilst it's old the number of tourists trying to walk over it others taking photos make it seem ridiculous as a Photoshop rather than a national treasure..You have to decide but for me it's a bridge too far.

A very medieval trip through Vietnamese history I found this bridge whilst wandering around Hoi An. The ancient city is so pretty, I didn’t plan my journey and felt in no rush to explore. As soon as I stepped onto the bridge, I was asked for my ticket. I redirected out and shown where to get one. You need to purchase a ticket which entitles you to enter 5 historic places, the bridge being one of them. The bridge itself, lends itself to beautiful pictures. Once inside, it quite dark, and full of pictoral history of the bridge. It is not a day trip I. It’s own right, but good as part of your day around Hoi An.
Sorry, but the bridge is totally overated... What to say - its a bridge, with 100 people or more on it at the same time. Im sorry, but for me a not must see in Hoi An...
It is a cute wooden bridge however best viewed from a distance up close you are trampled by Chinese tourists... there is a charge to cross it.
This bridge is part of what Hoi An is so you must visit it. It’s always busy. But worth a look when your lost in the streets of old town

We walked past this bridge on our first night in Hoi An. It is amazing and looks incredible. Not sure of how old it is but looks ancient. I took a couple of pictures and it reminded me of a trip to Venice. Worth a look and a few pics. We didn’t walk over it as was very busy. Probably better trying to go over in the day time.

Pretty but always too busy Lovely from the outside. Crowded most of the time but a nice photo opportunity. Buy an entrance ticket and use one of the sections to go onto the bridge itself. But not necessary as the outside view is my preference.

One of Hoi An's attractions This was a very busy area and it was quite difficult to actually get on to the bridge. Many people were trying to take photographs. The bridge itself is arch shaped and the wood is painted red and is known as Hoi An's emblem. It was known to exist in the mid 16th Century and as such, has been reconstructed on several occasions to the same design. Personally I myself was disapponted with the visit.
