The covered bridge sounded amazing, but we were disappointed by the reality. Sure, it’s an old bridge and has been well maintained, and worth seeing on your way through town, but not worth going out of your way for.
Walked over the bridge during the evening. Though it was a lovely bridge, there was not much to see. I wouldn’t bother with a tour, just go and see it yourself!

This is a short wooden bridge which you need a ticket to visit. Expected more to see of this bridge but was quite disappointed as not much to see on the inside. Nicer to take a picture of this bridge from the outside. You can walk across the bridge to the Old house of Phung Hung.

To be honest i was a bit disappointed with this bridge, having read so much about it i was expecting more, it was very crowded and I thought quite dirty. The water under it was dirty and had rubbish floating in it. It looked liked it need a good facelift. Lots of people around selling cheap touristy items. It was possible to wait for the crowds to dissipate to get a photo .
A wooden bridge albeit old, flood marks are there, but the history behind the bridge is the important part
My wife wanted to see this, so we battled through the weekend tourist crowds. Didn't bother to walk over it, busier than the Rialto Bridge in Venice, small and tatty looking, needs a good makeover!
It’s a covered bridge! Great place to use as a landmark. Beware that the attendant doesn’t take a ticket from you to cross the bridge.

Creaky crowded bridge Old bridge in the old city of Hoi An. One of the sites as part of the multiple site entrance ticket. More stuff for sale and that gets to be old. Nothing particularly memorable about it beyond the fact that it’s old.
It's a bridge and it's packed with people. I'm sure that if you are heavily into history or architecture it may have more significance but it's a bridge.

Most of the times you can just walk here but sometimes there's people who will ask you to pay. Officially it's to preserve the bridge and to stop it from being used by too many people but as there are literally thousands of people here every day that sounds quite unlikely...
Pretty bridge with interesting history to it, but oh my god it can get crazy busy, it’s only a small bridge and fills up very quickly with your groups, if you can get in do try!
It's a nice bridge with old interior and nice shrines/statues. If you go after 6pm you won't be controlled and there are far less visitors

Beautiful from outside; ok inside A very beautiful covered bridge when viewed from nearby streets. There’s a charge to go inside, and it’s nice, but it’s not worth the charge. The view from a short distance is the winner.

This old bridge is a beautiful example of the carving and colours, unfortunately it is rammed with tourists so to get a decent picture is hard, the river running underneath is filthy, full of rubbish and detritus, which unfortunately spoils it some what. It can be a bit confusing as to weather you have to pay to access the bridge but you don’t, only if you are going to go into the temples.

Well preserve historical site and impressive Right near the canal in a very busy section of Hoi An, this jewel has to be seen. It is well preserved and there is a little temple in the middle. I would recommend to go very early in the morning because it gets very crowded during the day.

The place is historical but sad to say its too overcrowded to enjoy the view. Cant take a good photo without any tourist on the background. Also the river looks dirty with lots of tourist's trash. No need to pay for ticket to pass the bridge but if u go to shrines, then u need to avail for the ticket

You probably have to see this as it is THE iconic site of Hoi An but it is absolutely rammed with tourists on their own and on guided tours. It's kind of interesting, especially the old photos in the side room, and the ceramic 'air bricks' and roof ornaments are lovely.

Pretty bridge but also be warned for charges! If you enter the old town via this bridge then there is a little ticket desk midway and they will charge. However, if you come into the old town from the other side, you can enter for free. The bridge itself is very pretty, especially when the lantern festival is on.
This is billed as a 'must see' attraction, but to be honest I found it over rated; yes the architecture is lovely but the actual visitors part is small and dissapointing

The ancient bridge with a roof looks like a temple. It was built by Japanese in the old days. It is said that statues of two dogs and two monkeys at the ends of bridge show the years of the beginning and finishing of the work. The other interesting thing of the bridge that you can take a rest after long walk or drop in it to avoid raining or hot weather.
Had to pay nearly 10 aud to cross. Better off just taking a photo. I was pretty cool to look at. But for 2 of us seemed a but excy
It is an ancient bridge but it is very crowded and space/bridge is very small. It is worthwhile seeing at night versus day time.
Beautiful to see at night with all the lights . Very disappointing during the day. There is a charge to walk across during the day.
It was nice,not worth the entrance fee,better of saving your money and taking pictures from the outside..
The japanese bridge is beautiful and well maintained, and nearly impossible to miss as it is in the middle of the ancient town.

I am not sure why are they charging tourists to enter this bridge because honestly when I went inside I was really disappointed. There was nothing inside and it was a simple bridge. However it looks beautiful in the evening from outside. I wouldn't want to pay for something like this when I cooukd enjoy its beautiful sight from outside for free.

Underwhelmed but worth a peek I recommend you walk through the bridge and take a picture because it is simply a part of the Hoi An culture. With all the hype I had expected more: maybe more colorful and bigger. However we are here during the New Year so it has tough competition with all the flowers and lanterns and festivities taking place.

Worth going to see but not paying to cross. This is a pretty bridge and a very popular spot for selfies etc. You can view it from a bridge near the lake or from either end, but you have to pay to cross inside it. It’s an attraction at night too when you may see people posing in boats etc with the bridge as a backdrop.
Worth a photo - and that’s it Nice thing to take a picture of, not a lot to see inside though. Make sure you catch it after dark. Pretty much everything, including the bridge, is prettier then :)
Nothing special, just a common history bridge. Got some interesting Japanese lucky dogs near the entrance.A bit lack of maintenance, the river is too polluted... Smelly.

bridge over the canal. its a nice bridge over the canal connecting the river within the old town. to be honest, i did not find anything much special with it. expect many tourists there specially from china.
The bridge is a monument symbolising the Japanese influence. It is very busy there and although nice to see and here about the history it was a little disappointing
If you are in Hoy An you'll see it. And that's fine. But it isn't anything overwhelming. Just take a look and move on
Japanese Covered Bridge Part of the historic old city of Hoi An. A busy area especially in the evenings . Lively atmosphere.Part of the organised tour arranged with the tour operator
Worth a look if you are visiting the Ancient Town of Hoi An. Looks very pretty at night. It's over 400 years old!

Something to see but just as good view from the side Lots of tourists flood on to this bridge because it is very famous in Vietnam (on one of the notes). Lots of people trying to get the perfect selfie. Just walk past, you get just as good a view
It's an ancient wooden Chinese Bridge first constructed in 1590s. Photo opportunity. Free to see and cross. Emblematic of Hoi An.
It’s nothing spectacular, but it’s interesting architecture, from many centuries ago, but not worth all the hype, but still worth having a look
Great to see and understand the history of such an important Icon for the Vietnemese people. The obvious skill of the workers has stood the testing from the elements and millions of tourists
Lovely to see from the outside Its really lovely to see from the outside. if you buy a ticket to cross it though, and it isnt much of a sight to see from the inside.

NOT what it is made out to be,small old wooden bridge that smells bad and doesn't really look like photos that you see online. In saying that still good to see bit of history ,the lld town around the bridge is main tourist spot in hoi an

Covered Bridge is covered with tourists!! Quite an old and beautiful bridge but because of that it attracts a lot of tourists queueing up to get their photo taken. Interesting to walk over (pay at one end of the bridge). Go inside and have a look at the room there. Makes a nice photo background. Go early in the morning, less people around.
Very neat but crowded This was a great landmark to visit but every time we tried to go in it was absolutely packed with people. Hopefully it’s a little less busy other times of the year.

not much to see.. old bridge with temple We went to the Japanese covered bridge in the middle of the Hoi An ancient town as part of the tour. According to our tour guide, the bridge was built in the 16th or 17th century by the Japanese but was taken over by the Chinese after the Japanese closed off their borders. There is a temple inside the bridge and there are dog statues on one side and monkey statues on the other... apparently because it took 2 years to build. You can spend a couple of minutes here but otherwise, it is quite ho hum.

Honestly, I don't get what the fuss is about. The Japanese shrine is kinda neat, but it's a 15 meter bridge, just kinda cool. DON'T use a ticket here to cross it. You don't need one. If they're being dicks about it, use the normal bridge outside. Also try to visit this early in the day if you don't want to share it with a thousand Chinese tourists
Not the only way to cross in this part of town, good photo opportunity and views to the river and harbour area beyond.
It’s so busy in Hoi An right now you can’t even get through the bridge without a bus load of tourists coming the other way
Great history but a tiny bridge. You need tickets to go thru and the shops across. Lacking in write ups to explain the history.
Okay bridge but not much to it Nice for a picture and about 3 minutes to sightsee, but really nothing particularly special about it.

Small witness of great era This place is a tiny but beautiful reminder of an colorful ancient history of Hoi An. It is a part of five-points ticket that you buy for 120.000 Dongs and you visit the bridge and a small shrine inside. In the night the bridge is opened for free and maybe thats enough for the feeling.
Go in the morning when it's lesser crowd! It's overly crowded after say 3pm to night where the tours start to drop by. So why not just stay in hoi an for a night and walk around early morning ❤️

Maybe I have seen too many wonderful bridges to judge this one fairly but frankly I thought it was an overrated let down. When our guide indicated that this was the famous Japanese bridge I could not believe it. It is small, ordinary and lacking in either imagination or architectural technique. The fact that the original was only restored in 1950 was more disturbing. Did not even waste a digital photo!

Local attraction but very average Nice history, but it’s just a small bridge - albeit a very, very old one - a very average tourist attraction- don’t pay money to walk over it, it’s not worth it. However, when it rains and it rains a lot in the wet season you can walk through it for free.

Nice history, but small, so not much to see Honestly there is not much more to say besides the title. If you use one of the sightseeing tickets to cross the bridge, you get access to some historical facts about it and you get to take some (<5) nice-ish photos. May be more relevant for some though, as we saw a ?Japanese? model-like girl being photographed on the bridge.

Personally I just merely went to view the architecture and appreciate its construction. Not sure I would bother paying to cross it to be honest, there are other sites in Hoi An worth spending your ticket on. A good guide book gives you enough of it's history.
Way too crowded with all the tourists. Even difficult to take a photo when groups of tourists got dropped off there.
it's a nice wooden bridge ... and if you are around, you should of course not niss it. We were there around 20h (Sat, December) and there were still a lot of tourists.
Interesting as part of tour Interesting as part of a tour but not sure I would want to pay to cross it alone! Important historical landmark nonetheless
Japanese Pagoda Bridge In my opinion really not worth making a special effort to visit. Did not offer anything different. However, it is in the area of the Ancient Town. We spent 5 minutes here.

I had heard of this and I respect it has a place and that matters. But as a central highlight for your journey, no .. not convinced. It is nice to see but you could just as easily miss it and not be concerned. I felt no compulsion to appreciate it in detail. It really is not as significant as some say, but hey if you are in the town it would be wrong not to bother.
One of the key attractions in the Hoi An ancient town. But it was so crowded that we couldn't see it.

Old & steeped in history A very old bridge with what appears to be, worryingly, original floorboards. You have to pay to get across & there’s little to see from the view. There are historical images of the bridge in the past.

The bridge has a history dividing the old town between Chinese and Japanese sections. It is nice but really it's just one small part of Hoi An. Stand but the river and look around and you'll understand what I'm talking about. Stand in the same spot and look around at night and it will take your breath away.

Better if you have read history If you’re with a guide, or have read about the bridge, it’s a nice site to see. It’s VERY crowded with selfie sticks and girls posing for photo after endless photo. But get past that and take in the wood and stone used to make the bridge. There’s also a very narrow and quaint alley on one of the sides that leads to a fun store selling old propaganda posters

Although as others have commented it is a tourist attraction with many crowded around, there are times when it is less busy and in the evening it is nicely lit up and quite photogenic with fewer crowds. A note as well -- there is a small fee to "access" the old town that includes a brochure that then covers the cost of admission to cross the bridge. The money goes to restoring the town so worth purchasing.
Historic but nothing great It is historically famous and you have to pay a fee to actually step on the bridge. Not worth the price. Simply take pictures from outside.
Pretty bridge but covered with tourists The bridge is gorgeous but it’s difficult to take a good picture with that many tourists around. Come early to beat the crowds.

The bridge is quaint and interesting, but it is a quick visit and walk through. The best part is across the bridge—a small, quiet street with some of the cutest shops in a Hoi An and no motor bikes! Go to the bridge if only to cross the street to shop.
If you are already in the area then go and visit... but not worth the effort just for this activity. Old historic area is great walking streets so you nay bump into if you're exploring this area

Yep it's an old bridge Yep an old bridge that's still there, adds to the already stunning charm of HoiAn. The bridge is small, narrow, good photo opportunity for 10 seconds but then move on to the more important markets, food, and charm of walking through this area.
There is not a lot to see here. It is one of the spots you can use your entrance ticket for. Small temple in middle of bridge.

It’s a bridge... get over it! I walked through the bridge without realizing I was on the bridge as I was coming from the beginning of Ancient Town. Only once I was outside did I realize where I was because there were hordes of tourists taking photos. I did expect more because my homestyle recommended it. I don’t know much of it’s history so maybe that’s why I couldn’t fully appreciate it. There is a temple inside the bridge but I didn’t buy a ticket so couldn’t go inside.

To cross that small Japanese Bridge you need a ticket that covers few monuments. You can choose between a full size pavillion, temple or the bridge. Yet crossing the bridge is not worth the money. Especially that you can see inside from both sides as few meters away there is a next free bridge. And from that other bridge or from the bank of canal you would get photos of the Japanese one. Note that I travelled 6 times to Japan and visited many prefectures and yet have not seen any bridge like that in Japan.

Its a.bridge. yes a bridge. But you need to pay for this bridge. Yes! The challenge is to go over the bridge without paying a ticket. Oké step 1 follow the big group of japanese people. Go in the group and shout understandble words. Than cross over. OR option 2. Say hi i want to cross, i need to go to the toilet. Putyour hands to your leggs en run like you mama is behind you with a pan. OR option 3 Just buy the ticket.. why not? Ps. You can walk around the bridge to reach the old city. Hahahahha

A bridge is a bridge is a bridge Perhaps the title is a little harsh as it obviously has significance for the locals and reflects a period in their history that they treasure. But, at the end of the day, it's a covered bridge - well maintained and with some interesting aspects. We had purchased a book of entry tickets that gave us access to a set number of attractions in town and this was one of them. Worth buying the tickets.

This was not in my plan. I read reviews and understand that we need to pay for it but it may not that special. However, we had a bad and unpleasant experience on our first day. Hotel staff told us ticket is not required if we go to restaurants only, unless we are going to certain tourist spots. But a guy stopped us when we entered the Ancient Town for dinner and shouted at us superb duper rudely. My husband doesn't want to make things big so he just bought the ticket. And since we bought tickets, we just went here to take a look. Well, it is just a bridge.
An old bridge over the river. I don't know if it has any particular significance in the history of Hoi An.
OK - you've seen and photographed it from as many aspects as possible walker through it even having to pay if enter from East rather than West - it historic but that's it.
Very nicely built. Pleasure to look at. Extremely busy with tourists. Hard to take a good photo. Go early morning for less busy.
Look don't be fooled when you go on to this bridge its short and at night they sell tickets for you to see sights but really its a scam so just think about what you are paying for before you buy
You have to pay 120 thousand Dong for a ticket to go to a few different places, but don't sell tickets for just the Japanese Covered Bridge. Anyway, there isn't really anything special to see here.

Interesting architecture It was an historical bridge that connects the Japanese and the Chinese part of town, it is also interesting to notice all the traditional Chinese architectural detail that was used in building this bridge. There is a temple adjoining the bridge and it is truly a piece of history that needs to be preserved!

Cross it late after 8pm less people and from 9 to 11 pm nice to see from the outside That was the must attraction I wanted to see, after all the info. I gathered on this town, we bought the absurd old town tkt just for it and, beli e it or not they did not asked for it As I said in the title try to visit is from the outside in the morning, before the day trippers arrive and cross it late, after they are gone; for us was the best way to do it
Nice bridge during sunset It’s a nice bridge seen from the bridge in front of it. Is special coz different than all the others in Hoi an. Worth visiting once.

would have liked to walk across it,but the day we ventured,it was clogged by hundreds of folk wearing tee shirts ,we had to settle with a few pics of the outside,after navigating to the other bridge and walking up a bit,there was up to 15 or more coaches awaiting the hordes of folk that where on the bridge.
Smaller than I thought. Missed the dog statues on one side but noted the monkeys on the other side. Didn't expect to see a big shrine there.
Beautiful area with too many tourists, but happy we have been to see. Would we come back, no. If you are wanting to see , you need to but once is enough

Okay, but nothing special Pretty bridge, but the smells (sewage) of the canal next to the bridge didn't help as well as the endless amount of tourists trying to take a photo of the bridge, but what makes this an average attraction is the ticket control to go on/inside the bridge.
A well-preserved bridge. It has been renovated to look beautiful for photography at night. It leads to more shops and eating areas
This bridge is in the old town of Hoi An tou will need a ticket to cross over. It is nice to look at n take a picture. Synonymous with the Old town

Neat bridge, sometimes need to pay ticket fee to cross Interesting history and unique shape and design. You'll spot many tourists taking photos near the bridge. Overall, it's confusing because you'll need to pay sometimes and other times not.
It seems one direction you have to pay another direction you dont. Thats kinda weird because it seems you can go in there for free to that shopping street.
Packed with people taking selfies and took us 5 minutes and 3 photo bombs before we hit the other side.
The bridge has an interesting history but the smell of the water underneath was overpowering !, that was in the daytime don't know if the tide changes that

Small bridge leading to some shops with throngs of people at night posing for photographs. If your in Hoi An then yes, wander over and have a look but, the Brooklyn Bridge or Da Nangs Dragon Bridge it ain’t.

Nice piece of history, but not really worth one of our five entrance tickets in the Ancient Town. You get the best pictures from the outside anyways, so I’d suggest reading about it rather than waste a ticket going through.
This bridge is a cool activity and I would do it if I was walking around hoi an or near the night markets
Pretty bridge, but very busy This is probably the busiest area of Old Town throughout the daytime. It is very pretty, but not worth much of your time because of how busy it is.
Get there early, you need a ticket to walk across it and there’s not a lot to see to be honest. Worth a visit but it’s heaving with tourists around the afternoon.

Very popular attraction We knew it was an attraction in Hoi An and came across it by accident while doing the usual tourist wander around the streets. If you want to go inside for photos you have to pay, not sure how much, but you can take pics for free from the street with it in the background if you wish, if you’re into that sort of thing.
It was a nice bridge, slightly over-rated though. Crowded during the day. Bad smell from the waters. Manage your expectations.

A nice little iconic bridge, good for photo shots. Don’t see a need to pay a toll to cross it, when there is a free alternative just 10 metres down. Nonetheless, a nice spot to take photographs in the early evening, before it gets overly crowded.
It's in the old city, while you walking you can go there Bit crowded in the night If you need to see in detail, go in the day time or week days
Its a bridge that separates the vietnamese quarter from the french quarter. IN the day its not that great to look at,after all its bridge and you'll probably have to cross it...at a fee.
A nice bridge, not especially big. If someone has been to Japan it may not be worthwhile. Good to fill in 5-10mins of your time.

The better view of the bridge is from the town side. Try to visit before the crowds so you can study the history. It is the historic entrance to the city. Use your imagination to picture it before modern trans port.
Sometimes false expectations make you disappointed at the reality . Well this so much advertised bridge except its historical importance doesn’t seem to have something distinctive.
Great history surrounds the bridge, glad it hasn’t been demolished. You can have a great picture taken standing in front of the bridge.

Something and nothing really Yes it's a pretty bridge, but blink and you'll miss it. Far better is to look at the bridges across the larger river, a stones throw away and the lights along the banks, much more amazing!
A must-see on the bucketlist when in Hoi An, but totally overrun by tourist so a Nice picture is almost impossible.

Highly photogenic spot, short visit The bridge offers great photo shoot, but the inside rooms, that require ticket, are very small and can be mostly observed from the exterior. Nevertheless, it is one of Hoi An landmark so cannot be missed.
Just a historical spot. Needs lots of repairs and restorations. Sometimes they check for tickets to cross.

This old bridge in the town is a good place to meet before going across to the modern shops and market at the bridge near by. Unfortunately the water is a little unclean and smelly which detracts from the old Japanese bridge. it is surrounded by many shops selling similar items and the Old house is nearby
Very old section of Hoi an , great to stroll around and get a feed and beer , just watch the masses of people moving through , good night market also .
Many other better things to see but ok! Better to see the amazing old houses and museum and spend your time at the galleries
It's a nice bridge but heavily overcrowded. Can get a nice experience from viewing it from outside. Does have a nice view of the river near the bridge

Don’t buy a ticket look from outside!! The bridge is very pretty but crowded with tourists. We tried to walk over but you need to buy the ‘old town’ ticket (120k dong) just to walk across. If you have the ticket already, you might as well walk over it. I haven’t brought the ticket as most sites you can just see from the outside so why bother?

Not for the claustrophobic Ogden Nash wrote “I think that I shall never see a billboard lovely as a tree. Perhaps, unless the billboards fall, I'll never see a tree at all”. That is very relevant to the Japanese Covered Bridge, The “billboards” in this case being the slow/non moving people stopping or stopped taking selfies and denying others the right to walk through or appreciate the beauty of the architecture. Try a different route in to Old Town for the sake of your sanity.

Worth having a look but often very busy! The Japanese Covered bridge is in the heart of Hoi An so you will probably see it before you actually plan to go to it. There are often people trying to sell you tickets at the entrance but you can just go across it. There are pockets of time in the afternoon when the bus loads of people from the day leave and before the bus loads from the evening come in when it is almost deserted and you can take some quiet photos.
It was a let - down unfortunately. It's a small bridge over a tributary of the main river. Off the beaten path a bit, not worth going out of your way to find...

A literal speed bump into old town This bridge may be old but it's really underwhelming. It's tiny and you can walk across in 10 seconds. The only significance is that it's often a pay point to get into old town. They are sometimes there and sometimes not. When they are collecting there are all sorts of other free entryways into the old town.
Part of the old city. Some artifacts inside as it used to be a home of some kind. A small fee to enter/cross, which you need to do to continue walking along the main street.
There is a fee to pay to walk across this short bridge. We think it was worth the money. Instaed we took a boat ride.
Went there because I was really excited about historical places but found that the bridge was not so amazing. It was managed poorly and I think that the bridge could be broken in a couple of years.

Tour guide very influential I have visited this bridge on 2 separate occasions 2 years apart. Very different experiences and your guide makes or breaks this. Previous guide Hewy with Intrepid tours was brilliant- interesting and informative. This week guide with Hoi an Express was just going through the motions- didn’t care. Bride was very busy with Koreans and Chinese tourists - guide could not get out of there quick enough.

Historical significant This bridge had historical significance to the locals. However, the residences after the bridge are now filled with souvenir shops. The architecture of the bridge is lovely but yet not much excitement. It located nearby Ancient City of Hoi An. Souvenir dealers are aplenty with hiked up prices. You need to bargain well. Busy area during the night and too many Koreans around.
The bridge is very pretty, and can get very busy during the day. For the best photo get up early before the crowds
Okay but nothing soecial Visited as part of city tour. Was okay but not outstanding. If you pass it visit it but don’t make a special journey.
We didn't cross the bridge as there was a fee to do so. You are able to get close enough to take photos so no real need to go on the bridge.

Makes for a nice picture in your photo album but is a bit over rated. The bridge makes for a nice picture in your photo album but is a bit over rated. It’s not that well lit up at night that it takes a good night shot so visit it early in the day when the crowds are down and move on
The Japanese bridge is nothing special. Just looks a bit old. Definitely worth a look whilst strolling thru Hoi An old town, but it’s nothing too exciting.

This place is way over-rated! Too crowded with tourists. There’s no crowd control. There’s a small exhibit inside the bridge, you’ll need to pay for a ticket to enter which I don’t recommend as there’s nothing much to see. The river beneath the bridge is smelly and has a strong sewage smell which turns us off. It would be much better if the river is clean.
Provides a nice photo opportunity from outside the bridge. Not much inside. Process access to the other side of the street for more shops (same items you can find everywhere else)

Gorgeous but shame you now have to pay A must see but a shame you are now required pay to walk 10meters across. Last year when we visited it was free. Another example of HoiAn making the tourist pay...I noted locals were not charged.

This is one of the oldest bridges in the town, and is a throw back to when the Japanese were here. It’s well preserved but there’s not a great deal to it although it is good for selfies. In summary, it’s certainly worth a visit but don’t go out of your way to see it.
The Japanese bridge should be seen and marvelled at. However you would think they would clean up the canal that runs under it. Not nice.
People make pictures look nice when in reality it's pretty gross. Dead fish etc in small amount of water below to smells awful. Too many people who literally push and shove.

Japanese covered bridge We went to have a look however we did not due to the fact that you had to have a ticket to look at a very small bridge which was very crowded with tourists. The outside seemed well preserved. It is nicknamed the red bridge but is actually pink.

기대한것 보다 작았지만 나름 운치 있었어요. 사람이 너무 많고 개천에서 냄새가 나는 것만 빼면 괜찮았어요. 특히 야경이 예쁘지만, 사람이 너무 많네요 It is smaaler than my expectation, but it is classical and romantic. However it was very smelly and crowded around there. It has nice view at night.

Hoi An ancient town is more beautiful in the evening so as Japanese Bridge. The bridge itself is not extraordinary but certainly it has historical value. Nice picture taking with japanese bridge at the background. Walk over the bridge.
As expected, many tourists swarming around the bridge, mostly to take pictures. So we took some pictures from a far, just for memories of Hoi An.
If you are in Hoi An, then you can use your time a lot more wiser than going to Japanese bridge. It is very good picture opportunity.

I’d heard a lot about this bridge prior to seeing it so it was definitely on my to-do list. The bridge was impressive to look at from a distance and the historical significance made it worth stopping to see and also crossing it obviously. Problem for me was it was too crowded when I went as it was that time of day when large tour groups were moving about so it took away from the overall experience but if you are in this city it is still a must see :)

Nothing too fanciful here but a lot of history. Looks really old and dilapidated by day. A different look by night. The green light looks eerie for my wife. There were young couples taking photos on a boat under the bridge.

We spent 4 days in Hoi An and already, by the second day, we felt that we saw almost all the attractions. The Japanese Covered Bridge is one of them. You can't miss it. It's close to the Night Market, Central Market, and is located inside Hoi An Ancient Town so you're bound to pass it at least once or twice (or 28 times in our case). It's just a bridge as far as most travelers are concerned and provide little more than a photo-op.

A bridge is a bridge... It's a pretty bridge but, fact is, it's just a bridge. A very crowded one since it connects the Ancient City to the Night Market with a zillion lanterns. You can stop on the bridge and get good photos of the river below with tourists and locals in boats.

As it says on the tin This is an old Japanese covered bridge. As it's old it's made of wood. As it's covered it has a roof. Otherwise it's totally unspectacular other than being there and in good condition....

Over publicized and crowded First the good. It looks cool. Now the bad, once you cross the bridge on your way to An Hoi island there will be a toll booth; at least if you are not Asian. We were asked for tickets to cross the footbridge from the end of the Japanese bridge going over to An Hoi. Of course the “official” collecting the toll with her “official” badge didn’t stop locals or Asians ... only me. I was astounded. You have to walk roughly a mile out of your way to the two side “free” bridges to get back to the walk bridge.
Talking so much about this bridge-we went to see it. a little dissapointing - encient, special and nice to see...
I had great expectations to visit the bridge. Although the structure is special, the pollution of the river or stream is appauling

Beautiful ancient bridge The bridge itself is nice and full of history, we like its architecture. The problem is you need a ticket just to take a look inside and they push you to buy one all the times. Kind of disappointed!

A Little History Helps but Bridge Itself Average We went there in the evening so we really could not see all the architectural details. The history helps as backdrop. It's in the Ancient Town & should be one of the stops.
Old bridge with a little shrine built inside. To walk into the bridge you will need to pay. I guessed the money helps preserve the bridge. Very old bride with 4 animal statues and a shrine

It's nice but a bit too crowded to enjoy or get nice photos. It's lit up quite nicely at night. It's a bit silly to have a ticket required as you need to cross it to get to the other side of the river so we ended up ignoring the ticket man a few times as we just wanted to go over the bridge. The people that set up stands at the entrance to the bridge are super inconsiderate for anyone wanting a nice photo unfortunately. You'll need Photoshop if you want a good photo. The temple inside isn't too exciting.

very old and a must see We did this as part of our city tour. It is very old...a great photo opportunity. Lots of things to see but do not miss out on the water level marks (when the tour flooded--which it does often).

There is a VND120k ticket which allows you to visit 5 sites/museums in the Old Town. We didn’t get the ticket and I think crossing the bridge is considered one attraction. Fortunately, we had walked down the parallel street along the river and crossed the bridge from the side leading towards the main town area. No ticket checks on this end! The bridge houses a temple. Don’t know why it’s called the Japanese Bridge, when it is clearly Chinese.

Crowded but interesting It was a National Holiday Long weekend plus full moon so the Ancient Town in Hoi An was especially crowded. We purchased our entry tickets for 120k dong each and proceeded to wend our way to this famous bridge. Too crowded by night and the same the next day. Don’t be worried your tickets last the duration of your stay. We will be back tomorrow to pass through.
It’s crowded. Tonnes of street hawkers and always so much rubbish around its hardly worth it for the view. Plenty of other more beautiful places in Hoi An.

If you’re in Hoi An city and walking around you should go and see the bridge. It’s beautiful, but very small and the whole experience takes around 5 minutes. It’s a little temple on the bridge which as well.

A little disappointed Given the bridge expects an entrance fee and is really busy - we expected a better up keep. We actually enjoyed the experience more walking back from dinner with far less people and we didn't pay the charge.
Interesting but not a must see It is part of the walk through 5h3 old town, which you pay to do. It is very old and is interesting, but to me it’s best use is at a landmark to meet someone.

If you are already in this area, it is definitely worth to visit. The antient town and the bridge especially is very busy and it is almost impossible to take picture without several people “photobombing” at once:)You need to pay for the entry 120k VND, but it includes another 4 entries to choosen places from the town.

Pretty and photogenic Lovely little covered and very old bridge in Hoi An, you can’t miss it when you visit and should stop for a photo. Hoi An is a very beautiful old town and a must do on your Vietnam trip.
A nice experience during seeing the amazing old city of Hoi An,listening about the myth and the reality of this bridge
Since this bridge was on our route from our hotel to ancient towne we decided to see it since it was part of our tour ticket price. It was a good example of the architecture for that period
Was there on a tour. I realize it is pictured on a currency bill but it think it’s over rated. Small. Nothing fantastic.

The bridge itself has a nice design even though it cannot compete with the original Japanese bridges. The view of the river is limited, however the main problem is the endless photos that are taken by tourists on the bridge. Sometimes they do not let you pass the doors at beginning and end of the bridge to look good. So it is only a passage between two sides of the old town, not a sightseeing to enjoy. And it is relatively small. Not longer than 10 meters.
An interesting small and very old bridge in the old town of Hoi An. Watch out for the people asking for payment for ‘tickets’, this is to visit a tiny museum on the bridge.
This was kind of silly. You must buy a ticket to step onto this very small bridge. While it is beautiful, the tourist trap hunger will eat you alive!
Must see but only once. Must see once, I wouldn’t pay to enter the temple part. Packed with tourists always. The story behind it is interesting

We tried to go over the bridge not realising you needed a ticket. Not being able to find the ticket office we walked down to the river and used the pedestrian bridge and looked back at the Japanese covered bridge. It was extremely crowded with tourists, so our view from outside was OK
Wooden bridge, 12m long... Nothing special and you have to pay if you want to cross it. See it from outside, or from the entrance, it’s enough.
You don’t need to have to queue up because there is nothing special inside of this small temple. Just see from outside to avoid the crowd and enjoy the beauty of the temple.

Was hoping for something quite spectacular after all i had read so was a little bit underwhelmed. You can walk across for free but guards hassle you for a museum ticket as there is a tiny chapel that you have to pay for...just say walking across.

was there on a sat night. too crowded to get in. have to buy a ticket to enter which i didnt. after the crowds lessen late in the night i went in eithout ticket. there's a temple on the bridge which is closed by then. n some statues on another side. not quite worth it to buy the ticket really. if u stay in hoi an town near enough, I'll suggest that u go early morning sunrise..before the ticketing starts. then u can actually get good pics without the crowd.all to yourself.
Not too much to see. We didn’t bother buying tickets to go on it. You can get close outside. At times it can be very busy with tourists.

It’s great to take a picture of from the outside but don’t pay for a ticket to cross the bridge if you just want a picture from the inside, it’s probably not worth it (unless you’re planning on using the ticket for the other sites too). When we went about 1pm it was pretty quiet and could get a picture without dodging loads of tourists. We didn’t cross at this point as we didn’t have a ticket yet. We went later in the day (5pm ish) - after purchasing tickets - to take a picture inside and it was overcrowded and not worth it. If you do want to cross, I’d recommend going earlier in the day.
blink and you'll miss it it's okay, take a photo and move on one street takes you right to it and you have to cross to continue we crossed many times at night and were not asked for a ticket

There isnt a lot that you can say really, the history of the bridge makes it pretty cool & the novelty of the bridge when compared to the rest of Hoi An is nice but it does kind of hide away making you go looking for it. Once you do find it, its nice but not something that I would say is a must personally
Very crowded at night It is amazing to see based on its age but it is very busy with tourists. You need to come at 6 or 7am to get a pic without tourists in it.

This bridge is tiny. You can walk across for free. One end has two monkey statues and the other have two dogs (to signify the lunar year it was started and completed). To go further inside you have to pay. We didn’t as it was very crowded. It’s not exactly picturesque, but it’s historical.

It’s quite pretty, it’s a bridge in an architecturally different style to the rest of the city, but beyond the novelty of that and a good photo op, it’s not going to blow your mind. But if you are in the neighbourhood, which we were, it’s worth a look, particularly at night. Just don’t go at low tide.

Its a nice small covered stone bridge with lots of tourists everytime. If you want to enter to the bridge you have to pay fee. (I dont know why, whats the point of this) Negative: awful smell, because belowe the bridge the water is black, full of rubbish : (

An old bridge in a beautiful ancient town This is a very old bridge, photogenic from the outside not much on the inside. You have to pay to cross it. HOWEVER, it is worth every Dong just to get to the other side. On the town side is tourist chaos, one over the bridge it is much calmer and hardly any traffic. There are also 5 must see houses and temples.
Interesting to see but definitely not worth going to far out of your way to see. It is 400 years old. However it is a very short, crowded pedestrian bridge.
Attractive piece of architecture but not necessarily something to go out of your my to see. Obviously just my opinion.
It looks beautiful at night but is packed with people walking across and taking photos on it. Quite annoying, much nicer to see from the boats that go up and down the river
The bridge was an ok attraction but crowded and pretty basic. If you spend any time at all in Hoi An you will need to cross over it.

Japanese covered bridge While the bridge itself was worth a look and a photo we could not hang around as the stench and the rubbish in the water below was horrendous What a pity it isn’t looked after better
It’s nice but not really a ‘site’ in my opinion. Probably won’t recommend to friends. Nice enough but not sure it’s worth the entry fee.

Definitely a must see when visiting the ancient city of Hoi An. It is a small bridge over a small waterway built some 400 years ago by Japanese craftsman and has historical significance. It's a good photo op of your visit here. A ticket is required to cross.
Too many tourists block the flow of crossing the bridge due to photography with Chinese Tourists ‘ extreme posing. With it being hot and humid weather, it is inconsiderate to slow down passing thru.

Pretty but over run & over crowded at times In my opinion and it is only my opinion this is good dead early or late to enjoy fully as I've found it's over run with tourists either trying to find their way over to the other side or file into the small room( temple?) decorated with old fashioned artifacts. It's an architectural attractive wooden bridge Japanese style on all the tourist group itineraries- so just try and dodge us before we get there. Entrance is covered under the Hoi an old town attractions ticket available from small booths at the periphery of the old town. No ticket just admire from the Lakeside.

Very pretty but extremely busy, best to visit here early evening, once the crowds have eased off. We went over this bridge a number of times and never entered the small pagoda, but you could see it from the windows. They charge a small fee to enter. Money is better spent doing the tour of the old town. It includes the bridge and it’s history.
Must have picture at the bridge otherwise you haven’t really been in Hoi An. Worth a look but it won’t take you long. It’s only a small bridge but steeped in history.
We were in Hoi An for tet Vietnam new year and it was very busy you could hardly get to the bridge early evening so I would suggest you visit in the morning if you are there at this time of the year

This is a very pretty old bridge but it only takes a few minutes to see, and overlooks a dirty rubbish strewn stream which is not so pretty. If you want to get to the other side without paying to cross the bridge you can walk along the main river for 100 yards or so and there is an alley on the right which takes you through.

Go early in the morning Our tip is get there before 9.00am if you want to see it at its best and then go again at night to see the awesome Vietnamese lanterns illuminating the Old Town.. You won't be able to see the bridge at night though due to the mass of humanity moving around it.. Jaw dropping!!

Interesting architecture Nothing to rave about. Interesting, yes. Amazing, no. I agree with the other reviewers, OK for a photo but not worth paying for. The little river it crosses is full of rubbish and stinks. To qualify for world heritage status the rubbish, especially all the plastics, needs to be cleaned up.
worth a quick walk-over a nice place to visit but quite short/small. not very much to see but part of the site-seeing tour of Hoi An so worth a quick look.

Ok for a photo but not worth paying to cross On a very hot day, you could smell the history caused by the low tide. Held my breath just to take this photo. Nice to see, the craftsmanship that was in use at that time.
Besides the historical facts, there is nothing special to see inside this covered bridge. It is much better to stand on the bank of the river nearby and to take a look on the old wooden bridge.

A 400 years old unique monument has become the icon of Hoi An. Built by Japanese in the 17th century for traders with the Chinese on the other side of Hoai River flowing south joining the Thu Bon River. It border Tran Phu Street on the eastern side and Nguyen Thi Minh Khai Street on the western side. With an elaborated roofed with wooden carving structure on bricks and is popular known as Japanese Covered Bridge, 18m long. There are two statues at the east and west entrances, one side is a Monkey (Thien Hau) and the other is a Dog (Thien Cau) each with incense urns in front for joss tick prayers. It is said the bridge was built in the year of the Monkey and completed in the year of the Dog and ot is here. In 1653 the Chinese built a small temple on the north-centre of the bridge (Cua Cau Nhat Ban) dedicated to the Emperor of the North (Ba De Tran Vo), a revered wooden statue seating in the main altar of the temple where the locals prays for the peace and prosperity of Hoi An. It was in 1990 the bridge was declared as one of Vietnam “National Historical and Cultural Monuments” and you can see it printed on the 20,000 VND bill. One of this ticket kiosk is at the left end corner of Tran Phu Street beside a café. The ticket for the five visits of Hoi An s’ Historical and Cultural sites is inclusive of this temple on the bridge. Actually ticket only required for the visit to for going into the temple at the bridge, for crossing to the western side of the bridge you do not require a ticket. But the ticketing woman is demanding for the ticket especially to foreigner as you approaching the entrance. The trick, if you want to keep your ticket coupon for other visits, enter the bridge from the western entrance there is no ticketing man on this side, as most of the tourist will enter from the eastern entrance. There is a foot bridge on the left near the river bank which you can just walk across the stream usually use by the locals, turn right at any lane and you will be on the main street of Nguyen Thi Minh Khai again where you can find the most art galleries of Hoi An. Now the Japanese covered bridge is on your right. Here you can visit the Phuong Hoang Old House

Peculiar Art - Poor Maintenance A very intesresting bridge to visit during your passage at Hoi An, but, like a lot of places in Vietnam, it is poorly maintained, the water is beyond dirty, which degrades the bridge itself.
I was expecting a lot more from this than what you get in all honesty. The bridge is small, the river below is filthy and overall it’s just nothing to write home about. Sorry!

Very old and intricate You can cross the bridge at any time when in the city, It is often very crowded, the ancient small living quarters on the bridge can be one of the free entry points on your ticket
No charge, if crossing it in the direction going into the Ancient Town. It is a piece of Đại Việt (Great Viet) history, and its relationship with Japan. Better looking from the outside.
The Japanese Covered Bridge is a quick stop as you tour Hoi An. Good photo opportunity, but it gets very crowded so go early.

Interesting, Historic importance. Crowded. You can spend 15 minutes The bridge seems quaint. Of historical significance. Arterfacts of Japanese origin. As a 'must-do" site, the entire tourist crowd descends on this place, and gets quite crowded in the small space available. Just take a look and get out fast.
Go early before the crowds This bridge is a very quick stop along the Hoi An walking tour. Really not a big deal but worth a picture if you can get there before the hordes arrive by noon.
A lovely bridge but incredibly busy with Chinese tourists taking selfies. If you do not want to be trampled, go early in the morning.
A "must see" in Hoi An, but only for 5 mins ! Interesting bridge over the most polluted river I have ever seen. Of course it is crowded, but please don't miss it.
Lovely to look at but the smell overpowers you and we didn’t visit in the summer. Still a must for pictures
Not something I'd set out to see, but stumbling across it was a nice surprise. It is a nice photo stop, especially at night.
Mobbed with people but interesting and very beautiful - not sure it is worth paying to enter though.

Given the age of the bridge, this is an impressive structure. We stumbled across the bridge as we walked around the old town. We managed to walk across without any charges (although I note that other reviews have suggested a fee has been charged). It’s not an attraction that you would necessarily make a special trip to see but if you do find yourself close by it is well worth the time.

Nice bridge with heritage history, however over rated in my opinion as an attraction. Don’t get me wrong , is nice , I just expected it to be a little more jaw dropping. You have to have a ticket to cross it, so don’t get caught. No ticket walk around.
Just a lit Japanese bridge One can visit it. It doesn’t take ant additional time, looks nice at night. Nothing out of the world. Good for one or two photos.
Obviously very old and historic and interesting but needs a bit of tlc to bring it back to its former glory

It's worth taking a look at this but it was a bit of a disappointment after the star billing it seems to get. There isn't a great deal to see and I preferred the temple that had been added onto the side rather than the bridge itself.

Visit in the evening for a picture outside Hoi An is famous for shopping and walking around old Town. The bridge is nice in the evening, bring your tripod for your photo. Don't think the rest was worth it.

On its own I have to admit I was a little underwhelmed. However you need to look at this attraction as part of the historical package that makes up the old city. If you want a postcard photo you will need to get up real early. I mean real early. At all other times it is a sea of people, selfie sticks and tourists flocking to get the picture of them standing at the arch. You should purchase a ticket to walk across the bridge but virtually nobody seems to hand over their coupon or is asked by the attendants. Shame really when the intent behind the tickets is to put money into the ongoing restoration of the town.

In need of love and repairs... The history of this bridge aspires romantic illusions . However in reality ,wasn't. There is a need to buy a ticket to cross the bridge . Although the fee is nominal it was my earnest hope that the funds collected will eventually go towards the muchly needed restoration and repair of the bridge. Nevertheless, a lovely historical feature.

Whilst very interesting to see the bridge is one part of a much larger picture that makes up Hoi An. If the chance is there for you its worth seeing the bridge both in the light and when lit at night - two amazingly different places.

Interesting- But Really is it Worth All the Hype! We loved walking around the streets of Hoi An's Old Town and enjoyed our visit to the Japanese Covered Bridge, but it really isn't an essential reason to visit this part of Vietnam. Lots of tourists milling about probably wondering (just like me) what's all the fuss about.

Pretty to look at, but overhyped We went at night and you could cross it without a ticket, but went back in the day time, and it's on of those places that are so hyped but in the end...it's a 10 meter covered bridge... that's it. I know the money is going to restore the town?? But really not a special landmark at all

It appears beautful from a distance - so best to view it that way. Why? It's a wondrous small wooden bridge that is overly crowded by tour groups and picture-taking. Overwhelmed by foot and bicycle traffic, one cannot admire construction or absorb historical value in presence, much less enjoy any "view". Takes less than a few minutes to cross over and exit to sadly same touristy atmosphere on other side. Summary: View from a distance is best.
It is just a bridge. Not really a big deal. A lot of tourists so avoid during peak hours. Set within the old town so a nice stroll to the other side of town that is a lot less touristy
Hard to take a photo since there were so many tourists walking around. Interesting and historical bridge.

Nice bridge but no big deal. This is a nice historic bridge. But it really isn't that big a deal or I just don't appreciate old bridges. I took pictures like everyone else but mainly because I didn't feel there was that much to see in town. I think there are more interesting places in Vietnam and even nicer bridges. Check out the bridge in Danang.

Strange little bridge. We stayed in Hoi an for 4 days. We walked through the market may times. We walked through the bridge about a dozen times and 3 of which we were asked for a ticket or had to pay, so we walked around. (About 5m to the other bridge) We didn’t see any signs to pay to enter or walk through, and maybe I am just naive, but this was a strange experience. I understand there is a temple of sorts there, which we didn’t want to enter, but unsure of what we needed a ticket for only a few times. Maybe it was only a certain time of day you had to pay. However I think it needs to be more clear for the many many tourists in Hoi an 🙂 just some honest feedback. It is a lovely little covered bridge though 🙂

If you’ve got spare time A nice bridge but definitely wouldn’t go out of my way to see it again. It was exceptionally crowded due to 2 tours groups scheduled at the same time, but overall an interesting sight with some history.

Can't say this is the best bridge I've ever seen or been on and wouldn't bother using one of your 5 attractions which you get with the Hoi An ticket. We avoided losing a ticket stub by crossing the river a little further up and circling back round to the road which has got lots of lovely shops and restaurants!

You can’t miss this bridge when walking around town! It is ancient and very lovely to see, payment required if you want to visit the information room but this is included if you buy a book of five tickets from the many booths dotted around town. Can be very busy especially after 4.00 pm when lots of visitors arrive for the evening.

Nice to shelter in from the rain.. Pleasant architecture.. reminds me of the Rialto Bridge in Venice because of its arch. Good shelter from the rain, but nothing special & packed with noisy tourists by day & early evening.
Walked through it while in the ancient city area. Pretty cool but also small. If you miss it not a big deal, if you see it in passing take it in and enjoy!

Be sure to get your tickets in advance, because they will be collected before you are able to cross over the bridge. The history is interesting and the structure itself is a piece of art. I don't feel like I really needed to cross over the bridge tbh. Taking photos outside could have easily sufficed.
The bridge was neat to see but as a part of ancient town— not really a separate attraction. I was glad we saw it but wouldn’t go out of my way for it.
Make sure you have the tickets and go early to avoid the masses.suprised that people allowed still to walk on the bridge in spite of its age
During the day, this was pretty crowded and therefore difficult to really see. It looks nice all lit up at night though
Not mandatory but good to see. The bridge is also a easy walk through from one part of the ancient city to another.
It's a pretty bridge teeming with tourists. The fact it is so popular shows how overrated Hoi An really is.
Nothing startling or of great interest. In fact it looks better in the dark. You may be asked to pay a fee, depending on your entry to the Old Town.

Dusk was falling as we arrived at the bridge. It’s a very popular spot for photographs. We decided not to bother going in as the crowds were to large and the magic of the lanterns in the darkness of night was starting to reveal itself. Yes, go take a few photos. Visit if that’s your thing. We didn’t use all our 5 old town entry tickets in the end so maybe it would have been worth entering. Apparently you don’t need to use a ticket to cross it without stopping by the shrine inside.
Decent bridge but wouldn't bother paying for ticket, just sneak down the alley behind and enter from the other side
It totally belongs to "must see". In the reality it is just crowded bridge surrounded by not clean and well smelling water.
Looks good at the entrance to the old town. But but. Makes a world of difference in the nighttime. Very cool
Really polluted water under the bridge It’s one of nice places to visit but it is much better if the people save the water polluted situation here.

It is definitely a nice looking bridge and I understand that it has its significance. However, it is just a small bridge at the end of the day and the money they expect you to pay to walk through is too much.

Part of the history of Hoi An, the Chinese, Japanese and French influences from the trade routes. Adds to the ambiance that is the town. The bridge gets busy as it is an attraction and getting a photo without anyone else is very difficult. There are locals who have business taking group pictures and emailing them one - I’ll leave it to you to work that one out. .

It was pretty, but I wouldn't consider it a must see unless you are into the history behind it. We crossed it at night and didn't have to pay anything, compared to the day when there is a charge. It is right by the night market, and can be seen from across the river, if you don't want to actually walk over it at least you can still see it.

Good photo spot with a toll??? Visited this location to take photos and walk across the bridge. We were met by a lady dressed in business attire and carrying a walkie talkie. She told us we had to buy a ticket first in order to cross the 10 meter bridge. We were very skeptical and blended into the crowd to cross anyways. A man at the apex of the bridge was attempting to check "tickets" but again we just went with the crowd. Still unsure if this was legitimate or a couple of people had just set up a hustle since there didn't seem to be a ticket booth on the opposite side of the bridge.
Historical bridge in Hoi An Just a small bridge but has an important history within the local area. Can get very busy in the afternoons and evenings. There is a cover charge but is worth the visit .
Bridge ,,, could see from outside Lots of people ,,, not neccessary to “ waste “ a ticket can see from outside , get great photos, either way

History around it is interesting, but if you have to pay more to go into the bridge, which I think you do, it is just that you cross the bridge to see more shops, which you have plenty on the other side.
There’s a small temple as you go through the bridge. As a normal temple you could pray there. This image of the bridge is printed on the VND20.000 note.
Ignore the signs and tickets...just walk through...we even push our bikes thru...crowded... best pics from the bridge just across the way.

iconic, ancient but its very small and very crowded! Japanese covered bridge is one of the icon of ancient city of Hoi An. Its very ancient with a very small museum inside (you need to pay for that, there are some old photo and description). the bridge is very small, short and very crowded

It has very ornate carvings which makes it quite impressive, but in the end.. it's a small little bridge, albeit an iconic landmark for Hoi An. Definitely go see it if you're in Hoi An, but don't go there just for this.
While the bridge is interesting it is not worth going out of your way to visit. We walked over it to get to the old town and that was enough.
Stopped here whilst exploring the old town. You can use one of your 5 tickets to visit the little back room on the bridge but I would save your ticket and just walk over for free.

Not interesting really It is as interesting as any other bridge. It is just another thing you take a photo of when walking in the ancient town, if you want to walk over it you need to pay, there is another free bridge a little further downstream.
Pretty bridge, but nothing special Aside from the historical aspects, it's just a bridge. There's a charge to walk across it (we didn't).

Thought this was over publicised. Yes this is very old and is unique but there isn't much to it. I guess you have to see it when visiting Hoian. Wedding couples having their photo in front of it had more appeal than the bridge.

Not worth buying a ticket to walk over this bridge. The Lonely Planet says you can cross for free if you say you are not visiting the small temple on the bridge but no way would they let us over. Good for pictures and there is a free wooden bridge nearby.

It’s ok. Don’t travel for it but it’s nice We were staying in the old town and bought the $4 ticket to enter a few historical sites and this was one of them. They didn’t actually take our ticket but if they had I would probably have felt that it was one of those moments where you shouldn’t really need a ticket for it - it’ll take you 5 minutes to view and is essentially public space. But I did quite like it and so if you’re in the area take a look. It’s cheap and it’s a bit of history and it won’t consume lots of time that you could be doing something else. Nothing to lose by going
The bridge was pretty and all, but it was hard to get a photo without hundreds of people in it. I would go by here when I visited the old town but not expect something extraordinary.

Historic Covered Bridge This historical covered bridge right in the heart of Hoi An old town is a short bridge that is being over touted as a tourist destination. Quite pretty but nothing much to shout about. Just a bridge to take photos for memories.

Overrated... Just an old bridge... Too many tourists... Well, maybe I am spoiled because I come from Holland and have seen a lot of older things (and more beautiful). Crowded place, small bridge, hundreds of Japanese taking photographs... Worth a visit for 10 minutes.
Guide books make this place sound really impressive, but it's not. Quite small, not too much to see. Interesting without being fascinating. Just one of many "attractions" in Hoi An's old town.

Go early if you are staying in Hoi An as the crowds arrive in early afternoon. The bridge is interesting and picturesque and it’s great to walk around ancient town in the early more hours , obscenity the crowds arrive grab a coffee and people watch .
It was a good spot for photography. Very scenic from certain angle. That was all about it I guess.. we were asked to contribute some donation, to buy a ticket before entering..

No doubt a very old wooden bridge, which is one of the group of attractions that you can pay to visit in Hoi An. However you can cross for free st night, although it is a little underwhelming. Quite a simple structure, although there is a small room/temple. Can get crowded and obviously damp and doesn't smell the best. Although this was a week after the recent flooding!
This bridge is part of the Ancient City. Just another bridge to walk upon and over. Good for a first visit.

Something needs to be done about the crowds for such a small bridge, impossible to get a decent photograph or take in its beauty. Ropes along the river side or a maximum entry onto the bridge at a given time would be a couple of ideas so people can actually enjoy the bridge. Shame.

This is an ancient construction of a small bridge, symbolic of the Japanese-Vietnamese friendship. It felt a little unsafe to walk across, its not maintained very well. Surrounded by a smelly little river. I wasn’t too impressed. You have to pay to get across during the day but its free at night. It only takes 30 seconds to cross it.
Great bridge with roof. A piece of art work in Hoi an.You have to pay to enter.A small muzium located inside the covered bridge.Interesting but not worth it.

It's an old covered bridge - what else is there to say! It looks very picturesque & Japanese - great for a photo from the modern footbridge that runs parallel to it. There is a small temple actually on the bridge that you can go into. I wouldn't call this a must see, but if you are passing, which you probably will be given it's location at the start of the old town, it's worth a little look. It won't delay you long.
Lovely old covered bridge, just a pity it gets so busy and crowded with tour groups pushing and shoving.

It’s a well maintained, old and pretty bridge with a small shrine inside. We didn’t need to use a ticket, but we were there in the aftermath of flooding, so perhaps they just weren’t bothering. It’s worth a trip, but every time we saw it, it was swarming with selfie sticks, so time your visit accordingly. Looks stunning lit up in the evening. Not a destination in and of itself, but worth stopping by.

The design of the bridge incorporates the Japanese, Chinese and Vietnamese architecture. Lots of people using the bridge. On one end of the bridge is the hip side with lots of cafe and shops. On the other end the place was definitely quieter and less crowded and the retail goods a lot cheaper.
Nice to walk through but have your entrance ticket ready cause they want it as soon as you get within a foot of the entrance

It’s a pretty bridge but I don’t have much more to say about it. You don’t need a ticket to walk across the bridge but some days it looked like people were asking for them. Very beautiful buildings either side of the bridge though. It looks good at night!
Tick the box to say been there seen it A small bridge worth walking to get the pic . It's towards end of town and can be included on your visit to the ancient city.

The bridge entrance has 2 dogs statues and exit part has 2 monkeys and this indicated the bridge was built in the year of dog and completed in the year of monkey(chinese zodiac), in other words it took 3 years to built this bridge.

I mean, it's a bridge. Maybe we should have gotten a guide or something cos we didnt really get all the hype - it is a pretty bridge, but all the places around it are a lot prettier. They also ask you to pay to cross it, but you can just go down the street towards the river and cross via the restaurants there anyway so I didn't quite get why we had to pay?
Worth a look but you won't end up spending much time looking. Great spot for photos. Make sure you've purchased a entry ticket into the old town and you'll gey 5 free passes for places such as this.

I continue to return to this hotel when visiting Hoi An because the staff is so friendly and helpful. The free breakfast is excellent, and the location is perfect. On this trip they upgraded my room at no extra charge. For the price, I highly recommend it.

Old and interesting, but you've just about seen it all before you go in... The title says it all, really. It's a must see to go past as you cross the bridge, but I wouldn't recommend using one of your tourist attraction tickets. There isn't much more to see inside than the shrine you can see from the outside. Take your pictures from outside and move on.

As part of the towns history it's worth a look I would not come to a Hoi An to see the bridge - but if you're in Hoi An you are going to walk across this bridge one way or another so you might as well appreciate the style and history. As part of the towns history it holds some interest.
Lovely old bridge, Great opportunity for photos on and off the bridge. Come back at night for a different view.

Not Japan but nice addition Very busy with tourists but is a definite must see site in Hoi An .. only takes 5 mins from your life as is so central. Youll need to be quick to get a photo as tourist central. Remember your Hoi An pass as there are checkers on the bridge.

Worth Walking a Few Blocks To See--Dodging Motorbikes The bridge is cute, not unusual, not spectacularly artistic. You'll find it just far enough from your hotel and the other sites to make it an annoying walk--forced into the street by parked motorbikes, and then dodging other motorbikes.

Nice view from the other bridge You can take a nice picture of the bridge from the path or from the other bridge. Having wasted one of our coupons going into the bridge I can say it really isn't worth it. You do need the old town ticket to cross the bridge, but you only need to hand over a coupon if you want to go into the room to the side. This was very unclear and the room was pointless.

Hoi An is a real treasure to enjoy, the walking, the food, the shopping, the way it lights up at night a wonderful experience! The Japanese bridge is quite interesting but I think the fee for walking across the bridge is a bit of a money grab. Walking through that area is great but the tickets booths, 3 that I am aware of, try to extract a fee to use the bridge & make it sound like you must pay to be in the area which can deprive the other businesses of tourist shopping. There is a path beside the bridge that allows access to & from the area that is not indicated anywhere. In our stay of 4 days we wanted to access the area on the other side of the bridge many times over that 4 days, each time they wanted more money. Money grab?

We visited at night time. Probably not the time to visit. It was crowded and this place is probably best visited during the day so you can see everything and photograph it nicely. If you want to see it without a lot of people then I suggest a very early visit! I’m sure it is very nice but we didn’t get to see it at the best time.

He a quick look at night and walked over. An attraction in town, sure enough nice little bridge; but didn't have a lot of meaning to us. It's right in town so you can't miss it; you'll see it whilst your there.

Worth a walk past but didn't blow my mind. We where recently in Hoi An. Loved the town. The bridge is interesting and worth a walk past but we really didn't understand the importance of it. To us it was an old decorated bridge. Nice but only with a 30second stop.

No need to pay to visit Impressive bridge, especially given that it has existed so long, but wouldn't recommend paying / using a ticket just to cross it. Can easily behold it from one side or the other.

Pay a ticket to enter the bridge and find this place is just over hyped. I can understand the sense of history associated with it but don't expect any earth shattering architecture or sense of landing back in time!!! Visit it if your are anyway in the old quarters.

Just Take Pictures, Charge For Crossing The bridge is nice to take pictures of, especially at night. There are a couple people on here who try to force you to buy a ticket to cross (or have the park ticket, which you can pretty much get away without buying). In the evening, if you really want to enter the bridge, you can probably sneak in if no one's around.

Overly crowded & ticket required to cross Unless you arrive very early in the morning, the bridge is always crowed. Also, they charge a ticket to cross the bridge. In 2015, we were in Hoi An & they didn't have such strict guards hounding for tickets. We just walked over. This time, they're watching like hawks. The Japanese bridge in Hue isuch nicer in my opinion & less crowded.
Expected more off that. Walked throught it, people were taking pictures of it but I really wasn’t amazed or something, i was more disappointed.

A sense of history but too crowded You certainly will feel the bridge has a solid history whenever you are walking on it. However there are usually lots of other tourists there at the same time so you might end up taking more pictures of people than the bridge. Also there is quite hot in there so you will not stay too long before finding your way out. Not worthy to take a detour to see it.
well,you can go and see it won't take a long time. to many people its difficult to take a good shot.
Nice enough however it was so hot when we were there and there were so many tourists that it was almost too much effort.

The history or the bridge itself it's cool...But as with most of the other attractions in Hoi An, you will be greeted by throngs of tourists hellbent on having their significant other capture their likeness in front of said attraction, presumably to fulfill the goal of having their photo taken adjacent to every single tourist-attraction that Trip Advisor instructs them to attend. If you find yourself at the bridge, you're better off grabbing a coffee at one of the coffee establishments that surround the bridge. Cocobox Cafe has a cool cat - I recommend the cat.
Nice to see as part of the old town Nice pot to visit while walking would. The old town, not a singular reason to visit but worth seeing. Nice part of the old history much like most of the old,town.

Could be fantastic because the history is but its nonstop over over crowded with mainly chinese people that care for nothing and nobody force them selfs into first place always so that takes the beauty for me away.
What is all the fuss about there as so many old houses and temples in much better condition from the same period.

Great bridge with history The history behind the bridge is great. It's also unique because there's a Pagoda attached to it. But besides that, it looks unimpressive compared to other bridges. There's too many visitors though!

From far, it's an old bridge, built in the 1590s by the Japanese. But this little bridge is highly emblematic of Hoi An. Most tourists would take a picture with this bridge if visiting Hoi An, and indeed, we saw a lot of people around it...making it hard to take a good picture for yourself.
Nothing to write home about but it is the climax of the ancient town visit. Historically the border between the Japanese and Chinese area
This is a must see if you are visiting Hoi An. Good to see as part of a walking tour. A very old structure and there is lots to see inside. Very crowded some days.
Whilst you're at the old town its easy to check this out. No ticket needed. Nice lightnig at the evening.

Not sure what all the fuss is about however. You have to pay to cross the bridge which connects streets in the town. I didn't find this out until later because I crossed and no one stopped me (oops!).
Not really much to see but it is worth a visit to get the old feel of this place and to take a photo or two.
Worth a look when exploring Hoi An, but beware the bridge is extremely busy at peak times. Best viewed from the street alongside the river.

Nice piece of history The bridge is very pretty and it's a nice piece of history being the oldest bridge in Vietnam, but that's about it. It is very busy with people taking photos in front of it. Still worth a look though.
Small bridge, there are many tourists here In the evening , when dark coming looking more interest
The bridge is really cool and there are plenty of good angles to get some food photos. It didn't quite live up to the expectations I had after hearing about it, but it was good nonetheless
This is at the end of the street in Old Hoi An. So if you go there you will see it. It has historical signifance

Over rated for a small bridge that looks old but is not For a high rated attraction this is so completely forgettable. Although it looks old, it looks to have been repainted and maintained within the last 50 years. The bridge is a covered structure built by the Japanese with a Buddhist temple attached to one side. Your better off reading the wikipedia entry on the historical context that taking the time to track this down and visit. This is less than 15 minutes to cover off.
Going through Hoi An this bridge has an amazing architecture, but same as the previous review, it's just a bridge at the end of the day. But worth looking through it nevertheless.
When you are in Hoi An, it would be a bit crazy not to visit the Japanese covered bridge, but having seen it, I don't understand the hype.
Impressive ancient bridge Impressive ancient bridge, although I sincerely expected more from it! Really small and crowded. Hoi An ancient town is overrrated in my modest opinion.
