Ancient wooden bridge. Well maintain through the century. Classical and well built. Nice view of the surrounding area.
Smaller that I expected and very, very busy. I think it could do with a makeover looks very 'tired'.

Very small wooden bridge leading into a more 'secluded' part of the Ancient Town. Not sure whether you're supposed to buy tickets or not and it would be better if Hoi An just made a decision and applied it fairly to everyone. There was a little table/desk with a sign saying Ticket Sales, but there was nobody there. Regardless, it's a very small, obviously very old, bridge, made completely from wood. The floor, the railings and the roof are all wood. If you don't stop to take photos, you can cross the bridge in about 10 seconds. Yes, it's that small.
This bridge is really old one It's like symbol of hoi an But I don't know why do i pay for
Classic Old Quarter Charming Archetecture A cool piece of history in the heart of the Old Quarter. Made for a great place to take pictures, can get a bit crowded at peak times.

It is a lovely bridge with some nice details. Over the river connecting the old town. Nice to see as part of a tour/ walk around the ancient town. With a tour guide you learn more about the history of it.
It's a bridge with a bit history Takes about 5 minutes to see it and cross it. No biggie, but nice. It connects two of the three sections of old town.

Interesting but not spectacular The wooden structure of the Japanese bridge is interesting to see, it is a small structure, but gets quickly crowded when tourist groups come to look at it. There are nicer pgeared more to selling embroidered tablecloths, napkins, silk scarves, etc.

If you know the history, it may be interesting. But for me, it was just a bridge. You can find Japanese letters(katakana) on lantern on the bridge. It says hoi an. The looking is quite different daytime from night. You should go there twice.
Sorry but I was so under whelmed by this bridge. After reading about it I dragged my family though the old town on the hottest day ever to find it and was so disappointed.
If you need to go, do it during lunch time, it is the time to enjoy it from the river and from inside

You can take a quick walk across this bridge- the view isn't much different from what you see on the street. There is a small shrine area inside the bridge, but not much to it, especially if you don't know what you're looking at.

Nicer to look at from the outside This bridge is very beautiful to look at from the outside but crossing over it is very anticlimactic, it's very small and there are people everywhere. The view you are looking out at isn't anything special. But it's free to cross so best to check it out for yourself.
View is amazing and all the shops along the area but too crowded. Most of the stalls are over pricing. Must be careful in buying stuff, and compare first the prices.
The most popular site in Hoi An it's generally crowded and because it's quiet small you must visit before the afternoon rush. Millions of photos taken every year. The museum is also tiny
In my opinion not the best part of the old town but certainly adds to the charm of the whole area. Lovely photo opportunity

Need some reservation This bridge is also famous in Hoi an which many tourists come to visit. However this is very crowded and people come and sit on bridge so we can not see anything. There is a small pagoda on bridge where people come to pray.
Walking over this bridge you do perceive the Japanese influence with the design of this bridge. An amazing walk through history. You won't spend long here but it's worth ticking of the list.
The bridge it's self, is only very small, and worth a look. Unfortunately the little water way it spans, stinks of stagnant muddy water.
Nice bridge, but Too many people around there. You don't get to enjoy going there when it's so many people pushing around.

its ok but I would never pay to see it again it is now all about revenue raising the face of Hoi An is changing fast no more traditional greeting of Xin Choa if they find out your Australian a very annoying g'day mate they find funny and its not
Nothing special especially if you have to pay to cross over. You can get across by another route. Just a tick to say yes you have seen it.

Average Japanese covered bridge Not sure what the fuss is about Quite nice but lonely planet says in top 3 attractions - see photo Also very odd monument in the middle which you need a ticket for However Hanoi is fantastic
The covered bridge is just that - a covered bridge! It has a place in the history of Hoi An and it is worth seeing. However, I personally did not think it that special!
Nice historic bridge, shame water is polluted. Lots of restaurants nearby or can cross other bridge for more.
You need to pay to enter (enter from the "monkey" end of the bridge to the "dog" side to exit. One crossing only or you will have to buy tickets to cross back.

Eh...it's a nice bridge. It's a nice bridge and it has historical significance for the town of Hoi An. It was just ok. It becomes incredibly crowded at night and there really isn't much to see. A quick walkthrough and a photo can be accomplished in about 10 minutes.

Not much to se here or do. A few things to read. i would still go just to say I had seen it. One side looks good but the back end not so. Very old bridge with good history . Only spent 5/10 mins here.
Went as marked on list of things to see. Went through and back again then took a photo. If you don't have time to see this I don't think you have missed anything

This draws a crowd because it is iconic and it certainly offers some escape from the hot June sun. But it's fairly limited inside room - wise and for interesting things to see and do. You basically walk across the canal via this bridge in about 2 minutes including the sightseeing. Besides, it's just another tourist trap where the touts hassle you to buy stuff - very tiring always saying no to unwanted sales pitches!
Yes it is a Japanese Covered Bridge; but it is nothing special inside or out. In fact it looks like it needs some repair.

Totally overcrowded. Go in the morning. This is a lovely bridge and a historical feature of Hoi An. However it is totally over run and almost impossible to go near in the evenings. People with zero spacial awareness and wielding selfie sticks like swords. Best time of day is early Morning that way you have a chance of getting a photo and beating the crowds of people who are in Hoi An.
They will try and charge you when entering the old city or over the bridge but just say no it's quite pretty at night as is the whole place
The Japanese Bridge is in Old Town. If stories of the Japanese war interest you visiting this attraction will also interest you. It was nice to see it but that is about all I have to say

Nice architecture bridge! This bridge seem to become a symbol of Hoi An ancient town. Its history was really interesting. There is a small pagoda on the bridge for praying. It was very crowded and we only stand to see it within 5 minutes because we need to move for others can come.

Nice old bridge, pleased to have seen it. Very busy with people. Would not advise a major detour to see it unless you really have an interest in old bridges. Pleased to have seen but not worth a specific day trip for.

Small and dilapidated The bridge is tucked away and can only be seen from th front, a building obscures the back. You cannot enter the bridge without a ticket which they demand you buy as you enter the old town. You do not need a ticket unless you want to enter one of the 5 attractions. Trying to get a photo is difficult and this is definitely not my favorite thing to see
Visited this landmark in Hoi An, it is popular and I can say I saw it but I would not go out of my way to see it.

nothing special, packed with selfie-taking tourists Try not to go at night as it would be packed with local/tourists. Lots of them with long selfie sticks trying to take a picture with the bridge? Nothing really special about the bridge.

Seen it, photo taken, move on Get a photo from the outside. We didn't pay to walk over it like others. Yeah its an old bridge, but I've seen better that are older. Would be even better if the canal it spans over was cleaned.
If you are an architecture junkie than this bridge is for you otherwise it's a nice shot for pictures and some information on Hoi An.

A pretty bridge. Must be made well because it holds the hundreds of tourists crammed on there taking selfies. You won't really miss it if you wonder around. But probably not an attraction in itself. Go in the early morning to avoid the crowds

Go when many are not about My fear when on this bridge was that it was going to collapse under the shear weight of the swarms of tourists crossing standing for selfies or otherwise just looking over the sides. Whilst I found the history of the bridge interesting including a Chinese temple being adjacent to what was a Japanese bridge given the the Chinese and Japanese have not historically been the best of friends was unusual. The magnificence of the bridge is taken away though by the manner in which the site is managed or more particularly not managed- just too many people such that you are unable to enjoy the experience
The bridge was interesting to look at and a good photo opportunity. worth a look when you are walking around the Hoi An Ancient City

Cool spot for a quick pic. If you come to Hoi An you'll definitely end up in the Ancient town where a lot of the action is, and you will come across the Japanese Bridge. Impossible to get a photo of it not crammed with other tourists, but still a neat part of the cities cultural heritage.

The centre of the Hoi An museum trail, with museums and temples on either side of the bridge. You buy a £4 ticket which gets you into all the sights in the town. The bridge itself is very photogenic during the day and lit up at night. When you go across it there is a little plaque explaining it's history in English, apart from that you do not need a lot of time here but worth seeing. Not good for wheelchair access as there are like 12inch boards to step over each side of the bridge.
It's an old bridge, yep. It's a nice ancient bridge that you can pass through. All good, but not a place to spend a lot of time. The old town around the bridge is reall interesting!

The bridges architecture is nice to admire but that's about it. You can have a look inside and it's good for taking for photos. However we walked across the bridge many times and in one occasion there was a woman there who demanded money for us to cross even though we'd crossed many times before with no problems. Obviously we didn't cross when she tries to make us pay and when we walked back later no one was there and people were crossing freely. If you go and someone tries to make you pay go back another time as it was definitely free most of the time.

Definitely an interesting bridge. Worth having a look at and will only take a few minutes out of your day. Some people have said its hard to find but just walk into the old town from the An Hoi bridge and turn left at Tran Phu street and there it is.
Do not buy a ticket, just ignore them. Bridge is nice, just walked over it and took some pictures. Definitely pass by while being in hoi an but don't make a day trip for this.

The bridge looks better at night with the lighting. Entrance ticket is required to enter this place in day time. You can get the entrance ticket at VND120000 per person. Each entrance ticket comes with 5 small tickets to visit the selected world heritage place around the ancient town.
Came to see the Japanese bridge as it was free to do/see! It was overcrowded and it wasn't anything special as such. But good to see if you're going past.

It's a bridge in the centre If you go to the old town you will find this bridge. It's a very small but full of character bridge. You must read the history otherwise it's a pointless exercise. Great photo opportunity.

See it both during day and night Honestly, there really isn't much to see here but you need to see it both during the day and night. It just looks totally different during the night when the lights are all lit up under the bridge. You will also need a ticket to go inside the bridge, but it's covered within the Hoi An Ancient Town pass.

They really try to get your money for everything and anything Its a bridge. And they charge to cross. Just because its one more opportunity to get money from tourists. Everything seems like a rip off at some point which is quite a shame...

The bridge was much small than I thought it would be. There are heaps of tourists trying to get photos. The only problem I had was that someone had left empty water bottles on the side of the bridge! They made it look so tacky.

It's a bridge. It is covered. There is a small shrine halfway across. One side has a year of the monkey statue ,the other has year of the dog - representing the year it was started and finished. Worth a look if you are wandering the old town.
The bridge is a must-see while you're in Hoi An, but the hype around it does result in a slight disappointment when you find out it's just another pretty bridge

Lovely old bridge, but have to pay to go over it, unless you walk around the back, as it's only one way they you pay. Unfortunately, it spans a filthy stream/river, full of rubbish, they have a long way to go to get their garbage sorted.
It is o did the oath to go thru the old town , old building but small , many people take photo outside of the bridge . Inside of the bridge consists of a house / temple .

I was a little disappointed with this bridge. It is pretty to look at but there is a fine line between quaint and run-down. The rubbish in the water and the smell, tells me this tourist attraction is not receiving the TLC it perhaps should. You have to pay to go across to the other side where you'll find a pleasant street to explore if you want to. On the bridge is a small temple. 10 minutes later we were looking to move on but it was worth a photo...

Its a great piece of history, wich can be flooded in the raining season. Beautiful structure of monkey and dog. It really striked me when i heard we had to pay, if we wanted to cross, because the other side of the bridge is marvelous. Its not much, but i think it is unneccesary.
Go at night as they didn't seem to bother with this ridiculous ticketing system, it looks better at night and the bats come out from the eaves
The whole bridge is only less than 10 meters. Other than the crossing of the bridge inside, there is nothing much to see on the interior.

The covered bridge was much smaller than we were led o believe and the entire structure can be seen in only 10 or 15 minutes. Again, as with other areas, if it is a busy time, be prepared for the crush of crowds on the narrow walkway inside the bridge. An interesting history but not a site we'd go out of our way to see again.
Not worth a trip unless you happen to be in the ancient town anyway. Bridge is small and unremarkable.
Well, it's not the Rialto Bridge in Venice but . . . . . . you can use it to get from one side of historic Hoi An to another. No reason to avoid it. It's the Japanese Covered Bridge, after all.

Old deteriorated bridge Nothing can be imagined the reason why people come and pass through this bridge. Maybe there are some special good reason which only I dont know. But roof is beautiful and historical place itself. Vietnamese bride and bridegrooms come take pics in front of this bridge as forever background.

I was very disappointed. I suppose when everyone was saying you have to see the bridge l expected something more dramatic. Yes its old heritage and you have to take the usual photo to say ..been there got the T shirt, but don't waste one of your 5 city vouchers just to walk through to the other side. Lots of art shops on the way to it which to be honest l found more interesting

It's a bridge built a really loooong time ago Our tour was part of our Vietnam package so the fee to visit was included in the total package. I don't know how much it was but i suspect not much. Now you can see it for free but to go on it there is a fee. Our guide gave us all the history about the bridge which is interesting and there is a temple in there as well, a little tiny one and he gave us all the info on that too. It took about 10 minutes. Pretty sure you could read all the information about it in a Lonely Planet guide book or google it and be just as happy and save a few pennies on the guide. Up to you. :)

Pretty bridge, but really nothing more to it than a quick snapshot to check it off the box. Entrance to go through the bridge (and 4 other sites) is 120k dong...so not worth it. We just took a picture from the outside and the view facing away from the bridge is just as pretty.
well its just a bridge, old and interesting if you into history etc but nothing to rave about,looks better at night when its lit up but a bridge is just a bridge

Overcrowded by visitors/tourists in the evening & night. Nothing Special to see for Asians especially. But, for others from Europe, America, Africa , Australia, Middle East., it would be worth the time and effort.

This bridge gets a 5/5 as far as being a bridge goes, it fills the function excellently. As the name suggests, it's also covered so also 5/5 for meeting expectations. Other than that it lacks 'wow' factor and I only stopped to take a photo as I was near it anyway. I wouldn't go out of your way to see it (unless your a massive fan of bridges) - especially if you're short on time.
Ancient town is a fabulous area and a must visit but the japanese bridge is just a bridge dont see the big attraction and why you have to pay to walk accross it.

Anice but overcrowded bridge This is a nice covered bridge which has become one of the landmarks of the town. As with everything else here this was overrun by tourists who lined up half an hour to get a selfie.
Another easy site to visit Hoi An has an interesting mix of architecture from previous inhabitants and trading nations. Great place to take photo.
Basically just a historical bridge which should be able to done in 10 mins or so(or even less) do read the story of the bridge to have better understanding. That it
Very popular to visit and very crowded. Read the history of the bridge - this is the best part and only reason to visit.

Historically interesting but not a must do Historically this bridge represents important links to the town's trading past, but as an attraction it is a little underwhelming. It is a good gateway to the quiet and vehicle-free streets beyond the town and this makes crossing the bridge worthwhile. These quiet leafy streets beyond the bridge are a welcome break from the noise and relentless pressure to buy in the main town. There are some interesting photographs in the rooms off the bridge and as part of the old town ticket system, it is probably just worth visiting. It is iconically photogenic.

The bridge carries its historical meaning in representing the connection between Japan and Chinese in business. We just walked by, took some photos. There were a dog and a monkey status there. Nothing really so attractive.

It's good. Gotta buy a ticket/donation to be made before stepping on the historic bridge though not a big deal but if you just need to get to the other side and don't mind walking a bit just go around the block lol. In saying that though the tickets you buy is a one off so keep them! Cause you can use it again for the rest of your trip in ancient town.
This bridge is located inside Hoi An Ancient Town. It is a Japanese designed bridge. Take lots of photos.
The bridge is nice for photos but thats it. Nothing much to see. You will walk past it so many times visiting the Ancient Village.
Nice to see if you re around One old town ticket required to enter the bridge, but it's not really necessary to enter (nearly nothing to see inside). Nice to see from outside.

it is a 20 m long bridge connecting two parts of the old town. If you wanna stroll through the whole length of the old city it is inevitable that you will cross it. In the entrance the ask to show your ticket for admission to the old city (a one time ticket that you have to buy for the equivalent of 6 USD in order to be able to wander through the old town and enter selected temples. The bridge has a wooden floor and features a prayer room on one side. It is a nice bridge but let's not overreact over it. You have seen it before, somewhere.
The bridge is a good photo opportunity but gets very busy and you have to pay to get over it? If you do decide to take the plunge you will find very little to get excited about inside.

Interesting....a little dodgy This was rather fascinating, however unusually (we were travelling with a local) they wanted us to buy a ticket to walk onto the bridge. This all seemed a little dodgy. The bridge is fascinating, enjoy

As appears on the VND20,000 note. This is one of the attractions you'll see as you walk around the old town. It was nice to get under some shade, a side benefit of visiting this site. While many will look at the bridge from the outside, looking from the inside out makes for good framed pictures.
I didn't appreciate this attraction until i had read the history. We paid nothing, but be weary. If you are not going to temple, ensure you say so!
Cute, one nice picture but nothing more Cute, one nice picture but nothing more. No need to pay anything unless you want to cross it or go on it.

If you've paid your entry price into the old town then you've got to do the bridge. Worth doing at night when it's lit up. There are often newly weds having pictures taken on boats near the bridge which is very pretty.

Nice enough, not sure what the hype is to be honest. Small bridge in the middle of town. Used to connect the ancient Chinese & (now non-existent) Japanese parts of the villages. Make sure that if you do want to see it, &/or take pictures, do so at high tide. It is a tad smelly otherwise
Japanese bridge was interesting This was interesting enough but i would not have gone out of my way to visit it. However i walked across it a number of times as part of a visit to the old city.

Interesting place to see...but costs a coupon of your old city ticket if you go from the old city across...coming into old city they don't charge. Make sure to go in the little museum and read about the bridge - otherwise you will completely miss the point of this site.
Visited this bridge, but I honestly don't know what all the fuss is about. Very ordinary and not lit up at night at all.

Not quite sure what the fuss was about? The bridge is pretty, nice to look at at night when it's lit up, but I wouldn't necessarily say it's a must-see attraction. You get it free with the city ticket so you might as well, and if you go over it the shops on the other side are cheaper!

Entering from the east side doesn't seem to attract any fees. But we walked there from the west side (Nguyen Thi Minh Khai) during the day and were told we had to pay a fee to enter the street from some ladies in a booth! Noticing some asian looking people after us who did not get asked to pay any money (scam!). We said no thanks and walked down another street alongside the river to by pass them.
Not much to see - it's just a photo op. Looks nice in photos but you just walk across the bridge. It's located inside Hoi An Ancient Town so all tourist visit this site anyway.
Nice but is it worth the fuzz? Well it is a bridge and it is Japanese. We saw it at night and day and at night the lights are giving it some nice shadows.
Packed with tourists and passersby....an interesting relic of old Hoi An but stay away unless you visit early morning.

Neat to see, lots of people though. We stopped by here to get some photos on the bridge. I guess everyone else did too. LOL!! It is very pretty, esp at night. Expect 5 other people in your photo if you are going :)
Do take a mandatory photoshoot with this bridge in Hoi An. Packs a load of history & people as well! Saw a dog & a cat on the bridge as well!
Read the history first. Not very meaningful unless you know the history behind it. It was also packed with loud and pushy Mainland tourists which scared our son. Well maintained.
Probably the only attraction I didn't think it was worth using the old town ticket for. Wait until after 1930 when it is free.
The bridge is certainly a piece of history. It's not very big and kids are going to get bored quickly, but worth visiting if you appreciate the history behind it.

Better to see at night Be careful about being asked for a ticket crossing this bridge. There is an alter for praying. I told the guy I had an entry ticket and was not giving a ticket for crossing the bridge. This is a pleasant enough bridge which you won't miss as it's part of Old Town but is pretty at night for photos.

A good inside area, but slightly less attractive outside... This looked pretty bad from the outside, as it looked like a little covered bridge. Inside it had a praying area and a really cool place that had lots of statues and religious things. Overall, this was an okay place, but a little boring.
Very pretty inside, less so outside This is quite a cute 17th century Japanese bridge with a lovely little shrine. It was also fairly quiet inside when we went. Worth a walk through.

Go early mornings to get the best photo shoots There is not much to say about this. You might be lucky to get a photo shoot with few people in your picture and for the bridge keeper to put the national flag out.

Lovely bridge, awful crowds A nice little historical bridge. Passing through can be a challenge due to crowds. Selfies should be forbidden on this bridge. Walked there many times, and in quiet times is very nice, but quiet times are rare in Hoi An

Interesting, but not sure if it is worth the ticket Visited the bridge while in Hoi An, on an extremely hot and humid day, so had a really hot visit. Not sure if I would do it again, but I guess it is just one of those things that you have to do while in Hoi An.

It is quite a short bridge but very old Japanese style. Best photos from off the bridge. It costs and really I guess that fee is for up keep of these historic sites. No idea of fee as tour guide paid for us.

If you're in Hoi An you will definitely come across this little bridge sooner or later.Whether you will be able to see it properly is another matter due to the hordes of tourists swamping it and doing the all important selfies. To me it just felt like being in a Disney theme park which is a shame given the history of this bridge and the whole of Hoi An old town.
When in Hoi An be sure to wander to the Japanese brigade lovely to see. Free to cross and take some photos. Try and ser day and night.
Historically important bridge! It may be a historically important landmark but doesn't look so fabulous. If you are in Hoi An it is good to see it and take couple of photos.

The Japanese bridge is of cause a must see in Hoi An. But really.. walk across the bridge and take a few pictures - it's all over in 5 min. The "story" that should intrigue the visitor isn't really visible and the attraction is therefore not that exiting.
The bridge was cool, but extremely crowded. If you go early in the morning, it's definitely worth seeing
Beautiful old bridge situated in the preserved ancient town of Hoi An. Worth a look if you are visiting the old town.
Just one more thing to see in the Old Town, but nothing much to recommend this over any other sight in the Old Town.

too many people. very touristy this bridge which has a complicated history is hard to see due to the press of tourists. I crossed it, took photos.. and thought about the documentary showing the bridge without as many people and was glad I could imagine it from that point of bview.
Positioned in the centre of town this can easily be incorporated into a walking tour of the town . Nothing too spectacular but interesting

Japanese Covered Bridge -Hoi An This little historic bridge dates back to the late 1500s and crosses the Thu Bon River. It's small and although it's limited in the information available within it - it's still worth a visit.
I would not make a detour to see that bridge! If you are a Japanese architecture fan or somebody that like bridge you could go take a selfie there.

Overrated lovely old bridge During the daylight hours, you buy a ticket and jostle with thousands trying to get a good angle for a wefie or selfie. Visit after 10pm and you will actually be able to appreciate the lovely little bridge. I wish they could clean up the river though.
Ancient bridge an interesting place to visit in Hoi An when visiting the old town. Wonderful to visit at night with all the brightly coloured lanterns. River trips and great variety of restaurants

Not as a great as it's hyped up to be Everyone was talking about the Japanese covered bridge but upon seeing it (and walking through it), it didn't appear to be that special. I understand it has historic significance and you will probably run into it during your stay, but you aren't really missing anything if you don't see it.

Having been to Japan and loving the architecture and designs this bridge is relatively worm down and not much care has been taken to keep it in good shape. It is very boring and not really worth more than 5 minutes of your time

Depending on the cost. Sometimes the old town will arbitrarily charge tourists (or even locals!) to visit the town - this ticket includes many sights in the town and can be well worth it. It also, however, sometimes includes a pass to get over the Japanese bridge. If asked to pay a price to view it, I wouldn't bother - simply walk around the back way. If you have the town ticket already, no worries. Most days however you will be able to pass freely through the bridge - even seeing the majority of the 'museum' from the door.
I'm going to be honest I don't know anything about this bridge except that it is very old and stuff. It was nothing special to go over, just much people.

Quite pretty - but you don't need a ticket just to cross it ! Quite a pretty little bridge which makes a nice photo, but when we tried to cross it we were asked if we had a ticket. When we said we didn't we were turned away. HOWEVER - you don't need a ticket ! If you just want to cross and not look at the temple which is on the bridge no ticket is necessary. Just ask the question 'Do I have to have a ticket just to cross ?' !!
It's a nice bridge, inside is nothing special. You can just watch it from the outside and not buy a ticket.
Pretty but not sure it's worth it A very pretty bridge to take some photos of from the outside but I'm not sure if it's worth paying the entrance fee to have a look inside

Nice place to see in the city center The historic center of Hoi An is a charming and beautifully preserved area that feels like stepping back in time. Its lantern-lit streets, unique mix of Vietnamese, Chinese, and Japanese architecture, and landmarks like the Japanese Covered Bridge create a magical atmosphere. With its riverside views, lively markets, and delicious local cuisine, it offers an unforgettable cultural experience.

The Laiyuan Bridge was built by the Japanese in 1593 and seems to have connected the then Japanese town to Chinatown. However, the current Laiyuan Bridge was completed in August 2024 with extensive restoration work. In fact, if you walk around, you will see that it is not a big bridge (about 20 meters long). Many tourists came and went, so I didn't get much of a taste of history. Automatically translated
This is a very old bridge that is worth visiting. Plan a walk across it as part of your walk around the Ancient City. Then move to the main water way to see it from a distance.
It was quaint and nice. It has character, but it's not as if it's a huge deal. If you are in Old Town, you might as well take a swing by.

Beautiful bridge on the outside Famous tourist attraction of Hoi An World Heritage Site. Visitors must purchase tickets in advance to visit five paid spots. Apparently, it was free if only the bridge traffic. It was good to be exposed to the history of Japanese people living here and building bridges more than 430 years ago. Automatically translated

It's not the biggest bridge (you can walk over it in less than a minute, but it will probably take you longer due to the crowds of people). You do need to pay to go across and, to be honest, the views of the bridge are better from outside. It's been restored compared to how it was before the town received UNESCO status. If you want the best photos, go early in the morning when everyone else is still in bed.

A very beautiful bridge and the small museum exhibition on the bridge itself is interesting to see - you need to pay for entry or use your Hoi an Ancient town visitor pass. It's super crowded though, we went to see it in the afternoon, there are hoards of people and even more instgrammers posing with their tripods/ cameras and Instaboyfriends. So, I recommend you visit first thing in the morning when it should be quieter. Its a quick 30 minute look around though, rather than a big tourist attraction.

Chic bridge with history... The Japanese covered bridge is a cultural testimony of the times. Japanese merchants built it in the 16th century. It is an architectural landmark of the city that is not to be missed. Although a bit overrated, but as a sightseeing simply belongs to a city tour. It bridges a small channel. However, if you want to take beautiful photos without crowds for your home photo album, you should arrive here very early, because as time progresses towards lunch and evening, it gets very crowded here. Automatically translated

History of Vietnam and Japan This bridge chronicles the history of friendship between Vietnam and Japan. This wooden bridge was built by the Japanese who lived in the area in the 16th century. It has been recently renovated and cleaned up. Some of them are in Japanese. Automatically translated
If you want to be quiet you have to go in the morning, in the evening it is crowded and the photos of which impossible even if the bridge is very nice illuminated.... Automatically translated

Pagoda bridge 18 meters long and 3. Built in the mid-16th century, it is called the Japanese Bridge. Legend has it that Namazu, a great sea monster with head in India, body in Vietnam and tail in Japan; every time he moved he caused sweats and earthquakes so to make him stop wagging his tail, they built the bridge like a sword placed on the weakest point of the monster. The bridge served as a point to discuss the port's commercial disputes. Upon entering there is a "pagoda", where a protective and punitive Taoist god of monsters is venerated. They stuck with me the two eyes of the door, a typical architecture of Hoi An. Automatically translated

Emblematic bridge in the old quarter of Hoi An The Japanese Bridge is one of Hoi An’s iconic landmarks, I had to go in the early hours of the morning to take good pictures without the crowds of people seen at night. Nobody comes to Hoi An without taking a picture of the bridge! Automatically translated

The Japan Bridge is called the Long Way Bridge The Japanese Bridge was built in 1953 by the Japanese, connecting the then Chinese street and the Japanese city. It is said that it was built in the year of the monkey and the year of the dog, so at the entrance of the bridge on both sides there was a monkey and a monkey guard. A small temple was built on the bridge to worship the god of heaven to bless the local residents. Automatically translated

Famous bridge in Anguilla Town The famous bridge in Vietnam’s Anchorage, with two monkey statues on the side and two dog statues on the other side, since the line is from the monkey year to the dog year. In the middle of the bridge is the North Temple. Automatically translated

It's a symbol of the city that's on the bill! Commonly known as Nihonbashi, it is said to have been built in 1593 by the Japanese who lived in Hoi An at the time. It is a sturdy structure that takes into account earthquakes and is remarkable for its architecture of the period. Automatically translated
Hoi An Japanese bridge, hardly worth the visit Fascinating structure, always busy with people, especially tour groups. This makes it difficult to move about and take pictures.

Photo shoot ,recently restored.2024. This bridge waqs built in the 1500,s,and still remains standing,although it has recently been closed for total restoration,we were so privalidged as it has now reopened,a wonderous site,lit up at night,get real photo with a camera,i phone do not capture its splender.
We are in the heart of the city and this bridge is the pulsating symbol of it. Thinking that the style is Japanese makes you realize how multicultural Vietnam is. Automatically translated
Just happened to be in Hoi An for the lantern festival and wandered past the refurbished Japanese covered bridge. Hoi An certainly has an interesting international heritage.

The visit came in the month after a year-and-a-half of restoration work that Japan also cooperated with. The buildings turned white overall, and the river, which looked like a slush, was cleaned. However, the sense of heaviness that had been felt until now has disappeared. Etiquette is the best way to buy tickets to Hoi An City to combat overtourism. Many day-trippers and individual customers are also making it difficult to pay for the service. Source of funds for restoration work and morning cleaning. As a Kyoto resident, I don't think the issue of overtourism is a stranger. Automatically translated

We bought tickets to this ancient bridge and realized it was a thriving community once upon a time. Now it's a tourist attraction and very crowded. In the evening beautiful lanterns decked up everywhere can be seen from this bridge.
I walked through the bridge a couple of times during my stay in Hoi An. I did not have a ticket and no one asked for one. It's a nice bridge and over crowded. It does fit in nicely with the old town.
A must see bridge in Hoi An. Nice covered bridge in the middle of the city. Not to.miss especially at night when it's nicely lighten
Must go to this area for a vibrant night life and beautiful photos of lanterns, boats bridges BUT the Japanese bridge is still under construction 7months down the line now

Went there in May 2023 as part of the Hoi An tour package. Didn't get a chance to capture an image of the bridge, as it is under renovation. It's filled with history, as part of Hoi An's ancient town.

visited here on 9 September,and found that it was under renovation, even though, you still have to buy ticket to visit it, you can see workers repair the bridge through the iron bar.i first visited the bridge in 2019, it is very beautiful, especially at night.so sad cannot have nice pictures here this time.i bought two bronze coins at the ticket checking counter.there is a series of 12 zodiac coins sold here, you can buy your own zodiac coin, i bought my own zodiac coin one,and also one zodiac cat coin, cuz year 2023 is the year of cat in vietnam, and it is unique, cuz in China and Korea, 2023 is the year of rabbit. will come here again, hope that time, the renovation has completed.

This is a lovely bridge in the heart of Hoi An. We couldn't walk on the bridge when we went, but we did go a while ago, so maybe you can now. It was still pretty to look at and was all lit up. It's centrally located, so well worth a quick visit.

The temple is a perfect blend of architectural beauty, lush greenery, and picturesque landscape. Visitors can marvel at the intricate details of the temple's architecture and be mesmerized by the spiritual aura that radiates from its grounds. The temple is surrounded by lush greenery and is a great spot to relax and take in the beauty of nature. The picturesque landscape offers stunning views of the nearby river and nearby villages, making it a great spot for photography.
Its nice bridge and we loved this architecture.The ancient town also was very beautiful.Walking arround 2 hours and enjoyed it
We visited this just as the sun was going down. Even my teenager said it was amazing. Beautiful at night.
The oldest bridge in Hoi An, built by the Japanese. A short bridge across a canal, there is a small temple in the middle of the bridge. The bridge gives you access to the old city area.
A very nice bridge accessible.by buying the old city ticket. Even don't get on it, walking Glacier pass it gives you a great view of the building.
Nice place to visit - It is both a bridge and a pagoda. Really old and good place for photos. It is right in central of Hoian
Super crowded, and the architecture is old and unique in my opinion. It is beautiful but quite a lot of people so I chose to go to shop nearby haha
Very crowded, there are many visitors here but it did not affect my experience much. The bridge looks quite beauty at night and people should visit here when they come to Hoian

ONE TIME VISIT FOR PHOTO OP Very crowded at night . So go there take your mandatory photos and get out. Bridge has nice architectural designs and ancient setting. Otherwise very crowded and most people’s not wearing masks anymore

Mildly interesting to see a significant piece of architecture. Worth walking through for something to do - not worth making a special trip for. Not Easy to get a photo. Unfortunately now it is just an overcrowded with tourist. I will try again, elsewhere.

This bridge is worth visiting It is More Than a Bridge. A very nice walk along the broad walk. There er a lot of people here, lots of food options in crescent mall. A magical place in the evening with lanterns lit up
This is a very iconic site, linking 2 parts of Hoi An's ancient town. I am a little confused, because on one occasion we were asked to buy a ticket to cross it, the next times not.

Historically noteworthy feature. Built by the Japanese in the C17th. An interesting Hoi An site. Worth a stop while you are in the area. Therr are some photos on the wall which show how the bridge has endured over the last century in tact.
Nice historical site. It is free to enter. Now not many tourists due to Covid. Which is so refreshing. So it is easy to explore.

great story behind this bridge After being out earlier to visit the Champa Kingdom, we returned back to our hotel, then on for a tour in the afternoon of Hoi An. We saw the Japanese Covered Bridge. It was very busy during the afternoon, but it moves fast. This bridge was constructed 400 years ago to symbolize peace between the Japanese and Chinese communities.
Visited the beauty spot in the Old Town early in order to avoid the crowd. The bridge is pretty with great Japanese architecture. A must visit whilst in Hoi An.

Good Photo Op, lots of history The Japanese Covered Bridge was constructed in the 16th century by the Japanese community in Hoi An. It connected the Japanese quarter to the Chinese quarter. The bridge symbolized peace between the two communities. It has been restored many times over the years and no long has the original Japanese architecture. It now reflects Vietnamese architecture. There is a small shire inside the bridge. There is also statues of a dog and a monkey on opposing sides of the bridge. It is lovely to look at. One of your tickets will allow you to cross one way. If you wish to cross twice it will use two tickets. It is a beautiful bridge but most of its beauty is looking at it from the road or other free bridge a few feet away in front of it. I personally wouldn’t use one of my tickets to cross it. It is small and was very crowded plus everyone wants the same picture inside and out front. Don't forget to purchase your ticket before heading to a this stop at the Old Town Booth. The tickets includes 5 entrance fees. You have 24 hours from time of purchase to use them. It cost 120,000VND (around $7.10CAD). There are 21 sightseeing to choose from.

Beautiful architecture - window into the past Start attraction of Hoi An Old Town, the covered bridge seems to be what every person in town flocks to during their visit of the historical building. Part of the Old Town Entry fee, this site counts for one of the 5 locations you can visit on one ticket. Inside, the main bridge is beautiful with the view out the front towards the water lovely, but the glorious painting on the ceiling being the most impressive. Toward the back of the bridge, there is a room that was once the location of a shrine, but now contains historical photographs of Hoi An and the bridge. There is also man who sells Chinese zodiac medallions. One thing of note: the east entrance of the bridge is manned by attendants, punching your Hoi An Old Town ticket. The west entrance next to the Old Phung Hung House, does not(or did not on the day we visited - take that as you will).
One good thing about being in Hoi An during the coronavirus outbreak was the much reduced number of visitors fact you could manage a photo of this attractive bridge with no people in it !

Beautiful arcitecture This bridge is very beautiful and the information about it is really interesting. The only thing is it is very busy with tourists so if you'd like ot get a good photo you will have to wait a while. We also didn't realise that you needed to pay for a ticket to go on the bridge, however it doesn't cost that much. I'd say it was the best historical thing to visit whilst in Hoi An.
Lovely little detailed bridge in the heart of Hoi An. The story behind the dog and monkey statues on the bridge is interesting. Worth a quick visit if in Hoi An.

Quaint historic bridge in Hoi An Ancient Town A nice looking bridge over one of many of the canals in Hoi An. This bridge has both historical and cultural importance both to the Japanese and Vietnamese. Leads off into a nice quieter section of the ancient town.

Interesting architecture spoilt by the ‘poseurs’ An interesting site in the old town, it’s just a shame that the ‘poseurs’ are there for a long time trying to get the perfect picture with them centre stage, which spoils it for the majority.
Didn't bother to go inside..the outside is already enough for us. This is the super mini version famous pedestrian bridge in Luzern.
Historic 18th Century Bridge Interesting historic and picturesque 18th century bridge..... looks great lit up in the evening for pictures.

I love the view of the Japanese bridge from the little bridge near the river, it is just beautiful of a night. When one thinks that it was built over 400 years ago and all the people who have walked across it and continue to do so makes it very special.

See it for the history This historic bridge was built by the Japanese to be closer to the Chinese side of the city. It was built in the year of the dog and finished in the year of the monkey, so be sure to see the dogs on one side and the monkey statues on the other side. Interesting from the historic aspect since about 400 years old!
This is a small historic covered bridge built to link the Japanese community into the town centre. It is definitely worth a visit. It is free.
Lovely view of the bridge making a very picturesque setting for taking pictures. Just back from the Bon River in the old town.
Interesting if you are close by Little museum type place on bridge At the end of a main shopping street and by the river

We were only in Hoi An for a day and tried to cram everything in. This old bridge has been well looked after and is so busy you think it might be damaged with all the visitors. It is a very popular spot to take photos so be prepared to wait a bit.
Pretty simple to be honest. It's way better to visit it during the night also. During the day is way too crowded.
Nice spot to visit along the way while walking in Hoi An. Better at sunset for beautiful pictures, you can’t miss in the small beautiful lantern city of Hoi An

A major attraction in Vietnam, especially for Hoi An, both locals and tourists. The bridge dates back to the 18th century and enjoys a very cool historical provenance. Be sure to take the time and walk to the end of the main thorough fare of Hoi Sn and check out this pretty cool bridge.
Just a nice bridge on the way thrue old hoi an. Very old and a short story hangs inside the little covered bridge where it comes from etc.
Nice little bridge in the heart of hoi an, make sure you go inside to see some interesting artefacts. Great photo opportunities out the front too.
Pretty old wooden bridge. Has a little temple inside. Be prepared for crowds which makes taking photos difficult.
well worth a visit . Our guide with Tripadeal did not highlight it for us but should have Good directions to it would have helped

Interesting historic building If you visit Hoi An you'll no doubt cross this bridge many times. It's visually interesting and there's a small display of its history and an alter. Only takes ten minutes to look around but worth it.
Interesting Japanese bridge Unusual structure of Japanese influence which spans a narrow canal. It has a small shrine within it. Well worth visiting.
Looks like something off a film set. Worth a look and walk over if only to get to the other side. Nothing more to say really.
The best view was at night from a parallel bridge. Don't cross too often; a tear is made in your historic pass each time
Lovely little bridge, somewhat a must see when in Hoi an. Definitely a part of your visit - perhaps I would pick that before the pagodas.

The bridge is impressive and looks fantastic. Trying to get a photo without hoardes of people in it is virtually impossible. To cross the bridge you do need a ticket and these can be bought from several huts across the Old Town.

Lived up to advanced billing - worth a visit I had read about the Japanese Bridge and seen pictures and was glad to visit in person. It is an interesting area in Hoi An, right near the river, with lots of restaurants and shops. I would definitely walk over here when you are in the area.

Of the many attraction in Hoi An, this one requires one to buy a Attractions Ticket which covers this one and few other museums and temples in the heritage town. We came to know of this later hence couldn't get past the entrance. However the bridge looks lovely from outside. Inside there were a lot of tourist hence plan you trip well to account for the rush. Must visit place for Instagrammers.

This is a lovely covered bridge with a tonne of character to it and very much worthwhile visiting. It’s well known so quite busy (even busier than normal as it was raining heavily and provided great shelter).

A Simple Short Historical Walk It's a short bridge but full of history. Best to have a tour guide to explain the history of the Japanese bridge and a Chinese temple just behind bridge which could be acces inside the bridge.
Amazing detail and architecture. When we were there it was very crowded so we didn't enjoy all of its beauty.
Hoi An was a wonderful place to visit. The old center and the beaches. Everything to enjoy. Colourful, friendly, nice food, lots to see and not too big. One of my favorite places
Almost impossible to see by day due to the sheer number of tourists crowding the area. However, much quieter by night and very atmospheric with the scenic lighting.

Small town and market Great for site seeing It was very busy when we went as it was close to the far East New year. But the buildings are amazing and very nice to see the old style housing. The market is very big and has a large selection of authentic things to buy as well as copy brands. The Japanese bridge is very nice to see and go through with a little museum in the middle to look at. Well worth a trip to the area.
Usually packed with tourists. It’s very difficult to get a good photo. Try going on a smaller bridge adjacent to this place. The bridge features on the banknotes of Vietnam.
A very well preserved bridge with a small temple inside. There are signs in English with explanations. Very hard to visit due to overwhelmingly large crowds of tourists.

A favourite of everyone visiting Hoi An this 400 year old bridge is at times very crowded. I suggest waiting and taking your time to appreciate this old bridge. Especially if you want a good photo with out 100 other people crowding the shot.
A beautiful piece of Japanese style bridge in this touristic city. Is one of the few peace of old town attractions in this part of Vietnam.

Interesting bridge but too crowded to enjoy Nice Japanese-style beige overlooking Hoi An’s river. Needs a ticket for entry, but quite nice to see. This is the only covered bridge in the world to host a Buddhist temple inside. So if you are into checking boxes, good place to visit. The bridge leads to a long street with several shops and street stands. Decent visit, if you have time to kill in the city.
The old wooden bridge makes contribution to the cities nostalgic atmosphere. It looks very nice especially at night.
Centrally located in the old part of town, beautifully ancient and worth a look, a pretty pic opportunity, very busy.

The bridge is almost at the beginning of Hoi An city right in front of the river and is mostly a busy and crowded place but still there are times of the day that you can enjoy it and take photos without being disturbed. Better visit it during day or early in the morning. It is a great old structure that travels you to the past.
All part of being a tourist and walking the alley with the souvenirs and coffee shops. Crowded but fun.

Japanese covered bridge People were living in this streets and they start to build and resurrect this bridge. Many interesting stories when you come here... Remember to purchase tickets when you during to this bridge.

need ticket to cross the bridge but nothing much to see... the shops across the bridge are pretty much similar to the ones outside. maybe can consider saving the ticket else where. unless u want to take picture on the bridge itself

Visit as part of the walking route Would be useful to have a guide to explain the history of the bridge and the significance of it to Hoi An. You would need a ticket to enter the bridge, which I think it is a bit wasteful. The bridge at night looks a lot better.

Blend of styles - Chinese and Japanese More interesting is the story behind why the bridge was built. Temple inside takes you back in time and wonder how locals lived and what they believed. Very busy so expect big crowds.

Some of our party thought the bridge was brilliant, others couldn't see why we wasted one of our 5 visitor tickets to walk over. The view was better from the outside. So, a split review. You will just have to decide for yourself.

Interesting, but not spectacular Shame that you are supposed to pay to access this bridge. Structure is interesting as is the temple in the middle. Great landmark from which to get your bearings. This is almost the easiest way to get to the old town and the waterfront shops and restaurants. Best seen at night in conjunction with the lanterns.

A well preserved piece of history in the old town. Extremely popular venue and a must see if visiting Hoi An, don’t pay the toll go to the opposite sid elf the village and walk back through, no drama.
Beautifully maintained landmark Focal point of the old town Hoi An scene. Pleasant area for noshing and shopping. Easily reached from Da Nang.

The bridge was shorter than I thought but a great way to get across the river (of course). On the other side there were masses of people. I was there on Tuesday December 3rd. I did not end up paying anything.
Very interesting temple on the bridge. This is a very old bridge with a temple located on the bridge and monkey statues on both ends. The bridge from the exterior also makes for some lovely photos.
well worth a trip to Hoi An The Japenese bridge was a nice little bridge to see while walking through the town
Looks better at night. Historically important but visually blah. Need a UNESCO heritage sights ticket to go in and cross to the other side of the canal.

A famous landmark of the Hoi An Old Town It’s small and has a small museum inside that you have to pay to take a look at and no doubt you will at some point come across it and take that Photo opportunity. But as others have said not really worth making a special trip for.
Easy to find as per the map and something to simply walk by and get a photo of. Well worth finding and stopping for the photo.

Pretty but don’t go out of your way The bridge itself is beautiful to look at but after that it’s over. The bridge is right in old town so it’s worth a visit to then explore the surrounding area, but the bridge itself is rather small.

Just a must see, but not very impressive Sure, the ancient architecture is always interesting to see, but compared to the fascination of the old town of Hoi An, the Japanese Bridge is not outstanding at all. During sightseeing you will get here, so visit it anyway
An intriguing structure in Hoi An, a wonder to see and explore with anti-rooms with old photos, and historical documentation. Take the time to see and photograph this bridge.
This is truly a great centre of attraction so watch out for large groups. Let then pass and then have relative peace to explore. A great attraction which needs peace to explore
Take the time to stop and read the signs. Lots of interesting facts and great photos opportunities. This bridge has significant historical context and should not be missed.

We visited the old town and purchased a ticket which enabled us to cross the bridge and enter a couple of other attractions. Was it worth 120,000vnd each, we thought so. Vietnam doesn't have a lot of taxes and without tourists paying these fees the Old Town would end up a derelict looking place that no one would visit. Don't be a cheap skate, pay the money and enjoy your visit. Vietnam is cheap enough.

It's on the highlights list, might as well go Nice but a little crowded- it’s “the thing” to see in Hoi An and while it’s nice, way too many people. For the best pictures walk down the sides and take the pictures from the outside by the river. Tons of tour groups that go back and forth; expect some bumping and shoving. This is one of the "attractions" that you are supposed to have a ticket for, but we were never asked and we went over the bridge 3 times. You can't miss it if you are walking around the old quarter. Worth a picture stop I guess.

interesting link between cultures built to facilitate trade between japanese on one side and rest on the other, it has somehow retained the feeling of yesteryear without sentimentality or fake splendour. honest, wooden, with a splash of sanctity.

Nice little piece of Vietnamese / Japanese history. Read a little bit before going you will appreciate it more then looking at a bridge. Gets pretty busy with tourist and tours just have to pick the right time when everyone is eating or going home to the hotels to get a great picture.
Worth seeing, not necessarily from iside You need a ticket to walk through it. But you can also watch it from a short distance from another bridge
This is a great photo opportunity. There is a small charge to cross the bridge. There are bridges close by where no charge is made
Worth a visit to see this wooden bridge but try to get there in the morning as it is overrun with tourists by 11:30am and you will see nothing.
It's a nice bridge, just that what you see in pictures, that's it. A lot of people including us taking pictures there since it is a must see.
Interesting & well preserved Ornate roof carvings and relatively plain on the inside. The little shrine/pagoda in the middle is interesting
Part of the local history Steeped in local history, you simply cannot go to Hoi An without visiting the Japanese Covered Bridge. Not prohibitively priced and centrally located.
Unique piece of history of Hoian. Better to go with a guide to complete the history. Nice inside to see the Chinese zodiac and buy lucky coins for the kids according their animal year.

Japanese covered bridge The bridge is built during WW2 the Japanese invasion. The bridge is still preserved well. It is located at Hoi An ancient town. There is a fee collected for tourist to walk on it. Pretty lovely place to visit during the day or evening.

Surprising and delightful This bridge is unexpected in a city where most of the cultural influences are either Vietnamese or Chinese and was built by a small Japanese community settled in that part of the city and still adding their own contributions to Hoi An's cultural mix. Rather sadly the river under the bridge is dirty and smelly!

Small Historic Bridge at Hoi An The bridge is almost at the end of ancient town. Its small but it kept Japanese and other people separated during 18th century. You can talk a walk, or hire bicycle or cyclo to visit this one of the iconic place of this unesco heritage site. To enter the bridge and go other side you need to buy ticket.

Whilst there is not a lot to see, please remember it costs about £3 to visit 5 attractions or places of interest. Interesting old structure with quite a bit of history attached. It will only take a few minutes so why not?

Beautiful place located in the ancient town of Hoi An. You will find it just by watching the massive amount of people taking pictures and crossing it, specially during the evening. Nice place, overcrowded.

A short 18th century old bridge in the old town spanning a canal/drain near the river. Was very busy & hard to access due to the number of tourists milling around. Very easy to get to, photograph but is very quick to visit. Ok but nothing special.
Amazing architechure, but to crowded and busy. Do not forget to buy a ticket if you want to walk over the Japanese Bridget and not just take pictures.

Mildly Accessible Site This is a nice, picturesque spot. Like all old wooden bridges, it’s got that romantic feel to it. It’s limited in terms of things to do if you’re on a wheelchair, since the room in the middle is a tad impossible to get to with a powered wheelchair. Maybe with a little elbow grease and volunteers, manual wheelchairs might be able to pass through it. The entrances to the bridge is blocked off with wooden gates, but it’s easy to move/lift to let wheelchairs through. Just be careful not to get splinters, pretty sure tetanus is still a thing. Oh, and the bridge as standalone is open for anyone to use. The ticket is for the room in the middle, I think.
Travelling with large group - visited one of Hoi An's sightseeing spots ! A lovely meander through old town markets - Photo opportunity....
Really picturesque place to visit but the ticket collectors were on the abrupt side. Still worth a visit.
Interesting area and Bridge to visit. The bridge is in very good condition and has some very interesting history, we loved walking around this area.
Go EARLY or suffer the tourist crowds, and don't fall in that water. Then follow the water away from the river for 5 minutes to see more authentic local living around the water storage area

Beautiful bridge in the heart of town If you are visiting the old town then you can not really miss the historical Japanese Bridge. Just follow the river road from the market with the river on your left and you will come to it. An extremely popular place for tourists to take photographs. Avoid the crowds and visit the whole area in the morning as the after lunch Chinese coach tours descend on mass in the afternoon.

Picturesque bridge - but busy The bridge itself is very pretty, and has great historical value, being built by the Japanese. I found the best view was from the outside, inside the bridge was extremely busy with many tourists attempting to take selfies/photos.
nice place to wander past, lights up beautifully at night. I think you can go inside it to pray, but its lovely to photograph

This ancient bridge is situated in the old city. The architecture is very unique and beautiful. You can walk across and examine the inside more closely for a small fee. It is also beautiful to see at night because they light it.

One of the most iconic landmarks in the old town and a must see for visitors to stop and take photos. It is very much in the centre of the town and one will surely walk pass it while exploring the area.

Quick Dash over the Bridge Just a quick detour whilst visiting old town. You have to purchase a ticket. It’s just good to see the old way people connected two areas. Won’t take more than 10 mins but worth the stop.

An impressive icon of the city's old town is it's covered Japanese Bridge, dating to the 16th-17th century. Be patient, wait for the crowds to pass by and you will grab a good photo without the selfie army hanging off the side.

We were there on Saturday morning, it's already quite busy. We did came back the following day just to have a look around again and it's very busy. So we just passed and look from the other side. It's nice though.

This Japanese covered bridge brings significance in the history of Vietnam and do take a walk across this quaint bridge. There is even a small temple inside the very short bridge. Very quaint and full of history. Do both the night and day visit as they are both worth the walk which also links to ancient town

Not really an attraction since you're gonna walk through it if your in the area without even trying. It's on a bank note though, so be sure to grab the essential travel photo holding the note next to the real thing.

My little trip to Hoi An This is a small bridge to set the correct expectations, but there is a significant contrast to the buildings and shops either side of this bridge. I was quite impressed in the newer Japanese side where it was definitely more creative with more galleries to view.
I’m actually not sure if the story on this bridge as there were no English plaques anywhere. However, it’s a cool old bridge that looks very old.

Great architecture and associated history but a little over crowded to be able to really enjoy the location. That said and done I wouldn't have missed this historical piece of construction given the chance to do it again.

Really Japanese, these bridge If you visit this bridge you will already encounter an enormous crowds on the way there. To be able to cross the bridge you must have already bought your city pass for the old city. With this pass you can visit five sights in ancient town. If you would like a photo of the bridge, take it from the Japanese side, because it is a lot quieter on that side.

Worth a look as you wander the old town. An interesting piece of architecture which is popular for photos. Not that exciting overall but on the tourist loop. If you like history, photography and architecture make sure you see Precious Heritage by Rehahn and then seek out The Quiet American cafe/bar as well - the latter is a bit hard to find - don't be distracted by the name it's a stunning building with an interesting history and very photo worthy.

Japanese Bridge @ Ancient Town Hoi An The Japanese Bridge is the only covered bridge in the world to house a Buddhist temple, and was built in the 16th century as a symbol of goodwill between Chinese and Japanese merchant communities. Unlike most other buildings in the old town, which are decorated according to flamboyant Chinese and Vietnamese architectural styles, the Bridge has retained a distinctively Japanese character, with subdued ornamentation and references to Japanese history. Best time to visit is early morning between 5.30 am to 8.30 am if you fancy pics without any people in foreground.

Japanese Covered Bridge The Japanese bridge (Chùa Cầu ) is the most famous monument of Hoi An. The Japanese covered bridge , built in the 1590s by the Japanese community of Hoi An, to create a link with Chinese neighborhoods on the other side of the river, is a fine example of Japanese architecture. time. Over the centuries, the ornamentation of the bridge has remained relatively faithful to the original Japanese design. The French have leveled the road to make it more suited to their motor vehicles, but the original arched shape was restored during the major refurbishment work done in 1986. The bridge, recognized as a relic national cultural and historical site in 1990, is the symbol of the city. For those who stay in Hoi An, the best time to visit is very early in the morning because there is nobody. ..
Quaint little bridge built many years ago. Highlights the vast cultural changes that the region has experienced.
A lovely landmark to see, however if you want to see it in all its beauty, visit during the day when tourists are at a minimal and not at night when it is overcrowded and full of tourists!

Unique Japanese Architecture! We are going along with our central trip of Vietnam, the J. Bridge such an historical at architecture rarely left in this ancient town. Although missing out the morning view but still catching the evening time of the J Bridge. It says why tourist attractions here . It cost you 120k ~ $6 for each visitor ! So far so good Hoian !

Lovely old bridge between Chinese and Japanese sections of the old Town. The bridge is most often (and best) photographed from the river road. If you get too close you can't really get it all in a picture. And photography on the bridge isn't really anything special. It's worth crossing the bridge to the old Japanese side because it's much less crowded there and even feels cooler on those hot afternoons.
We stopped by to see the Japanese Bridge. It’s perfectly situated in the Old Town. Once you’re there, take some pictures before you pay for the ticket to walk across the bridge.

There is no need to use one of your 'tourist tickets' just to cross the bridge and see the small statues at either end, just if you choose to go into the side room (not worth a ticket). Obviously a photo spot, and an interesting part of the history of relations between Hoi An and Japan.
Was worth a look on the way to the night market and wondering around the old town shops and eateries
Old architecture and a good spot for a photo shot. You can stroll to this bridge within the Ancient City. There are many cafes and restaurants in the vicinity of the bridge
Just a covered bridge This covered bridge was nothing special. Some of the pictures you may find of it are from festivals or other special occasions.

An ancient covered bridge spanning a small tributary of the river, flanked by a pair of carved dogs at one end and carved monkeys at the other. Historically built by the Japanese but renovated numerous times. This is a symbol of the old town. One needs to get tickets to get into the old town to visit the various sites, but this lasts for a week, so it is worthwhile.

The Japanese Covered Bridge is the perfect symbol of the cultural mixture that became Hoi An. As a main entrance to Old Town, the bridge handles a lot of foot travel. Guarded at either end by monkey and dog gods, in the center of the bridge is a small pagoda. One side of the bridge is covered by the walls of the pagoda; so, only the riverside of the bridge provides a glimpse of the beautiful carving done by the Japanese traders when they created the bridge. The pagoda contains excellent photographs of earlier times in Hoi An. The late afternoon is the best time to photograph the outside of the bridge.

Enjoyed the bridge and also the cool and shaded shopping street beyond. Loved being able to browse the shops without being hassled to buy. The little temple on the bridge in interesting. Very crowded, but it was a holiday.

I visited the bridge during the day and didn’t find it be that impressive however another evening when I visited with friends it was far more impressive in its setting - surrounded by lit lanterns and candles which made it worth the visit.
Nic and looking beautiful so many crowded areas people are click photo Nice palace in hoian Japanese birch
Japanese Covered Bridge The Japanese Covered Bridge is a must see, when you visit Hoi An. It is a pretty place to visit both day and nighttime.
Although crowded when we went, the other couples agreed it was amazing to walk across this bridge. A must see if you ever visit Hoi An
Worth seeing if you are in the area. Interesting historical story associated with this bridge. Situated close to all other attractions in the old town.
I didn’t know the Japanese had a long history here Was a surprise to me that there were communities of Japanese merchants from hundreds of years ago. Enjoyed the sight, worth a visit for sure.
A Shady spot at the end of the markets very unusual and interesting. Great photo op and views from the bridge.
It's a bridge... They charge you to cross it. It's a bridge... Pretty nice but also pretty short!!

Bridge synonymous with Hoi An It is a landmark attraction in the town. It was made by the Japanese in 18th Century as a way to reach the Chinese quarter across the water. The Japanese architecture still remains intact. Today, the bridge has become synonymous with Hoi An. When entering from either side of the bridge, you will see dog and monkey statues on both sides believed to guard the bridge. There is a small temple on the bridge dedicated to the God of Weather, Tran Vo Bac De. The people pray here to fend off any approaching disaster. When you walk on the bridge, you can feel the vibrations as if it is going to break now…but, the historical piece remains intact. You need to show the entrance ticket when you enter the bridge. They cut one part (implying one attraction) from the main ticket.

Japanese Covered Bridge in Hoi An This is a pretty attractive piece of architecture in Hoi An's touristy yet very charming old Town area. There are plenty of scenic spots nearby and walking along the river in the evening was very pleasant.
Mate it’s a beautifully constructed historical monument which connects two pieces of land which are divided by a body of water.

A part of Vietnamese history The Japanese bridge dates back to the 18th century built to reach the Chinese quarter on the other side of the river. The bridge is guarded by 2 dog statues at one end and 2 monkey statues at the other. It's not the greatest attraction but it's fun to walk across the small covered bridge just to say you did it.

Significant and sturdy This small ancient, historical bridge is the attraction in the Hoi An Old Quarter. One has to pay to cross but I just viewed it and took photos from the exterior. However, something should be done about the stench from the river! It needs a good cleaning...
Great piece of history You will be charged to cross. It is always super busy but definitely a great piece of history. You can see it for free but staying outside of it.

Not “a bridge too expensive” Cost to see 5 old town sites including this sweet little bridge (using tickets bought a few metres away) is 120,000 i.e. £4 (80 pence per site). Not sure what price Geno from Michigan paid... There’s not all that much to see, but I got my 80 pence worth.

Historically significant bridge You couldn’t possibly come to the ancient town in Hoi An and miss the Japanese bridge. It’s lovely to walk across and also to view from the side. The bridge is smaller than I expected but it is an important landmark so I recommend you do not miss it because it is very beautiful.
Don't know if there is any time of day when there are less people here. Possibly early morning. It still is a very nice attraction.
A lot of people used to visit here who are in hoi An it's good place to visit and have some photo and a lot of fun recommend for everyone
Interesting and unexpected A small covered bridge in the centre of the old town was one of the five attractions we visited, not a lot to see but the history of the structure has to be admired
Nice to see and find out the history of the bridge. In fact the history of Vietnam itself is both fasinating. Looks nice at night time all lit up. Well worth a visit to the riverside in the evening

Crown jewel of Hoi An The Japanese bridge is the sight to see. Depending on the time of day it can be busy. You can cross it and visit the wee rooms on the bridge with old photos of the bridge in the past. It is one of the ticketed sights in Hoi An.
Good if you’re passing by to take a few quick snaps! Blends in well with the environment. Wouldn’t go out of my way to come see this but then I am not an avid lover of ancient architecture
We visited while wandering around the old town. We got some great photos but didn't go inside as we felt our cultural tickets could be put to good use. A must see but not to spend a lot of time at.

Charming but really crowded When we visited, the bridge was full of tourists--too many to get a decent photo or enjou the bridge. Cross the next pedestrian bridge over the canal and take your photos of the bridge from there.
Easy access to the Old Town. Good for a photo. Well preserved and worthy of paying the small amount for conservation, when using it.

Exterior is hood for photos Iconic tourist attraction that looks great in photos but you don’t need to walk across it or pay to visit the museum. Take your photo and walk across the nearby footbridge.
This is lovely old quaint bridge in the heart of the town. We stumbled upon it by chance and had a photo and enjoyed the architecture.

A must see when you visit Hoi An day or night very beautiful and very old and hard to avoid it’s presence in the Ancient Towns heritage and appeal To walk over it you must purchase a ticket A photographers delight
Ancient C14th Japanese wooden bridge guarded by two monkeys and small shrine.Interesting but a little underwhelmimg after so many stunning attractions of the ancient city.
Very old bridge, but well worth a visit and get the history of the bridge from a local perspective. Also wonderful walks by the canal and watch all the boats coming and going.
Caught some tour guides dressed up in traditional gear and was lucky to get a photo ( see attached). Looks great as a part of Hoi An.

Don't waste a ticket here! The bridge is only beautiful from the outside. On the inside it's just dark and small. It's not worth spending a ticket just to go across. It's better to cross the other bridge, where you have a nice view to the Japanese covered bridge. We did like the street that comes after you cross the bridge: it's quiet (as mopeds can't cross the bridge!) but you can always go around and get there anyway.

Visit early in the day Pretty cool attraction right in the heart of Hoi An. However, stop by early to visit and get pictures, since particularly in the evening it's shoulder-to-shoulder crowds around it.

If you are in Hoi An go and see the bridge The bridge links the town to the Chinese area and is steeped in history. We did this as part of a walking tour and it was interesting to hear about the statues on either side.
My wife and I visited the bridge while in Hoi An, you need a ticket to enter the bridge,and there are photos dating back in time of the bridge,and nice step back in history.

Slow down to take it in Visit but be patient. It was very crowded. Groups arrive in waves . It’s a good place to alternate studying cultural and architectural details and people. I was asked for a ancient town ticket. The area in front is pleasant.

Significant icon in old Hoi An town It’s just a bridge and nothing really special until you know the history behind the bridge. Disappointing that at certain times during the day you need to buy a ticket to enter or cross. It is often so crowded you need to wait 15 minutes to cross and there is no chance to get to the side to take a photo. Go early in the morning if you want a good photo
