Historically significant entrance Walking into the main entrance of the old town of Hoi An, you can’t miss the Japanese Bridge. It is an amazing structure and full of interesting points our guide instructed us about. Eyes above the doorways, the rooms by the sides etc. it is is however not that well maintained and of course in peak times extremely crowded. Go early morning for a slower pace. You can avoid it on other visits by going in on other streets. Worth seeing though and understanding the mixed race history of this trading town.
It's a bridge with a story behind it. Worth a visit since it can't be missed, but for most it will probably be a few minute visit.

The covered bridge might be a little overrated.. Its worth a visit if you are walking through town, but honestly it was so busy we could barely get a picture and the whole buying a ticket thing was strange.
Japanese covered bridge located in old town. Enjoy walking and buy local souvenior. Very beautiful historical of Japanese architecture.
I nice piece of history, and it worths a while to walk through it and feel the historical vibe. I didn’t pay the admission to enter the tiny museum though.
The bridge very beautiful and picturesque. The best time to walk on it though is early morning just after sunrise, to beat the crowd
Not only is the bridge worth the trip the little town is lovely. Lots of nice restaurants and shops. The downside is it is a bit crowded with tourists.
This bridge is a pretty way to go from Hoian town to the night market. It's worth a visit but only needs 10mins.
Very old bridge in Ancient Quarter This very old bridge is an iconic landmark of the Ancient Quarter of Hoi An. You can walk over and take photos looking out over the river. It can get very crowded!

This old wooden bridge of Japanese origin is one of many interesting buildings from past eras in the history of Hoi An. It is the gateway to what was a Japanese precinct in the old port city. It is one of the must see sites in the Old Town of Hoi An.
I’m not sure what the draw is to this old bridge that is much too crowded with people taking selfies and not paying attention to everything else around them.

You need a ticket to enter the Japanese bridge, in order to see what is inside. A small temple build inside the bridge. It might be just a short distance walk thru the bridge to the opposite side, but it's worth a walk to feel and look into the history that is left behind.

It is a kinda have to see whilst in Hoi An - grab your snap with the monkey and the dog. You will come across it while walking through old town just look for the maddening crowds. Most famous Japanese bridge in SE Asia. You'll see why Monet loved it. If stopping on the bridge to visit the small shrine to wish for good weather for your trip, you will need your ticket otherwise just walk through. Good selfie spot on the riverside looking back at the bridge. Very close to Gam to drop in for their legendary Vietnamese iced coffee in the well room hidden in the back. I hope you found this review helpful and enjoy your visit to Hoi An.
Combination of Cultures The Japanese Bridge was a beautiful structure that joined parts of the Ancient Town. It was certainly a place for everyone to take a special photo.
Well worth the short walk Lovely old covered bridge, beautiful. There is a small room in the center of the bridge. Worth a look inside.
Stumbled upon this bridge while exploring Hoi An! Perfect Instagram photo! Though beware a lot of tourist around trying to Get that perfect picture too!

While in Hoi An you have to see the bridge, but be aware that it can be very crowded. Nice to see at night when it is lit up and great for picture taking. Go across the river and eat at Mango Mango which has a great view of the bridge. Eat on the terrace upstairs overlooking the bridge.
The bridge over the river It’s a great place for orientation in the old town and on weekends fun to see all of there wedding photo shoots.
Great place to see and walk around, doesn’t take long to walk around but very picturesque. Go early to avoid tourists!
500 year old bridge over a feeder stream to the main river. Entry is 20k VND which allows an up vlose look at the temple within.
A remenant of the past. Bridge connecting the japanese and chinese quarters in the past. Having a small temple on the bridge itself. Interesting.
Nice because of the little temple Not big but still interesting. In the old center of Hoi An. The temple is interesting. You need a ticket but there a lot of free moments
More than 1000 year old Japanese bridge well preserved and a popular tourist attraction in HoiAn.. Worth a visit
There's no need to make a night of it, but well worth visiting and passing over, particularly after dark.
What can you say about a bridge, it was old and had an interesting history but nothing more than that.
Nice place but really crowded This bridge is a nice little gem in Hoi An, unfortunately also very popular, which makes it crowded at every times of the day I can by. But still worth the visit.

Overcrowded but beautiful The bridge is very beautiful but again, its hard to take a photo of it without others in it going fo a million selfies. You might just download a photo from the internat and stand there and enjoy the bridge :)

Amazing bridge and definitely worth seeing. Try coming earlier in the morning and beat the crowds. It was uncomfortably smoky from the incense and crowded the first day we were there. The tour buses and the hordes seem to arrive from about midday. So if you are staying locally, come early and get a good photo without lots of people crowding your lens.

Walking around you must cross it... but remember to pay the city entrance fee... The strory behind it is more interesting than the bridge itself although it is a must see in Hoi An. It is used by town officers to check whether tourists that want to cross it have paid the city entrance fee. Makes a nice backdrop for photos although most of the time the bridge and the area around it are pretty crowded... The small temple on the bridge consumes one of the entrance tickets from your city card...
We passed over a few times , can get really crowded as Korean and Chinese tourist come by the bus full on organised tours to see .
One of the main attractions in the old Town is the Japanese covered bridge, so beautiful! Right near the river with lots to do and see.
This is a wonderful walk though time. The feeling of timeless and history

This bridge is a testimony to the diverse cultural and ethnic heritage of the city. Beautiful by day or by night, it’s worth stopping by to take photos. It’s filled with tourists as it is the city’s architectural symbol. The fact that there is a temple attached to it is quite fascinating and unique in the world.

Choose the morning to visit it as it can be very crowded after 12am. Also, you won't have to pay. 20000VDN to cross the bridge. But this is one typical construction that you have to see to understand the ancient history of Vietnam, and especially of Hoi An.

It's interesting and makes a good picture. We did go in to walk through the little shrine but I wouldn't recommend that, not very interesting . View from outside and walking though the main bridge is nice though.

The bridge was smaller than I thought before. I suggested to come early in the morning to get better photo, with less ppl around. In the afternoon it gets so crowded. There is a little shrine inside the bridge that you have to pay if you want to visit. I am not sure whether you actually need to pay some fee or not to enter or even to walk across the bride. When I was there I didnt have to pay for the fee to get inside and walking across it, but when I left I heard the gate keeper asking some tourists to pay some fee when they wanted to enter the bridge.
Visited this beautiful bridge while touring Old Hoi An, it is a beautiful remnant of the old city with a very nice temple inside, the lights are night make it spark

The Japanese Covered Bridge is a must see attraction in Hoi An. Spanning a relatively small canal it is a symbol of the connection between Chinese and Japanese communities from centuries ago. Its construction is remarkably well-preserved with a later attached Temple of Chinese origin which is accessible to visitors.

Interesting but crowded It is an intriguing looking bridge, quite lovely in fact. Like much of Hoi An - quite pretty but teeming with tourists. Recommend visiting at different times (preferably early before the flood of tourists descend and also at night).
Astonishing Japanese architecture over the canal An ancient covered wooden bridge spanning the canal - fantastic place to take photographs of Old Town.

Not really an obvious "wow" but good to see. Shame the access is semi-ticketed. The fact it has lasted so long in a fairly harsh environment with lots of use speak volumes to its quality. Seems almost lost in the mass of visitors. Glad I have seen it, glad too that I did not go too far out of my way to see it

Just a small covered bridge which has been maintained over the years. Definitely worth a visit for the photo opportunity. Not sure if it is worth one of the 5 tickets issued by the local tourist office. Just to share on this 5 ticket matter : There are several old buildings, shops, homes, assembly offices, places of worship which can be visited. And access to these are through a ticket. The local tourist office sells 5 tickets in a lot, which is generally making tourist narrow down their choices to 5, unless they are willing to buy another set of 5 tickets. If I recall correctly it costs about $7 for the bunch of five.
The place is very beautiful and not crowded. Is located in the old town area and has an entry ticket requirement.

Buy the old town Hoi An pass and you get 5 attraction tickets that come with that , for 120,000 dong. You are contributing to a UNESCO world heritage site so it’s a good thing to do. Use one of the tickets at the Japanese bridge, it’s well preserved and has interesting artifacts and history inside. It will take you 5-10 minutes to look at thoroughly
Gorgeous place to be!! Need few days in here to see the whole place with no rush. Definitely recommend!

Gorgeous old bridge but the market around it is amazing The history of the 400 year old bridge was fascinating but the market leading up to it was very memorable and there were lots of friendly people ready to pose for a photo among the lanterns
Read up on the history before you go The way to really appreciate this short span is to understand a bit about the significance of the historical context before you go.

The Japanese Bridge is beautiful at night. I read people were charged to stand on the bridge. This was not the case for us. We saw it at night and we got to stand on the bridge and take pictures without any fee.
The bridge is small, very beautiful with all the work that was done. There’s crazy people on one end who scream at you saying you have to buy a ticket. Watch out

Historic bridge worth a quick visit This fabulous Japanese bridge is the only one of its kind, with its unique room built on the side if the bridge. Built to provide access for the Japanese to trade with the other side of the waterway, the small bridge was built in the 1560s and is still in excellent condition. Worth a quick visit!

This little bridge has been around for hundreds of years. There are dogs and monkeys guarding each end, and a little temple in the middle. The most interesting thing are the marks from the flooding that periodically inundated the old town (submerging just about everything.) Nice place for a picture.

At the end of the main drag through the Old Town There always appears to be some discrepancies between the responses to the question ‘how old is this’ and ‘is this it’s original state or has it been rebuilt’ but one truth appears to be that the Japanese traders built this. Actually attractive and interesting piece of the history jigsaw of the Old Town Needs a ticket so don’t try to simply walk into it. Watch out for the marks which show how high flood waters have reached over the years - recent years in particular. Not unusual for the path alongside the water front to flood (did while we were there after constant rain.)
It was good to see and I really enjoyed the outside architecture- inside was busy and not too much to see. But overall enjoyable experience.
Nice spot in old town Cool bridge. Swarming with tourists but great spot for a pic. Easy to find in the little town. It’s more of a place to stop by as you wander the old town
Yes I've been to Japan many times as we have our immediate family there and the construction of this bridge is 100% Japanese and a must see
Which you might visit as one of five choosen sightseens for one ticket. Look better outside, than inside, from my point of view.
You have to cross to the other side of the bridge to explore more about the ancient town. Stay longer and you will see how beautiful the atmosphere is when it is lit up at night.

Beautiful Ancient Structure Japanese Bridge is probably the most photographed spot in town so expect it to be crowded - all the time. However, I went for a run nice and early one day and had it all to myself. It's apparently the only covered bridge in the world with a Buddhist temple. You have to pay to go into the temple - but only have a ticket in hand to go over the Japanese Bridge. Apparently, the French straightened the road-bed so that cars could more easily cross but it was restored to the graceful arch after the French era. It really is beautiful.

It's right there in the old town and something you should see. This is "just" a bridge but it tells a lot about the history of the city and how the Japanese influenced the development and merchant life. Also its quite remarkable and beautiful to look at
Interesting wooden bridge. However, it’s very crowded with all tourists. Need to purchase ticket to cross over.

Beautiful Ancient Architecture Ancient Architecture of the bridge is wonderful. It was started in the year of the monkey and ended in the year of the dog. 16th to 18th century. (Both have statues inside the bridge) It connects the Japanese side to the Chinese side. Keep in mind it's very crowded with tourists and there is a small entrance fee. (It's used to maintain the bridge, so it's utilized well) Keep the ticket with you, as ticket checkers are around many entrances and exits.

The Rialto Bridge of Hoi An It is nice to understand a little bit of the history behind the bridge. Plenty of photo opportunities from all sides. Worthwhile to check out all the detail work inside the bridge and the 2 little altars. From there you can go into the old part of the city. Nicely illuminated at night.

Nothing fancy and really just a covered bridge spanning a waterway. What is interesting is its age and construction. You have to pay to walk through it but you can bypass it by using another free bridge just down the road. Given the number of tourists jammed in the bridge the money we paid was wasted as you could not enjoy any space. Came back the next afternoon and there was very few tourists around. Saw the bridge from outside on both sides of the waterway and enjoyed it.
Rich in history, beautiful old bridge, seems stable enough when tons of people are on at the same time. Beautiful carvings.

Very crowded at times......the main thoroughfare for town folk crossing the waterway. Easy to miss the historical significance of the bridge. For a small charge you can go into the small temple complex on site, dedicated to the early Japanese residents and their plight.
Pleasant walk throughthe old town of Hoi An After a pleasant walk through the old town of Hoi An you will reach the bridge. We did not visit the museum (Lots of tours).

Lovely old bridge across a stream towards the end of town, made in a Japanese style. You need a ticket to cross the bridge, which is always busy. Inside the bridge, there is a couple of rooms off to one side in the middle. There is another bridge close to the Thu Bon river, from quickly can get a great view of the the Japanese Covered bridge.

Didn't get a really good look because so many people inside and outside I'm sure the Japanese Covered Bridge is a structure that should be admired. Unfortunately there were hundreds of other tourists who were trying to do the same thing at the same time as us. And those were in the minority because the hundreds of others were there just to take photos to be posted on social media so they could prove that then had been there......

Crowded entry with ticket only. While I totally appreciated the history and ancient architecture of this bridge -- you can appreciate it externally without necessarily buying a ticket to walk inside. However - the ticket money IS used to maintain it so - even donation wise - you should do it.

Japanese Covered Bridge Japanese Covered Bridge is hard to miss. It is in the center of the shopping district. When you walk through, you will see a prayer room inside. It is a very crowded area. Hard to take pictures.
Nice - Nothing special You will cover this as part of your walk around old town - Hoi An. Good for a photograph and move on.

I’m sure the historical importance elevates the attraction. Great photos if you get up early in the AM and approach from the main river side. Not really a “can’t miss” attraction. Otherwise it functioned perfectly in the task of moving from one side of the ravine to the other.
A beautiful historical bridge A must see in the old town of Hoi An. Good photo ops from outside on the road than on the bridge itself.

Japanese Bridge in Hoi An The Japanese Bridge was constructed by the Japanese community to combine it to the Chinese part of Hoi An. Is was covered to protect people from sun and rain. You can cross the bridge free of charge. In some hours of the day the bridge is overcrowded, so I will recommend you to come there in the morning. Then you can better enjoy this beautiful and romantic bridge.

Cycled to the ancient town to see the bridge. Tried crossing and was told we need a ticket. We walked around it instead of through it. Overheard a local guide tell someone you only need a ticket to see the shrine but the local officials like to prey on tourists and ask them to buy tickets. We didn't bother with tickets and just had a look from the outside. Sure it's nice to look at and it's a part of history but that's about it.

Don’t need to pay for ticket if not visiting temple If you visit the Japanese Covered Bridge in the morning or early afternoon, there will be guards who will ask for an entrance fee. If you are not planning to visit the temples and such and simply want to cross the bridge, just walk around it. In the evening there doesn’t seem to be a guard so people can just walk through freely. A picturesque bridge worth seeing

Lovely japanese chinese bridge Lovely bridge build to connect two sides that are guarded by the dog and monkey gods on each site. The bridge is a short one with a small temple inside. You are able to purchase your zodiac lucky sign for usd1. One thing interesting is that onw of the zodiac sign is "cat".

Charming bridge in town I love all place what this town have, just the ticket store they didnt give us information that much, one ticket for 5 places and in the town there are more than 5 places so if they provided more information that would be great and perfect

Historically significant But currently a Photo Opportunity with a Fees A gift from Japanese to Vietnam dating back to 1590 (restored in 1986) ,this covered bridge is now a photo opportunity for the large number of tourists visiting the area. You have to pay an entrance fee to cross over. However since we visited early morning during our walk in Hoi An Ancient town , we did not have to. It will be criminal not to take photos of the bridge or on the bridge while in Hoi An -so we would recommend you to do it anyways
This is literally walking over history. A gift from Japan to Hoi An. Ancient pictures, the wood, the sight... it is a must see/walk.
Nice piece of history Enjoyed our visit to the bridge during our walking tour. Good to have someone to explain the history and meaning of the bridge to you.
Great entrance to the oldest part of the town. Still looks original and wasn't to crowded when we went.

Nice way to spend some time You need to buy a ticket for this attraction. It’s nice to hear the history of it and on one side of the bridge it is a lot quieter and the markets are cheaper due to less people traffic. We were there in August and the only downfall was there was a stench coming from the water as we walked along.
Pretty, historical but no room to swing a cat. I guess you have to see it, but early in the morning is best to avoid the hordes of tourists later in the day. A photo.op and move on is my advice.

AKA Cau Chua Pagoda Bridge . A nice 17th century wooden bridge, one of iconic symbols of Hoi An . Lots of tourists around , all day , trying to take a photo/selfie , so you really have to hope to find an empty spot for a brief minute , so you can use your camera too . (Or better take shots of other people taking shots of other people taking shots etc.......) . Tickets .
We thought it was interesting to look at. I read all the info cards The water really smelt- maybe it needs more frequent flushing?

Definitely visit if you’re in the area A lovely example of Japanese design in Vietnamese streets. If you read the history from the guide books, the Japanese covered bridge is definitely worth a trip to see if you’re already in Hoi An. The Japanese Bridge forms part of the old town and, as a whole, the area is amazing. You’ll only spend 15 minutes here reading the history and taking photos but for the price of a ticket (as part of the Old Town pass) you should really head to see it.

Went to the bridge and had look around. Beautifully designed and maintained. However, gets so busy with Chinese tourists and wedding parties, especially in evenings, that’s it’s difficult to have any kjnd of moment on the bridge for yourself or a photo opportunity. Go earlier in the day to avoid the crowds,
It is extremely popular and famous Japanese Covered Bridge. Ideal for photography, surrounded by beautiful Vietnamese buildings.
Nice place and worth a visit You cannot miss this bridge while walking at the old city. Lots of history and charm and also money paid to cross the bridge. Definetly worth a visit!

Hoi An ancient town - japanese bridge Definitely go in the evening but check the hours as attractions close about 5-6 pm. We got confused a little bit as you have to by a ticket to continue walking along the streets of Hoi An. This little town is just amazing. Wish could stay longer.. Go as per map provided and enter with tickets to chapels and little shops - you will find out a lot of interesting facts
We visited the bridge as part of our walking tour and it was interesting to hear its history and walk over it.
One of the popular sites within ancient town A bridge with it's historical significance. Recommend to visit the site before the group tours occupy the bridge in the noon.

The Japanese Covered Bridge is just one of the many attractions in the Old Town. We didn’t buy a ticket, which meant we couldn’t cross the bridge but we walked up to it and then found a road that leads around behind it and brings you out on the other side.

The short bridge offers a scenic view of the river and a large part of the ancient village of Hoi An in Danang. The night view is great. Both ends are flanked by deities and in the middle of the bridge there is a small ancient temple. Very nostalgic!

Impressively old structure that screams "History" If you are walking around in old town Hoi An, you can't miss walking over, or walking by this bridge. Just be sure to stay aware of its location, so you won't "miss" noticing it as you move along with the pedestrian flow. Depending on the time of day, it could be really crowded, or you could have a chance to snap a tranquil deserted scene. Personally, it is not a place to put on a "must see bucket list", but if you find yourself already in Hoi An, and you need to wait more than 30 minutes for your table at the restaurant you picked for your meal, take a casual walk to touch a piece of history.

Small, nice, go at night It's right at the Centers of old town activity. Go at about 1900. Nothing much to do on the bridge itself but it's a good point to aim to which brings you to the nice area. There is history to the bridge, we did not explore.
Hard to not go here if you visit Hoi An. Quite small but you do get a sense of the history when you walk through. You will need to pay but a pretty nominal amount and the kids get in free.
Impressive if limited. This is very small and seemingly always busy but it’s an impressive structure nonetheless and deserves its iconic status within Hoi An.

Passed the Japanese bridge in our explorations of the old town & tailors. Would say it was one of our favoured spots as it was calm, relaxed & the shop clerks were not as fussy on the other side of the bridge; which resulted in us purchasing more goods. Spent a good deal of time here
it is a cool place/design and I think if you are around that area you should pay the old bridge a little visit be sure to bring your camera 📷

Dont think you would miss out much, unless you are deep into historical sights. I walked in through a couple of times without a ticket and they dint seem to mind. But tourists who looked confused or new seemed to be pushed for a ticket and was restricted entry.

If you can get past the overtourism that has besieged Hoi An, I see no point in walking the streets of the Old Town without visiting this major attraction. Yes, the Japanese Covered Bridge is small and, like everywhere in the vicinity, it's usually overcrowded with tourists. Although we saw all kinds of temples, palaces, shrines, assembly halls, and other cultural structures while touring Indochina, this 16th century gem was a unique historical marker in Central Vietnam. In my book, that made it worth the minor entrance fee and the obligatory wait for other photo-snapping tourists to get out of the way of my camera! :-)

Nice old bridge with a nice view The bridge is nice to look at. But the water under it smelled very badly when we were there. I think there is a little museum inside the bridge, I saw some people selling tickets during daytime. But to cross the bridge you don't need a ticket ;-) The bridge is all the moments we were there very crowded with tourists. But that's with the whole old centre actually...

Small but unique bridge Its a beautifully formed little bridge. Look out for the statues of the monkeys and dogs at either end that guard the bridge. The water does look rather stagnant and filthy which is a shame.

What a magnificent historic place to see with your own eyes. I couldn’t believe the beauty of it at first when we walked up to it. The Japanese Covered Bridge in Hoi An is an iconic cultural and architectural landmark of the ancient town. Built in the late 16th century by Japanese merchants, the bridge showcases a blend of Japanese, Vietnamese, and Chinese architectural styles. Spanning approximately 18 meters over a small canal that flows into the Hoai River, the bridge creates a picturesque and historic setting. Inside, there is a small temple dedicated to Tran Vo Bac De, a deity believed to bring protection and good fortune. More than just a tourist attraction, the Japanese Covered Bridge symbolizes Hoi An’s rich heritage and the harmonious fusion of Asian cultures.

A true symbol of Hôi An! The pretty "Japanese Covered Bridge" has been there for centuries and even the most accomplished Vietnamese scholars are uncertain about its precise year of construction, but estimate that it was probably built around the 1590s. In ancient Hôi An, the Japanese and Chinese quarters were separated by a canal in the "The Bon River". So, Japanese businessmen decided to build a bridge connecting "Nguyen Thi Minh Kai Road" (on the Japanese Quarter - West Side) to "Tran Phu Road" (on the Chinese Quarter - East Side). As legends tell, its construction started in the "Year of Dog" and was finished in the "Year of the Monkey". Therefore, statues of two dogs were placed at one end and of two monkeys, at the other end. Inside the bridge, "Tran Vo Bac De", a Taoism deity - the "God of Weather", is enshrined. More than a bridge, it is a symbol of Hôi An and represents the fusion of the various cultures found this city over the past centuries. A visit is a must and may "Tran Vo Bac De" bless your journey in Hôi An, a Unesco appointed "World Heritage Site" since 1999! Enjoy!

this beautiful bridge is the symbol of Hoi An,it was constructed in the 1590s by the Japanese community to link it with the Chinese quarters.some says that construction of the bridge started in the year of the monkey and was finished in the year of the dog. that's why you can see at the one side of entrance, there is a pair of monkeys, and on the other side of entrance, there is a pair of dogs,so dont miss them, they are worshipped by locals now,like the gods.There is a temple on the bridge,it pays tribute to the Taoist God of weather, Tran Bo Bac De.and inside the temple,there are some historical photos on the wall,dont miss it.i bought a luck coin there.the coin carved with 12 animals images,it is based on vietnamese zodiac(no rabbit but cat instead).the surroundings of the bridge is beautiful,you can take many pics here,also there are some food stalls and cafes around,you can have some light meals if you feel hungry. at night, the bridge looks more charming,i visited here last year in september, just one day before the mid-autumn festival, very attractive, you can see many lanterns around this area, so better come here twice.
A really iconic landmark, small, ancient, pretty and quaint. It is a link to the Chinese and Japanese quarters of the town. Built in the 1500's it has been lovingly restored .

See This Attraction Right Now. Awesome historical site. It is free to enter and roam around. Now not many Chinese or Korean tourists. Which is so refreshing. So it is easy to explore. Beautiful place to visit during the day. I highly recommend.

We got there early enough that it wasn't too crowded. The little "museum" on it was a bit crowded and very HOT inside. Great views of it and from it. Loved that I got a good shot of a bride and groom having their photos taken.
Go early in the morning to avoid the crowds and get better photos. Very traditional original bridge that has withstood the hands of time.
Must see if staying in hoi an. Discovered it one day on a leisurely cycle of the old town. Lots of tourists in on day trips from buses. Nice vibe up around here.
nice place symbol hoi an lovely time walking on the bridge in hoi an city!! I like to take photo and go on bridge, history bridge my guide mr.loc ( mrluckybuddytours) nice recommend

The best example of Vietnamese-Japanese cooperation Japanese Covered Bridge is a landmark of the Old City of Hoi An. It is probably the best example of Vietnamese-Japanese cooperation and a mark of the olden days of the Japanese merchants who made Hoi An their home. It has a temple inside and a stairway connecting people from the entrance of the Old City to the other side of it. Of course, at night it comes to life and every tourist wants to have some photos with it.
Steeped in history and character, this bridge is a must visit. Perfect atmosphere and great views of the bridge from the old town

Quintessentially Hoi An, we were lucky to be visiting Hoi An during a downturn to tourist numbers and such we were able to enjoy this special building without the tourist numbers. Got some wonderful photos to treasure of our visit.
The picture of the bridge is in the 20000 $ dong. Really is a must see in hoi an. A bridge between the Chinese and Japanese in Vietnam

Beautiful wooden bridge This is a pretty wooden covered bridge built by the Japanese. There is a small room in the centre with an altar. At one end there are monkey statues and at the other dog statues to denote the 3 years it took to construct.

Japanese Covered Bridge in Hoi An, Vietnam Visited the Japanese Covered Bridge and was amazed at the design and structure of this wooden bridge - still in use - built in the 18th century. There is an interesting temple dedicated to the Taoist God of weather where one may pray to stave off impending earthquakes or such bad weather. There are water marks inside the temple that mark how high the waters reached in historical times of great floods.
Deffinitely worth buying the ticket to see 5 sites in the Old Town, the Japenese Covered Bridge being one of them. It doesn't take long to walk round but was very interesting.
Beautiful Japanese bridge A small but well preserved Japanese’s bridge in the heart of old town, great to see day or night, but quieter during the day, popular photo stop.
This superb bridge is a real magnet for tourist and a must see in beautiful Hoi An We walked over it on several occasions and took photos both inside and outside

To cross the bridge you have to buy the city ticket, although we did walk over it a few days before without having yet bought our ticket. It is interesting to see, if you appreciate architecture and out the back is a rickety floorboarded museum. It only takes about 15 mins to view, and is good to see at night, if you can see through the crowds. It is something local to Hoi An and a way to escape the same same same of the markets.

Interesting and historic - Get a ticket to cross during the day Firstly you need to get a ticket to walk over the bridge. They are available in books of 5 each which also permits you to venture into some Historic buildings (available from the ticket office, but I am sure you can get them elsewhere). Not a lot to see but worth walking over during the day - Free at night . The costs help maintain areas of historic importance
Nice and pretty much original. There is a small ante-room at the back which you need a ticket to get in (the ticket office is just there).

Came across the Japanese bridge by chance and it is very nice. Beautifully decorated and well kept with a very old purpose. Infrastructure; connecting the Japanese community with the locals to help trade.

Icon of Hoi An: Small Yet Special Constructed in the 1600's, the Japanese Covered Bridge was built to connect the Japanese and Chinese quarters of the city in a gesture of peace, by spanning the narrow canal below. The interior of the bridge contains a small shrine and other details. The carvings and architectural details on the exterior are worth a stop to absorb their beauty. In Vietnamese: "Cau Nhat Ban" is shorter than I expected, yet understandably the icon of Hoi An. Visitors are said to be limited to 20 at a time to protect the integrity of the structure, although the numbers seemed far greater when we initially crossed through. Best viewed from the side exterior at canal's edge, this is a good location to get the perfect photo.
This was an exception to the generalized over commercialization of Hoi An. Very scenic and interesting.
Beautiful landscape, a must see in the historic center of Hoi An. Plenty of shopping and street vendors surround the area, colorful lanterns hanging everywhere.
One of highlights at Hoi An This old bridge was built long ime ago and is under good maintenance. It is unique and has it own story. Visitor should not miss it if you are in Hoi An.

Such a nice bridge! You can actually enjoy the full impact from the outside more than from the inside, but there is a lovely “altar” inside when they “bong” when a donation is received...pretty little thing!
This is a must if you are in Hoi An. Lovely old bridge and you can visit the old house, fee required, from it

This bridge is impressive. I love taking pictures and the bridge has a soul to be captured! I would recommended you to do your activities at daytime around 3pm - 5pm, it's historical, well preservation of the locals to the bridge, wiki to get to know the bridge for more information about it.

Username: An Bang Sea Adventure Homestay Hoi An
Comment: Local people still keep ancient and clean in this place. The water or river through the bridge is very clean . We really love the pictures of Bridge . So we print and set up in the house
The bridge with a ridge , a little hill gave me a chill, thinking of the thousand feet to have passed , seeing and touching is a memory that will last. But
Whilst walking the town we came across this lovely old little bridge. It's lovely. Worth looking out for.
Nice experience. Unique highlight of Hoi An. Best time to visit early morning, less visitors. Beautiful time around 4-5pm because sunlight, but beware very crowded around that time with Asian tourist
This bridge is a highlight of Hoi An. Good to see that bikes and motorized vehicles are banned. So precious—I felt like I could hear the conversations of yesteryears.
A beautiful old covered bridge that really is set apart in old town. One of the old and beautiful parts of history. Great pictures.
A historical monument and a must see for all the tourist in the area. Good luck trying to get a photo of just the bridge.
The Centre of Hoi An Old Town Visible from everywhere, this landmark signifies the centre of the Old Town and joins the Japanese and Chinese sides of town.

ONE OF PRETTIEST TOWNS I HAVE EVER VISITED The old town of Hoi An, by the river is one of the most magical places I have visited. I could have spent days walking this area. I realized, as well, that the only reason this town has been kept so intact historically is that it was one of the very few Vietnamese town not bomber to smithereens by the American B52s. Thank God!

What a beautiful spot in Hoi An! The bridge is very ancient with much history built in 1593 and finished in 1595, The architecture is very pretty and worth a visit. It is lovely in both daylight and at night with perfect lighting. There is a temple inside the bridge...walking bridge.
A very photogenic bridge both at day and night time. We crossed it many times. Take pictures and enjoy it. Do not miss it!
Wonderful place to visit. Don’t miss it. We loved the lanterns and the old town. Hoi An is beautiful at night.
The history we learnt not only about the bridge but Viet Nam was interesting and knowledgeable will definitely come back
This is a cute little bridge that's worth visiting if you are in old town. It's normally packed with people!

Impressed for us, Japanese! It was New Year's Day and was crowded. The fact it was built over 400 years ago was so impressed us. A board of the name of this bridge in Chinese character was hung. “Came Far Bridge“ was written. Old Japanese were sure to realize that here was far from Japan. It took about 5 hours by plane now, but in old days it took for a long time to come here by ship. How hard it was! This bridge looked strong and looked like Nihon-Basi (it means Japanese-Bridge, one of the famous bridges in Tokyo). I wondered if it reminded Japanese at that time of their mother country.

Wonderful 18th century architecture We walked across the bridge enroute to the old town and found the construction solid and architecture fascinating. Well worth stopping to look inside at the turn of the century and 1950’s photos of the bridge
I loved seeing the construction of this wonderful little bridge. We were there early in the morning before the huge crowds were about. It is also fantastic to see at night with all the lantern lights
Definitely a must when you are in Hoi An, everybody needs to take a picture here. You have to pay an entrance fee during the day, in the evening it was for free. Go there and take a picture!

Very crowded night market where you need to have gut to bargain. Any stuff other than food, to start with at least 50# of the value. You may get if you do not loose your stand. There is an evening show nearby river at Japanese bridge. It basically shows Vietnamese olden way of life as well as different attires show. It is not to be missed if you visit night market at Hai Lon.
Lovely bridge with good history Gets very busy at times Weekend or morning seems to be quite for some reason

Very pretty old bridge in the tourist area of the old town. It is historic, free, unless you want to see the small museum attached. That could be seen using one of the attached coupons on your ticket to old town. The museum was small but interesting. If you are walking around old town, you definitely want to at least stand on this bridge.

A complete fairy tale This Japanese bridge gives a feel of a fairy tale in the middle of the market street is this bridge for which one has to purchase a ticket if one needs to enter it. It has a small prayer place too. It’s a very cute thing bridge worth passing thru
Hoi An ancient town is particularly spectacular and no amount of time would be enough to experience what they have to offer. So worth it

So many walked here before you! Get a feel aof ancientness! This bridge heaves with old stories. Enjoy it with a sense of curiosity! Hope you are the type that loves tasting your food and savoring every morsel, because that is how you should enjoy this ancient site. It is fascinating to look so far into the past!

Great photo opportunity Lovely old bridge. In the day time you need a ticket to cross but in the evening you don’t. That’s also the case with some of the other historical places in town but do buy a book of tickets to help them preserve these wonderful historical monuments.
Short and sweet, but is a beautiful bridge and lots of people take pictures of it. You are meant to have a ticket, but no one checked it.
We have seen the bridge last june when it was so busy in Hoi An. Now in low season it's way more relaxed to watch and walk over.
Highly recommended this site for pictures and education! I love to walk to the back of this bridge where seeing local scene!
A small traditional bridge. Crowded. But must see. It's near the Hoi an market. Lots of eating and shopping options nearby

We'd bought a "visit your choice of 5 historic buildings out of a list of 10" ticket from a small tourism office, (very friendly and helpful by the way), and the Japanese Bridge was a lovely destination. We were only there for 10mins, but we read every info sign and visited the 3 or 4 little rooms. So interesting and iconic.
The beautiful Japanese covered bridge is one a kind and worth taking time to view and take in the history.
A must see while in Hoi An steeped in history , doesn’t take long to visit but does get very crowded.

20,000 VND site.....well worth it To be on a note is saying something- well this iconic vision is found on the 20,000 VND note and is well deserved. The history is great and the 2 iconic animals - the monkey and dog greet you at both end of the bridge. Only trouble may be finding the time to grab all those goodies.....
This is an interesting bridge, lots of historical information. Get there as early as possible as it gets very crowded.

It divided the Chinese dwellings from the Japanese. A small, intricately carved foot bridge with a Buddhist temple on the side. It is in the heart of Hoi An city, the third largest in Vietnam and in my opinion, the most beautiful. Known for its instant services, ie, tailoring, footwear and what have you, the city has a lot more to offer. The markets are humungous and you can get some great bargains. Best of all, almost every thing is made/ crafted in Vietnam, it is in Hanoi that you need to weed out Chinese fakes.

First thing first, way thousands of people visit here even if they have to but a ticket. People try to take at most photo as possible and no place to set foot on. However, it is one of the signature symbol of Hoi An, you must visit there.
It's not a big bridge but the history and feel are worth it (not too mention the architecture and smell of time)

Hoi An is great for history geeks like me and this little bridge is stunning just to experience for a few fleeting moments. Its in the centre of the hustle and bustle but that just makes it more a part of its own history.

It is difficult to miss this. Right at the centre of How An. It is majestic indeed. Try be there at sunrise when there are less people around and the light in a good day is just right to see the true beauty of the structure
This place in Hoi An is well worth a visit as it’s hard not to be impressed by a structure that is over 500 years old and still standing!

Yes, it’s very worthwhile As you can see, I can’t understand how anyone could not be impressed with a 15th Japanese Covered Bridge in Vietnam. Also remember, when was the last time you saw one incorporate a pagoda?

Set in a beautiful part of the old town which is perfect for walking through. The bridge has a long history and at night looks very attractive. Think you have to pay to cross during the day. The river underneath could do with a bit of a clean up but despite that it’s very popular with photographers.
Beautiful historical bridge Certainly worth looking at whilst in Hoi An. Located in ancient town near all restaurants and river.

Japanese covered bridge in Ancient town Hoi An,you may be required to purchase ticket to walk through the bridge depending on what time you visit ticket purchase also gives you access to some other ancient temples and sites in ancient town.
Lovely little old bridge a great photo spot in this charming old town - is a good short cut to the slightly quieter part for shopping
Absolutly must see at Hoi An. Crowded place but it is natural if so many people want to see something unique.

Japanese Bridge photo stop Historic Japanese Covered Bridge with a temple to visit in its centre. Architecturally significant piece of history in the heart of the Old Town. It does get very busy with tourists trying to take photos but it is open all day and evening. The temple inside shows some of the history too. There is a walking bridge that you can cross if you want to miss the throng of tourists and it’s also a great place to take photos of the bridge from. Definitely worth checking out while you’re walking through town

Traditional Japanese architecture in mid of Vietnam When in the old town of Hoi An you will walk through the Japanese bridge. Try to also be there in the evening when lights are also bring another angel to look at it.
Great piece of history Very touristy but find out the story behind the bridge. It’s very interesting. Good photo opportunity. Right in the heart of the old town so very easy to find

Iwent here with my special friend. we came here to do travel and meet special singer whom we were very admired. I think that Hoi An is very beautiful places to live, to love and make beautiful memories with people. If you go to Vietnam, please go there.
The place is in the middle of town and is a magnet for lots of people to see, view and take photos of.

Visited Hoi An with my wife and we loved spending time in the Old Town, the covered Dragon Bridge and the lamps in the streets were on my wife’s ‘to do’ list while there and it didn’t disappoint. What a beautiful place! You can buy a book of tickets from the old town which allows you access to most of the attractions that you would usually have to pay to visit. The street food is incredible (actually all the food in Vietnam is incredible), happy hours in the bars is amazing and seeing the river with its lanterns is beautiful.
So pretty. Very popular spot for taking photos and a excellent backdrop for when you are dressed in the traditional fashion
Beautiful ancient architecture. Easy to do for all ages. Inside it gives you the history of the bridge.
The Japanese covered bridge is a must see if you visit Hoi An. It’s right in the middle of old town and is also on the 20,000 VND note!
This is a very old and interesting bridge we were lucky that it was not that busy when we visited it
This is the first time I come to Hoi An and my friend recommend visit Japanese Bridge and feel great😬

Hoi An iconic location Fascinating bridge from the 1700's which linked the Japanese and Chinese parts of the two. It is fun to photograph inside and outside of the bridge and there are some wonderful sculptures of two dogs and two monkeys. Don't miss it.

It’s only a small bridge but it is a must see at night time. It’s an old ancient bridge that a lot of people are on and around at night time but the whole ancient town is very busy at night time after 430-5pm

Not quite sure what the fuss is here. Yes, it's a bridge that isn't that big and goes from one point too the other. Yes, it's cute if you're into cute, but apart from that there were a lot of people taking photos so maybe this guy missed the whole point. But hey if you're into bridges go for it!
Wonderful sight of old world charm, recommend visit to this ancient town.ride around on bikes or simply walk a visit inside these homes is a must
Japanese Covered Bridge Enjoyed walking across and through this bridge, had problems trying to get a decent photo because of all the wedding parties lining up for their shots!
Stunning ancient bridge Beautiful especially at sunset - great for photo's. Wonderful to see these ancient teak homes in the Ancient Town.

" hope this bridge is saved," There isn't much to see with this bridge.. But when you consider its age, and the way it was constructed, in wood, it has stood the test of time, and let's hope the the many thousands of tourists that visit it while in Hoi An, dont eventually cause its demise.. The local authority has now put in some kind of fee too walk over it, but you can admire its splendour just by standing to the side and take pictures.. So worth seeing if you're visiting Hoi An. Not hard too find it.. Walk along the river with the old town to your right, walk through the market area , where fish, vegetables etc are sold. Keep walking and follow to the end, follow around corner and bridge is there.

We visited the bridge in the evening time, what a sight to behold. Lit up, the bridge really is a marvel to look at. It was very busy with lots of people taking photos and walking along the bridge but that took nothing away from its beauty. A must see if in hoi an.

The bridge is covered with a roof, reason being it also housed a temple and with deities worshiped by the early Japanese settlement. It is said the the bridge separated the Chinese settlers on the other side of the bridge.
It is most popular for Kodak moment. It is very ancient bridge to connect Japanese traders on one side and Chinese traders on the other.
The Japanese bridge is well worth visiting, we went with a personal guide, who was able to tell us in English its history. How well they built things even so long ago.

Japanese covered bridge dates back to the 18th century and is a beautiful historical piece of Japanese architecture. It is one of Vietnam's most iconic attractions in Hoi An. It is said that it was built by the Japanese then living in Hoi an as a way to reach the Chinese quarter across the water. The entrances to the bridge are guarded by weathered statues: a pair of monkeys on one side, a pair of dogs on the other. This is a nice place to visit.

It was so interesting to walk across this old bridge. The cooperation between the Japanese and Vietnamese is apparent in the architecture of the bridge and surrounding buildings. There is a little temple room that’s part of it also. Very humbling to be on something with so much history. It won’t take much time out of your day to visit, so you may as well!
Get up early and go at 6am. You’ll have it to yourself, it’s beautiful in the morning light without the crowds. Very serene. At night it’s just a mess of tourists.

Beautiful piece of history Visit early in the morning if you can to enjoy this charming historic bridge before the crowds arrive. The only other visitor there on the morning kindly took my photo. Remember to leave a donation.
Worth seeking out. Amazing to have stood the test of time. Has a few old photos which are worth looking at.
This is fascinating great for day and night pictures but very busy. You need to go into Hoi An old town to visit this.

I can't believe it has taken me 57 years to visit this amazing bridge. By far and away the best Japanese Covered Bridge I have ever traversed. My wife and I had only just crossed the bridge when damn it... We crossed it again. Lol. What can one say about a bridge. Lovely Bridge. Seriously.
Interesting little bridge that looks nice at night. Well preserved and definitely a piece of history that needs to be taken care of. Less people during the day.
Worth visiting. Go to the street on the other side to see some beautiful buildings. It was quieter and no traffic which made for a pleasant walk.
An ancient monument in the centre of hoi an. The crowds are big but what you would expect. Be patient and you will get that photo

The night market at Japanese is a must visit when in Hoi An. The whole street is full of vendors selling varied items like food, jewelery, clothes, souvenirs. There are many small cafes along the river. The market is lit up with lanterns wherever you look. We took many photos of the street with lanterns in background. And we also did boat-ride which was very calming. After that we did a bit of shopping (try little haggling, it works). Ended our night with some pizzas and beer at a cafe near the bridge. One of our most memorable experience while in Vietnam
Intricate craftsmanship! This wooden structure represents Japan on one side and China on the other, with one side being the head and the other being the tail of a dragon.
History between Japan and China One of the oldest structures in Hoi An which the bridge was built by the Japanese whilst the Temple in the middle of the bridge was built by the Chinese.
Great historical bridge This is a must place to see in Hoi An. It is a small bridge but it really cannot be missed. So much history and beauty. We just loved it.
This bridge is beautiful! I recommend you go first thing in the morning (before 8:00am). This way you have the bridge to yourself.

Opened in 1719 this unique wooden bridge is 300 ears old. The traffic it has endured in these 300 years makes it more unique and a memorable experience not to be missed. Another rare experience on our travels through Vietnam.
If in Old town its hard to miss. Only problem is its so busy there with all the bus tours. Well worth a look of in old town.

If you are interested in history, Hoi An is definitely a place to see and Japanese bridge is a real spirit of ancient town. A lovely place in a very busy part of Hoi An. A great spot for making amazing pics.
Beautiful bridge in the old quarter, this Japanese covered bridge brings significance in the history of Vietnam and do take a walk across this quaint bridge.

Beautiful bridge in the old quarter... The river is quite dirty but very picturesque. I can imagine in peak season it gets packed. Cross the bridge and some more beautiful streets sweeped wkth lanterns selling souvenirs etc
Very cool place to visit This is an amazing little site tucked away in the old town of Hoi An, it's very old and very interesting though it's a lot smaller in real life than it looks in the photos :)
Very photogenic bridge Cross it and take a photo of it. Not to be missed when in Hoi An city. The only drawback is that it is allways so crowded.

A beautiful view from the bridge, but for young lovers don’t expect a romantic moment here as it is very popular and gets very busy. If you are patient or an early riser you can get amazing photos of the bridge without the crowds
this is another awesome place in Hoi An, very old and everything tells a story in this town, well worth the visit when in Hoi An

Lovely bridge. Visit in the day and not light up at night and also super busy. If you want a photo in front of the bridge without people walking in front of the camera get there about 3am or just accept you will share your photo with other people.
We saw this bridge at night and it was just divine. It was very busy with tourists. Worth seeing though.

Can get crowded, go there just to get your photo taken to say you’ve been to Hoi An. Problem being some members of the tourist community take so long to take a photo and insist in standing metres apart which blocks half the bridge width so no one can move. It’s good for photo bombing though.
We crossed over and with our Local buddy tours Thanh he got tickets and we went in so interesting a must to see
Look, it’s a bridge, we don’t have those in America. Its a bridge, it’s not big, its made out of stone and wood. It looks old. 10/10 would Naruto run again.
To walk around and look on the bridge and the rooms off it is a fascinating experience. To know the bridge is 900 years old is quite mind boggling coming from such a young country
The bridge was interesting, and our hotel (Alley Beach Boutique Hotel) gave us free passes. Amazing restuarants. We went to one with craft beer - best food that we have had in Vietnam!
A gorgeous place with a wonderful view over the river. Tonnes of culture and a very interesting story behind it.

Go early to miss the crowds. This is a lovely old bridge with a small building attached. For the building entry you do need a ticket, although later in the day it seemed that people were being stopped to buy a ticket just to cross via the bridge. If you want a good photo do come early as by 10.00 am when the coach parties arrive it is very busy.
This is a must to see in the daytime or in the evening we thought it unique it was a pity we could not walk across it as it was so busy
Most visited site in Hua Hin Can't miss the place when you're in Hua Hin. Very nostalgic and scenic surroundings perfect for some nice clicks

Whilst in Hoi An we visited the old Japanese Bridge its a part of History you can walk over it for free but if you want look in the displays you have to pay a small amount to look at them. you have to walk over the bridge to get to a few Restaurants close by.If you go to Hoi An its a must visit but go early in the day when its cool or in the late afternoon when it gets a bit cooler.
Lovely structure and very spiritual a place all visitors must visit to take pictures. The old town is beautiful but be warned at night time it is very busy !
What a beautiful structure, made long ago and very characteristic and in keeping with surroundings. A good focal point or base for exploring.

The Japanese covered bridge and the area beyond in ancient town is a pleasant and informative experience. The bridge, still in use, is a great testament to ancient building techniques and an example of the continuity of Asian style. The ancient houses and shops are also very interesting with goods for sale that do not exploit the tourist dollar to the degree of some of the outside shops. Having said that, prices are still very reasonable. The colourful paintings and Vietnamese style art denote happiness and optimism.
Another fascinating Hoi an structure, in a fascinating town of many mixed cultures. Loved Hoi an highly recommended

Learning the history of this bridge was the best part of the bridge. That and watching all the women taking pictures of themselves. At the bridge too funny. But the bridge does have a great history be sure get a guide so you’ll know the story.

Cute piece of History. The bridge is a lovely piece of history & is an insightful experince! Fantastic place for photo opportunties, but make sure you get there early in the morning to beat the crowds.

I didn’t know to expect such detail and I hadn’t shown appreciation for this covered bridge until I was on the bridge Unmissable. We didn’t get stopped by ticket inspectors. Cooler shopping area on the other side of the bridge.
Small little bridge, but so intricately made and full of history. a credit to the local people / Central Government for keeping such a historic monument intact despite the unfortunate recent history
Beautiful and historic Japanese bridge in the old city of Hoi An. It has been rebuilt and renovated a few times since it has been built.

Interesting and very beautiful If you are contemplating crossing this bridge - Get there early after 3 separate night attempts we decided to turn up at 10 am and finally made it on and had a look around. This is very popular and very much worth the effort to get on and look around.
Lovely the first time Beautiful piece of history but accessibility depends on time of day! Go in the morning as by evening it’s almost impossible to cross due crowds

Historical Japanese bridge You just need to see this Japanese covered bridge. It was first thing what we saw when walking from our hotel to the Hoi An ancient town. Beautiful historical place in the daytime and night.

When you buy Hoi An old town ticket, you get a chance to see 5 places out of 22 listed. Number one of this is Japanese bridge. A must see. Go in the morning when there are hardly anyone and can take time to explore peacefully & take photos. Evening it's packed with group tourist.
Interesting history to learn about the things that have influenced the Vietnamese people and the family values they hold we can learn a lot from them

Wonderful little bridge full of charm Can’t remember how much it cost if anything but such a beautiful little bridge and so full of charm. We were there on a National Public Holiday so was very busy but even then easy to see why it’s so popular. Highly recommend visiting this quaint little bridge, even if you don’t go in the view from outside if magical.

This ancient beautiful Japanese bridge ,hidden in the vast old French quarter of hoi an, is definitely worth viewing, because of its culteral significance ,l thought it would be larger to be honest ,but it is so lovely ,and in traditional Japanese style ,and architecturally ornate ,it's construction is significant ,and takes a beautiful photograph ,
I don’t really know about the story of this bridge but I just love the structure and unique look! Located in the UNESCO world heritage site of Hoi An!
Lovely bridge and great to read the history of the bridge. Would be good to see this piece of history maintained.

Go early to get a photo without the crowds Back in 2009 you could have a photo without anyone else in it. Now it is crowded especially in the evening so go early in the morning and you may be lucky. Was sad to see this year so much rubbish in the water......

You cannot really miss this, it's on the main walking route into Old Town, sometimes you need your tourist ticket, sometimes not. It's very pretty during the night time, and an interesting 3 minutes crossing from one side to the other! Great photo opportunities, especially from the walking bridge adjacent.

Yes even if it is just a wooden bridge it has so much of characters. So much history is behind this bridge how the Chinese are separated by the Japanese on this bridge. The ambience as well as the architecture changes between the bridge is rather interesting.
A great piece of history Such an amazing piece of architecture, very popular tourist spot and well worth the visit - just don't be in a rush.
Built in 1593 by Japanese merchants to connect to the Chinese neighborhood on the other side of the river. There is a small temple in the inside of the bridge . Absolutely a must see site In Hoi An.
Didn’t find anything special, It’s a different style bridge, probably Japanease(is what they claimed) in Hoi An town. A ticket to walk along this bridge was little too much though, nothing special.

We had a nice stroll along the Japanese covered bridge. It’s quite a popular tourist attraction given its historic past. When you are in Hoi An, I’m sure you will end up going here. We went in the evening, as the weather was nice and the streets were beautifully lit with colorful lanterns.
Really interesting bridge close to the old town. It's very small and in great condition. We didn't have to pay to cross

Historic Japanese Bridge During the time that you spend wandering around Hoi An a visit to the Japanese Bridge must be included. Originally opened in 1719 it was built by the Japanese so that they could cross the river into the Chinese Quarter. It is a really iconic structure in Hoi An. Once you have walked across you find yourself in a lovely road with trees, shops and restaurants on both sides. Being out of the main shopping areas there are usually less people here and a much better atmosphere to roam through the shops.
This bridge is a must stop in Old Town Hoi An. It is very busy but worth a stop. Great place to take some photos.

Beautifully restored Historic Bridge If you visit Old Town you must go over this historic bridge It’s a great place for a photo and tells the story of how the Vietnamese Japanese and Chinese all lived together peacefully in this ancient town
The Japanese bridge in Hoi An is well worth a visit you can go inside during the day and walk Favourite time of night it is a wonderful little bridge and looks good in the evening light
If you are in Ancient Town you will be very close to the Japanese Covered Bridge. I definitely recommend popping over for a couple of photos.
It was nice feeling to see an old bridge surrounded by local market. Amazing place to see. We took the boat ride here.

I had visited the bridge earlier this month, the history, architecture and the location truly complement each other. Built by the Japanese to literally bridge the gap between the two-quarters of the old town (i.e. Japanese and Chinese). It has an altar of the Japanese weather God inside and some intricate inscriptions at the entrance. Really worth a visit
Wow a lovely bridge, we took a guide for a tour of the city, we did not know the history, highly recommended a visit and guide.

Very Interesting and historical building Small and easy building to wander around and read about its history.It is very popular so you need to be patient and wait until the crowds of visitors have dispersed.
Fantastic japanese architecture! I guess this bridge is the most photographed object in the city! You can read about the Japanese in Hoi An on the net.

This is a great place to identify with the different cultures our species have created over the many years we have been on earth. It is a great structure to reflect on differing ideas with an attempt to bring commonality.

Early Seventh Century It’s just what it says ........ A Japanese Covered Bridge. It was built in the early 7th Century by the Japanese trading community, based to the west of the town. It is unique, iconic and may be a very small, short bridge but it’s a must see when in Hoi An. Being one of Hoi An’s most prominent landmarks it gets very busy here because everyone wants to see, photograph and walk across it so leave plenty of time. Even though for most of the time there are many visitors, if you bide your time you can get some nice photographs.

This is the gateway to the beautiful old town of hoian. You can enter here from various directions. There is no fee for it but I think there is some kind of musuem or pagoda inside for which them may charge some amount.
A must see . Its very small so you don't need much in the way of time. Its also croweded. There is a smallish temple.

Part of any Hoi An visit I wouldn’t advise anyone to go to Hoi An especially to see this bridge, but Hoi An is well worth visiting for its lovely character, so the bridge forms a focal point for the trip. It was first constructed in the 16th century by the Japanese to link them with the Chinese quarter of the town. The roof was constructed as a shelter from both rain and sun. Inside the bridge, there is a small temple to the god of weather.

Historic buildings tour The Japanese covered bridge is one of the five ancient monuments available to be visited in old Hoi An. The bridge itself is constructed of timber with no nails and is a fascinating insight in to the historical old town of Hoi An.
Very beautiful and colorful located in ancient Hoi An town Wish for luckand enjoy lanterns on the water

Small but a nice sight and a good view point to the river. They ask to see the touristic attractions Entrance Ticket (less than 5 €), which is nuts cause the bridge needs to be used when you walk main street and want to cross a creek (or make a small detour).
Amazing sight that my travel companions got to see. Visit the small shop on it to buy a coin of your birth year
Need to buy ticket before you visit the Japanese covered bridge but you can take photo outside and go to another city part without any ticket.
Cute and old bridge in the middle of the ancient town of hoian. Of course super crowded but nice to see at daytime!

Must visit this bridge. Compare it with your $20k dong note as well! Pretty cool! It can get very busy during the day, but if you be patient, there will be pockets of quite time to take your own composition pictures!

At this Japanese Covered Bridge you can find lot of interesting things,old furnitures and old architectures.Everytime lot of people walking across the famous picturescfull bridge.I offer the trip to this wooden bridge for everybody.

Walk through time. This historic bridge gives a glimpse in the past of this great country. See the shrines the carvings and feel the atmosphere of a snapshot in time. Its narrow passage is low but listen to the voices and the accents from all over and enjoy.
Amazing to see this so pretty. Surprised given its construction they allow so .any people to cross it. Definitely a go see when visiting Hoi an.

leads to market and river. has barriers so no wheeled access. quite an arch but small bridge. highly decorated on outside. wooden construction. area has flooded over this in past years. also place to get tickets for exhibitions in market area.
If you are in Hoi An you just have to see this. Its really worth of it. The only thing that is not good id that in the evening the water under the bridge smels a lot.
If you can battle yourself amongst the crowds it is well worth a visit.The history of the bridge is most interesting and it costs just a few pennies to cross.
Japanese bridge Hoi an If you go to Hoi An this is a must to visit. The bridge has great historical and cultural significance.

The beauty of places like this is you get a real sense of what it was like in the days of wooden structures and transport. Today's materials are less earthy and can leave you with a cold feeling. Heritage sites reveal a way of life we have all but lost in modern industrialized cities. There is that ancient country feel but it is steeped in rich Asian culture. There are great black and white photos in the bridge cottage showing images from 1950.
A bridge built originally between the Japanese & Chinese communities. An historic bridge, a must see to all visitors. Fabulously built and in great condition. Again, a must visit!
This provides an interpretation of the extent to which Japanese culture has influenced Vietnam. It’s a very small attraction to visit- should not take more than 15-20 minutes.
Definately a must but even more so at night. The bridge and the surrounding district is a vast array of colour from the enormous amount of colour lanterns and illuminated Boston the river.

Beautiful especially in the night with all the boats lit with light/candles. Very crowded in the evening. So if you prefer to shop go early and stay to enjoy the light show. Side walks are converted into mini restaurants.
This was a small but interesting bridge in the middle of the ancient village. Worth a quick stop and a photo as you take a leisurely walk through this great part of Vietnamese history.

Definitely worth visiting! Visited this beautiful old bridge during the Lunar New Year ( Tet ) period and was really surprised and privileged to find out that the admission fee had been suspended! I thought this was a lovely gesture and epitomises the importance of New Year out here. That said it was a shame that the river was strewn with rubbish ( paper lanterns ) which had looked so beautiful the previous evening all lit up floating along ! Still worth a visit as it forms part of this country’s incredible and interesting history.
Hopefully you will have bought a ticket to visit the various sites within the village, they do check this at the bridge. I went a couple of times, great for photographs. Can get very busy.
Really lovely old bridge. If you pay the 120,000 dong, it entitles you to entrance to I think 6 attractions in the old town, so you can use that to cross the bridge and visit other sites too

Great Piece of History Best to go very early in the morning in order to avoid the mass of tourists that get there after 9pm. We arrived with our Tour Guide about 7.45am and it was virtually deserted, which allowed us to get some really good photos. There are some very interesting black and white photos of the bridge and people during the early and mid 20th Century.
Lovely old bridge but does get absolutely packed with people taking selfies so if you see its packed use the wooden bridge close by
I have seen covered bridges before but this one was very special. Very crowded but absolutely worthwhile a visit
Amazing looking bridge which really sets off the World Heritage site of Hoi An. Interesting construction, well worth a visit
beautiful wooden beams, interesting history , you can see this in the day tour with dragon travel viet
This is a nice walk passing some nice interesting places en-route to the Japanese covered bridge.It is a beautiful place to go wandering at night.
The best time to visit and to be able to fully appreciate this beautiful bridge is in the early morning. You then can see all the details, inside and out.

A small but elaborate wooden bridge built by the Japanese in the 13th century. The Japanese built this bridge to go across to the Chinese quarters. A set of Dogs and monkeys are carved in stone indicating start and end of building the bridge during each of the Chinese years. Both sides of the bridge have unique cultures and come colorfully alive by evening.
Our tour guide took us to this historic and picturesque site where we imagined all who had gone before us over the bridge.
Beautiful bridge, they will ask you to pay to go over it, we never did as we felt it wasn’t worth it...

Actually not that far at all. It is in the centre of Hoi An and really easy to find. Head for the Hoi An Ancient Town and it's pretty much in the centre of things. Really ornate and pretty and photographs nice too. Great for selfie. Saying that you'll be fighting off the other tourists for the opportunity.
What a beautiful sight Beautiful old wooden bridge in an attractive old town. Lots to see. Typical Vietnamese houses. Don’t forget to look up! Some balconies and roofs are just stunning.
thats must see in vietnam ... the want to money if you across the bridge few steps is new one for fre and you can make nice photos this old bridge.

The bridge is a good photograph opportunity from the outside. To go inside you need to use one of the 5 ticket booklet for sale at kiosks. These are not expensive and allows entrance to various UNESCO sites in the old town. Nice interior. You can walk through as it is a thoroughfare in the old town.

What a great place Hoi An is at night. Walking across the bridge with a hundred othere from 50 or more countries was a sight to behold. The view across the water to the island is something thet we will remember.

Whilst not huge in size the bridge is a focal point in Hoi An, be it a meeting place, a place to get your bearings or just admire the history. It’s the second time we’ve been to Hoi An the first in 2009, Hoi An has lost none of its character.
Well worth a few minutes of your day to admire this classic bridge. Gets very crowded in the day so if you want good pictures get there before 9.30am

It was lovely to see the bridge and hear the stories of the floods from the locals We came here as part of a motorbike tour and it was great to see the village and the countryside. We also met some locals and had a demonstration about rice and how it’s removed from its shell. All very interesting and presented with a lot of humour.
Not much to say it's a must see blew me away on how old this place was and it's still standing. Fun Fact have a look at your 20000 dong note and you will see this bridge pictured.

Iconic old town attraction You cant really you say have been to Hoi an, without jostling with the myriad of visitors, to get a piccie with no one in it. The bridge was built in the late 1500’s to connect the japanese community with the chinese town, it is surround by myth and legend, we like the story that the bridge represents the body of a monster, the head being in india and the tail in japan. This national treasure also has pride and place on the 20,000 dong note.
The symbol of Hoi An, the most magnificent attraction in the old town, located in the end of Tran Pu street or walking street.
Lovely bridge and piece of history. It does get very crowded though so early is a good time to get a great photo.
It's a gorgeous piece of history. The colourfully Lancaster town. If you love to take photos, this is your place. Don't miss it

My 2nd visit to this lovely old fishing village. Wonders never cease to amaze me. It is the St Tropez of Asia. Although the weather was not on my side on this day. I managed to enjoy the tranquil atmosphere, and the beauty of the buildings here.
You need to pay to use this bridge but the ticket can be reused so only pay once. The bridge is fantastic. Very ornate.

Of course, it's gorgeous and charming. They charge to cross over it, but in the evening, I notice that they are not enforcing ticket sales. Good! It is likely the most tourist thronged place in Hoi An! So, my recommendation is go early in the morning - before 7:30 a.m. That's when you can enjoy it without crushing masses of people ready to suffocate the life out of you. :-)

There isn't much to see on the bridge but it does make a lovely backdrop for pics specially at night
We visited this bridge in the late evening so all the lanterns were lit up. It was a beautiful walk and great to see. It’s also a great photo opportunity.

Short break from Saigon Well worth a visit. Hoi An is charming. Only downside is that you need to be prepRed to be hassled to buy anything from kitchen gadgets to clothes

It joins the Chinese side to the Japanese side. It was first built in the 16th century and again in the 18th century. There are statues of a monkey and of a dog, the starting year and the ending year of the construction.

Japanese Bridge Hoi An Best time to visit is late afternoon where the lights are on. And while crossing the bridge,you have the lake with nice streets food, where you can have dinner and a nice view of the river, with boats and on the other side the Lantern...

Star attraction of hoi an, incredible beautiful bridge... It's set in the picturesque town of hoi an also known as lantern city. Totally worth a visit.
Look at this bridge, it's really nice! It belongs to the old ancient buildings at Hoi An! Its also nice to see at nighttime!

Beautiful Architecture This is the iconic site in Hoi An. I got up early and reached the Japanese covered bridge, it was less crowded when i reached, try get there early for a tourist free picture.
