My wife and I love the experience. Very beautiful white sand. Staff wasn't as friendly as expected. And make sure to negotiate :)

Seen better, but the ATVs are good fun If you are coming here, come with the view of hiring a quad bike and having some fun. It isn't that great a spectacle and after about 10 minutes you are just waiting for your bus to take you back. Hot and not much more to do than look and grab and ATV …so do it.

Okay, but missed the sunrise!!!! We left our hotel at 5 as instructed by the owner and arrived at the dunes at 5.45, by the time we were half way up the sun had already risen! My advice is leave earlier!! The dunes are really cool though. Can get some amazing photos. It is really busy during sunrise but from 6.45ish 90% of the people left and it was so much nicer. There are loads of tourists tearing around loud quad bikes which ruins it a bit but apart from that it's cool. Much more fun running down them than walking up!! After reading other reviews I assumed you could sand board down these, but you can't unless you have your own board! The boards are available at the red dunes.

A warning about the corrupt police We didn't actually make it to the White Dunes due to a fake police roadblock, which I'm writing this to warn everyone about. There is a fake police roadblock that pulls over foreigners on the main road. They'll tell you that they have a photo of you speeding (they don't) and when you ask for proof of this, they immediately change their tune and say that you're driving without a licence. Even if you show them a licence from your home country, they don't care and demand somewhere in the region of 800,000 - 1.2 million Dong from you. Haggle like you've never haggled before! They will use a range of tactics to get you to pay up, usually intimidation and threats. We stood our ground and insisted that we be taken to the police station, then we got the guy who rented us the bikes to go and speak to them on our behalf and ended up getting them back for 100,000 Dong each. Inconvenient, but better than forking out millions. I've heard several success stories of foreigners ignoring their gestures to pull over and speeding straight through. They allegedly give up pretty easily. Good luck, and enjoy the dunes!

gorgeous dunes,bad about the atvs You are not given enough time if you are on a jeep tour to walk to the dunes so hou are pretty much forced to rent an AVT or pay some one to take you tothe top as i did.waste our time.

Took an early trip to see the sunset, at arrival lots of kids offer a ride on their quad. To my opinion they are very noisy and not really worth the money. We saw some people stuck in the sand with a non working quad. The sunset is nice but not amazing.

Tours in Vietnam tend to be similar. You need to pay an amount of money, are put in a vehicle (in this case a rustic jeep), are driven to the sight and the driver points out to the highlight in front of you and says "White sand dunes, 40 minutes". In brief: You can visit the sanddunes, best time is maybe in the early morning (after the sunrise). At this time there are not so many people, you can walk up and down and take some nice and funny pictures. Don't expect a peaceful lonely desert, but some places where you can really be on a picture with nobody else. But please don't go there by jeep, it's absolutely not worth the money.
The bikes was not very fast , one of them broke down after only 5 minutes, 350 k vnd for 20 min , it was fun but I did expect more
It's nice with very warm weather but flow. Walking is not recommend. ATV is better to take you in the good location for photo.

The only place half decent. Luckily for us, it was a very quite time around 2pm, not many tourists around. Say no to everything anyone there offers you, they all want to scam you (in fact, the whole of the local community are trying to make you pay for EVERYTHING) good photos, extortionate prices to be driven around or to rent a quad, we walked through the dunes and it was quite a lovely time! 10,000 dong each person, 5,000 to park your bike. The ambience was killed by nearby trucks/jeeps/quad bikes being noisy, I guess you can't have it all.

Ok for a slice of nature, do the jeep multi site tour A round trip in a jeep that takes you to the fairy stream, traditional fishing bay, white dunes & red dunes is a cheap 4 hour long sight seeing option, & you'll probably be feeling like you should check them all out if you are in Mui Ne for a few days. $7 US I think it was each. There's not a huge amount of tourism worthy things happening in this area if you aren't a committed Russian sun bather, or pot smoking kite surfer, and there aren't many places not littered with trash unfortunately, as you will have observed if you've made it this far in Vietnam. Keen photographers will want to explore these vastly different natural settings regardless. The fairy stream seemed to be the only exception, and you can find it and walk it for free with ease, so that was the unexpected highlight, despite being so simple. It was pleasant while the rest seemed a bit of a chore. No real need to hire the quad bikes at the dunes, a managable 25 min walk around and you'll be satisfied. They appeared to break down a lot, but quad fans might still have an enjoyable time. There were sled things available too but looked fairly useless. Expect your tour driver to be pulled over on the highway by traffic cops for un-roadworthy vehicles, our jeep's dials were all broken and it's well overdue 2014 registration stickers were forcefully removed. Saw many others in the same predicament on the way. Seems to be the regular routine here. In the end we got to see some different countryside, got some good photo opportunities, and had some stories to tell, but we were pretty happy to get back to the hotel pool to chill and recover for the rest of the day from the hot sand blasting. Would have been nicer if they cared enough to take away all the trash, Vietnam still has some tourism basics to figure out in that respect. 5/10.
Not really my thing but I did enjoy seeing the landscape. The lake is pretty. Much smaller scale than the Oregon sand dunes, but still impressive.

Great for Sunrise but be warned..... We got there at just before 530 am to catch the Sunrise and it was a great scene to see. The most frustrating thing was that it was nearly all ruined due to the fact they had the quads going up and down the dunes. Hardly romantic or peaceful!!!! If you get there earlier then head for the highest sand dunes as the quads don't seem to go that far up. Worth to do as part of a few stops but not worth going just to see this.

Why would anybody visit? The entrench fee of about $0.50 get you get into the crowded park. Here you are met with wall to wall coffee and souvenir shops. For $3.50 you can even get a short ostrich ride. The two ostriches are overworked and one of them, while I watched, collapsed. The dune buggies are old, slow, and very noisy. We visited late in the afternoon, a few days after TET, and visitors were picnicking with loud music and enjoying themselves. The area, even the sand dunes and the lake were littered with plastic bottles and bags, pieces of papers and food stuff. However, the dunes, once you get away from the roar of the dune buggies, the masses of people and the garbage, are beautiful. If you visit it is more comfortable in early morning or late in the afternoon when sun is not going to fry you.

Perhaps we should have researched this trip before booking I had hoped to take some nice shots of the sunrise, but with hordes of tourists and quad bikes racing around, it was not to be and not at all what we had expected. The dunes themselves - pretty underwhelming. In my opinion the dunes at Pyla near Bordeaux in France are far more spectacular.

I shouldn't really have been surprised but was a bit taken aback by the number of people there and the noise of the quad bikes ferrying people to the top. This was an unexpected 300,000 vnd on top of the price. Not the most spiritual of events

Arrange a private tour We booked a tour with our guest house to visit the sand dunes and other attractions in Mui Ne. Our first stop was the White Sand Dunes. Be aware that even on a tour, you will have to pay 150k+ to be driven to the top of the sand dunes to see the sunset. It's possible to walk but with the tour, your time is limited. Also note the men driving the ATVs/quad bikes are reckless drivers. If possible, go in the afternoon and drive yourself. The sunset wasn't spectacular. You'd be better off arranging a private tour so you have plenty of time to visit. 40 minutes simply isn't enough.

As a dane I have seen so many sanddunes, that I was not all that impressed. That being said, it was a pretty sight, and even though I could only spot one Lotus in the Lotus Lake, the drive there was so beautiful, that it was all worth it. If you care to rent an ATV, it is 400.000 VND for a small one and 60.000 for a bigger one - and this is for 20 minutes. You can drive here on a scooter, the roads are fine and the traffic really is not bad, but my behind was pleased that we drove in a jeep, as it is quite a long drive from Mui Ne. Also there was hardly any signs, so it may prove difficult to find
Yeah ..ok sand & quadbikes ok ...put sandhills and quad bikes together that's what you get. 2 hr experience ..maybe longer if you're into it.
There are not many excursions around Mui Ne and Phan Thiet,so visiting white sand dunes is a spending time if you have nothung else to do
Was fun riding quads but the quads on offer are very underpowered and do break down. Be careful not to ride down any of the steep dunes as is difficult to get out. Very pretty and a good laugh

Afternoon visit at White sand dunes We rent a scooter and drove to the dunes. Journey took a little less than an hour. There wasn't any signs. However using map gets ypu there. Dunes was nice, felt like desert. We were on the dunes 30-60 minutes and we saw enough sand. This isn't realy a must see place, but if you need some new experiences it is ok. I think best time to visit at dunes would be at the time of sunset.

The dunes are nice but aren't the biggest. From tops of most of them you can see the surrounding cities, etc so don't expect dunes to the horizon for some Lawrence of Arabia views. Big sales push to get a quad bike which we didn't do. It's 400k VND - 600k VND to get one for 30 minutes which is a bit pricey. Walk up the dunes was fun but very windy. We went on the afternoon tour and got sandblasted quite a bit so bring sunglasses to protect your eyes. ATVs are criss crossing the dunes like maniacs so if you're coming here to sit on a dune and have some deep moment, you'll be bummed by the constant roar of engines.

Managed to get a tour for 5 dollars so was cheaper than getting a scooter. We only got 40 minutes there which was a shame. Didn't really give me enough time to get to the top of the dunes (found it very hard to walk any faster on the sand ). The first part of the dunes as you walk in is pretty dirty. Lots of rubbish but further up was very nice and impressive. The quad bikes were very expensive (600,000) but did look fun. Was definitely worth seeing and was much better than the red dunes.

In the middle of nowhere It takes about 45 minutes to go from Mui Ne to the white sand dunes. When you arrive, you need to pay 10 000 VND per person in admission. You can walk around on the sand dunes if you like but you can also rent a quad or go in a jeep. I think that the quad cost 300 000 VND for 20 minutes so it’s very pricy considering that you are in Vietnam. We got the impression that the jeep that took us there (which we booked from a local travel agency) would drive around in the sand dunes but that wasn’t the case. You need to pay for another driver in order to ride a jeep on the dunes. I guess that they want to give you that impression otherwise it seems quite meaningless to go there in a jeep which has a top speed of 50 km/h. It was a bit of a scam but that's unfortunately common in these kind of places. The thing I appreciated the most was to watch the scenery.
It's ok. Though we did not try the quad bikes. Nice enough sand... It's probably more yellow than white. Very impressive highway to this place..

It is absolutely stunning to find white sand dunes in the middle of Vietnam close to a town but the place is very very touristic. Everyone's renting the quad and racing on the dunes. Also it looks like its made on purpose for tourists as the dunes are delimited to a specific area and there's also an entry fee. It's nice to watch the sunset from the dunes but if you have already been to a real desert you won't miss out on anything in Mui Ne.
Not as big or as white as at home. Was still interesting. Would have liked to see the lotus flowers open.

There were ATVs, sand, selfies. Basically what you expect from sand dunes. It's hard to compare cause my Paoay Sand dunes (Ilocos, Philippines) experience was just AWESOME! Drivers were more exciting so it was like a roller coaster ride vs the short steady trip here. There was a beach stop over in Ilocos. We got to do sand boarding in Ilocos. Sand boarding at Mui Ne was at the Red Sand Dunes (it was more like a slide, so I heard and never bothered to try). But hey, if you're in the area go give it a try.

We came here at 530am to watch the sunrise. It's beautiful but there were lots of people. Our tour cost 150000 dong each and you walk up or local boys will take you up and around on a quad bike for 200. We walked and it was lovely, got some nice photos and it was very relaxing. The only downside is that at about 630 7am the locals start ushering you down the dunes and to go. I know we were on tour but it felt a bit unwelcoming. As we were leaving other people were walking up and some locals started telling them to leave but they left when they realised they were not on a tour.

It's a nice place. I mean, for those who've never seen dessert before. You can rent atv but well, it's pretty expensive. What i like to do here is ordering an iced coffee. It's vietnam anyway, they get good coffee :)

Definitely one of the highlights of Mui Ne. Rent a bike and enjoy the great scenery on the way to the dunes. When you arrive you have to pay 5000d for parking and 10000d for entrance. The 'biker boys' will tell you that it's too far to walk to the dunes but it's really not that bad. The bikers charge about 400000d for a 20 minute drive around so for the budget travelers amongst us, it may not be worth it. Try to get there early so that there are less tourists in your shots (because you should be taking pictures). Stay for sunset if it's a great day but be aware of driving back at night. We drove at night and we were fine as the roads are not busy.
The White Dunes are an odd site, until you understand the geography of the coastal arwa, then it all makes sense! Worth a look.

The white dunes are OK but have seen much better in other countries. The Mui Ne tours cover this and the red dunes, fairy stream & fishing village for around 90,000VND so best to do it this way if you're interested.
I have never visit any desert before. The view is super breathtaking ! Definitely worth to pay a visit if you at mui ne !

Like the name suggested, this is a small area of sand dunes far out of reach without a car or a bike. Everything here are very expensive and not worth the money and the time. Nice place for people who hasn't seen a desert.

Fun, but not mind blowing We did the sunrise tour, so we had to get up at 3.50 am. Reaaaaaally early. We drove there with a jeep (very full, kind of overbooked) and arrived there just in time. The dunes are nice,but not super amazing. You can rent a Quad Bike for 300.000 for 20 minutes. They are really loud and very annoying! Do not get one! But you can take some nice Photos if you walk away from the big crouds

smaller than expected I thought the size of the dunes would be immensely huge like the one in Dubai..or close. We get to ride on the ATV (recommended) and rental costs about USD15 for 30mins. And guys...you don't actually get to ride it, there'll be some young dudes riding along side of you and they're able to give you 1 or 2 shots of thrill up and down the dunes. It's pretty thrilling though. But yeah..the prices' a bit steep :/ Managed to get a couple of cool shots. Panoramic view at its best! PS: check out the link website provided. Kim (the tour agent) quite reliable and prompt

If you rent a motorbike and drive there there are sometimes cops waiting there to stop you. We were stopped and didn't want to pay the fine, so after some arguments we were taken to the police station where they took our bike. We went back to the rental place and they helped us get our bike back for 200,000 dong. It seemed to me that it's just a corrupt system to get money from tourists (three Americans had their driving license and still had to pay 40$ each because it was not international). So either take the organized tour there or just don't stop to them ;)

Spoiled by the organisation The white dunes are worth visiting. Let's get that out of the way first. Even for someone who lives near the Dutch coast, where white dunes are everywhere, it was impressive. But the organisation ruined the experience for me. The white dunes are often, if not always, combined with the red dunes, fishing village and fairy springs in a four to five hour jeep tour. The jeeps are oncomfortabele and pretty much half the trip consists of driving. The drivers are all about the money and don't seem to care about the quality of the trip at all. We got 45 minutes at the white dunes, and half an hour af the other places. Not nearly enough time on the white dunes to make a quad worth renting. The trip felt rushed and cut short, which it actually was by one hour. Then again, with the poor service we did not even mind getting back to the hotel. We took the early trip (4:00) to catch the sunrise, which was nice to see, and it must have been a lot cooler and quiet than the afternoon. Which one you take may depend on how much you like sleep or sunrises. Overall conclusion: nice dunes, poor trip. Average total. Arrange your own transport if you can.

Don't hire the small wheeled quad bikes. The white sand dunes and lotus lake are very impressive however our experience was slightly dampened by the quad hire. It is a great way to get around the dunes yet for 300 VND hire for 20 mins we spent majority of the time getting the men to come and fix the ancient bikes. A wheel fell off the chain fell off and two Vietnamese men begun to hot wire a bike in front of us to hire it to us. After much of a fail the owner took my boyfriend around in his bike and said he needed danger money for the experience. But they are much better than the red dunes. Don't bother with the tour hire a ped and go yourself.

Lots of sand in your shoes :))) Well, we used our hostel service to get there in early morning to see a sunrise. However, we were unlucky and sun was rising up in the clouds. It wasn't my first time at the sand dunes, so I wasn't so much impressed. If you ever visit Lithuania, do not miss Neringa region as sand dunes are amazing there! PS. next to those sand dunes you have an option to ride an ostrich.

The sand dunes were much further away from Mui Ne than I realised but the journey was fun in an open jeep. Once there I was disappointed that what should have been a beautiful natural wonder was spoiled by zooming quad bikes and jeeps thundering past. The sand dunes were quite impressive and it was fun walking up them, but it could have been so much better.

This is a desert very need the coast, huge dunes of very white crystal sands, you can hire a quad bike or slide down on mats or you can admire the beauty of the area. I recommend a visit here mainly for its uniqueness.
Go, but with low expectations Good fun for kids, specially if you do the off-road thing by quad bike or 4x4. Don't expect too much of it, though. It's a nice way to spend 1 or 2 hours, nothing more.
Rented a bike and ride about 30km there from my hotel. Had fun with the ATV...other than that, the sand dunes offered really nothing much

About one hour from Muine by taxi/car. Nice view, but the experience somewhat destroyed since most people go there by ATV or Jeep. Some nice views and a dirty coffeeshop by the entrance. Rather noisy when many local tourists. You may rent various boats on the small lake. Entrance fee 10.000 d, Atv rentals from 300.000 d

Not really a must-see Went there with the trusty Sinh Tourist (formerly known as Sinh Cafe) on a half-day tour that combined the fairy spring, white sand dunes and red sand dunes. Both sand dunes are only value for money because the tour was dirt cheap. Don't get me wrong, I don't mind a bit of noise and lots of tourists at all, but there were so many quad-bikes driving around the sand dunes that the place was rather ruined than special.

Well, of course it was full of sand 😊 but it was also very noisy from all the small tractor cars around. We ended up staying for a short period of time, and am not quite sure we recommend it... Some of the kids enjoyed it, some didn't

Nice sand dunes but too noisy Recommended to go there before sunrise since it would be too hot after sunrise. Besides, scene of sand dunes was nice during sunrise and sunset. Though this sand dunes is bigger than the red sand dunes and the scene was better, the noisy of the bike was so disturbing that we felt very annoyed there. We could not enjoy the scene. Not so recommended if you had no interest to ride the bike.
Generally nice. If you want to see the sunrise you have to wake up at 4AM. Overall rating average, I would say. very touristic!

We visited as part of our trip.the dunes look lovely ,very picturesque. But as others have commented the sound of the quad bikes is deafening and awful. They also have jeeps to take you to the different areas of the dunes. The cost was 600 000dong, very expensive. Supposedly for 20 minutes. We were in the jeep 2 minutes,he left and said he would be back in 5 minutes to give us time to take photos, he left to go and pick other tourists up. We did not bother waiting as he took so long and explored the dunes on foot. He then came and found us and told us to get in the jeep which I refused as we were enjoying walking on our own. He arrived back a little later and took us by a lake ,we were in the jeep for 3 minutes, he then left again to pick up others.he came back to find us and we said we would walk back,so he demanded we pay him there and then, we said we would pay when we got to the bottom as we had been told. They said we could not go down until we paid. We refused and paid at the bottom. So in short jeep ride was just over 5 minutes,not the 20 they say and a total waste of money. Go by foot,it is tiring but more fun.

A friendly UN reminder According to UNICEF and other world organozations who aid and support children; under no circumstance should children be given money for begging or other services (school is where they should be). With that said i was pleasantly surprised to have only ran into one child selling the "slides" at the orange sand dunes. Doing better earth! Last time (8 urs ago) they were covered with these poor kids.
Nice but have seen better, could be cleaner. We ended up with a cab tour and it was fine, the canyon is so small it made me laugh, some very pretty countryside. The fishing village is the best!

Nice place - don't rent bikes!!! We went there as a part of a Tour arranged by our hotel Bach Doung (nice place but overprices on sidebusiness...) We paid 7$/pax which is above average as most places charge 6$/pax. When we arrived they tried to charge extra fee 10000 Dong Per person. We said no but got access anyway. We also turned down bike and sled rentals - just not worth the money. Scam for 300000+ / 20 min. Had a beautiful walk instead.
This is another must see? Many white people going crazy on 4 wheelers. I enjoyed the brief bit of foliage and lost looking dogs that you just want to feed, but it won't kill you if you miss it.

We went to some sand dunes to watch the sun rise a part of a short tour organised by a local hotel. It got off to a shaky start...advertised as jeep tour but we were actually squashed into a taxi with six others! Setting off down the road in the dark, the journey seemed long but probably took about 40mins. Our driver bypassed the 'entrance fee' and pulled up by a cafe, instructing us to walk and pointing. As it was still just getting light it became clear as we walked that we were headed to the top of a large dune to watch the sunrise. An increasing number of very noisy sand buggys/ATVs were bowling up and down the slopes from all directions, we felt it was dangerous at times as a mix of more practiced local guys delivered passengers at speed coupled with less experienced rental drivers wobbling through the crowd at some pace. It certainly isn't a tranquil experience...a bit of a 'been there, done that' one. Even here plastic and other rubbish is commonly seen.
Pretty...but expensive too expensive to rent 4x4. Also, kind of annoying that they make you pay 5000dong just to park your motorbike

Don't book with Kim Travel! don't pay more than 7$ for a sanddunes Trip For a 4 hour Trip to the sanddunes you should not pay more than 7$. beware of the agencys, who ripp you off. i booked with Kim Travel and they made me pay 350 000 Dong! i was squeezed in a car with 8 other people, each of them paid not more than 150000 Dong. don't ever book in Mui Ne with KIM Travel!!!!

SCAM ALERT at pretty dunes! Rented a motorbike from Mui Ne and drove the 40something minutes to the dunes, the road is fine the majority of the way but gets a bit tricky toward the end as it's very sandy and on a windy day sandgrains will be pelting into your eyes making it rather uncomfortable. On arrival we were asked to pay a 10,000dong entry fee per person and told to follow a man on a motorbike, BEWARE, DO NOT follow this man he is taking you directly to the scammy side of the dunes, making us turn left toward a different carpark than the main one straight ahead. We then had to pay again to park our bikes (when a girl in our group spoke in Vietnamese to some locals who had parked there they said they had parked for free). The man then took us directly to some very aggressive and pushy men in orange t-shirts who immediately started trying to sell us quad bikes for 400,000 dong for 20 mins per person, claiming there was no other way onto the dunes unless we wanted to walk an hour (not correct! the dunes are huge and all around, you can walk as much or as little as you want, would take maybe half an hour to get to the base of one of the bigger ones). Claiming there was no other way for us to get to the bigger dunes before sunset, the men eventually scammed us into hiring a jeep, 150,000 per person after haggling (in total 750,000dong) for a one hour drive around the dunes is what they told us. This was the biggest scam of all! After just 15 minutes in the jeep, which included 2 stops for photos which took up 10 minutes, we returned to where we started having seen basically none of the dunes. We of course complained, but as usual they didn't care, threw us out of the jeep and laughed in our faces. Just horrible and avoid at all costs!! Instead of turning left at the entrance fee point, go straight ahead to where all the tour groups are parking. We checked this afterward and here you are able to park for free and quad bikes are 300,000 for 20 minutes but you are able to have 2 people ride per quad bike at no extra cost. You can of course still also walk to the big dunes from here and it looks less spoilt and less full of quad bikes that you will have to jump out of the way of. We were alot less hassled and pushed to buy anything at this side. The dunes themselves are very pretty and quite spectacular, well worth seeing, however my experience of them was a bad one unfortunately due to a group of horrible individuals so please be aware! As an add on, the area where they will make you pay more/scam you is also full of some terrible animal cruelty. Lots of chickens running around with half their feathers plucked out, including the baby ones, geese being kicked by the women working there. I know it's Vietnam and this is almost expected but does not make it right and just added to the bad feeling of this place.

Never having seen sand dunes like this before it was interesting. We never rented a dune buggy or 4x4 because we were told by another couple that they break down & people don't get their money back. While out there, we witnessed a couple breaking down; him staying with the machine while she ran all the way back for help. Something wrong with that picture..there should be walkie talkies or some kind of communication with the 'office'.

Best thing to see in Mui Ne Went on a sunrise tour at 5am. There was quite a lot of people but it's not hard to walk a little further. We didn't rent an ATV, it was fine to walk and I didn't think the noise from them was that bad but most had already parked on top of a dune when we showed up. We paid 700,000 dong for a jeep tour for 2 visiting white + red sand dunes, fairy spring, fishing village, and a market. We found cheaper but liked this guy better. We left the white dunes (1st stop) 10 mins before schedule which meant that we had all the other stops to ourselves before the rest showed up

Further away from the city than the Red Sand Dunes, this is the first place where most of the tours take you. You come here to see the sunrise and hence have to wake up really early in the morning for the tour (I woke up at 4:30 in the morning!). The sand dunes are huge covering a vast amount of land. A good place to take photos especially at Sunrise. The early morning sun casts a golden hue to the sand making it look beautiful!

Took some great photos and slid down the dunes on the mats which was great, however be wary of any people(especially children) trying to help you (slide down, etc.) unless you are willing to give them money. I have no issue with donation or providing money for assistance given, however in this circumstance, after providing what we thought was a generous payment, my friend and I were followed, harassed, and yelled at after we refused to hand over more money.

White for photography Over all same as the red sand dunes. Suit for photography and took many good pictures of me. Went there in the morning and I think should be better for the view in the afternoon. Say around 2-4pm.
So far from the hotel. Not sure I'd do it again. Try the red sand dunes. They are closer to the city.

The white sand dunes are nothing special for me, but it's very different from the other landscapes in vietnam. It's a small area and you can compare it to the dunes at the Dutch coast. Its fun to visit when you have time left. In my opinion its not necessary to hire quads or a sledge. It works not that great and is an absolute waste of money. Definitely not a must.

The dunes themselves are beautiful, but the whole experience was spoilt by the shonky quad bikes. The gasket overheated very quickly and my husband and two kids were sprayed with hot, green coolant which was not pleasant and my little one walked back down the hill in tears. We argued till we finally got some money refunded. I told them I would give them a bad review...and here it is!!

Worth visit if your going yourself or pay $6 from hostel but if you've seen sand dunes before ie canaries/NZ/OZ etc its just same as any other place. Dune buggies look cool for but maybe spoil the tranquility of the place with all their noise and fume pollution

Be WARNED! Fantastic scenery BUT SCAMMING on the way back... As it's been 6 years that i come to Mui Né, the White Sand Dunes is one of my favorite activity when there is not wind enough to kite surf... Been there several times and saw it changing. In 2009 that place was a wild piece of nature... But then it became a trap... Even if the dunes will remind you Sahara if you've been there before. Two options to go there: By your own (rent a motorbike) or by tour (usually Jeeps) Easy to find as there is only one road to go there, the beautiful wild beaches on the road will help you to figure out how was Mui Né before the touristic invasion. Stop every 10 meters to take snapshots of these amazing part of Vietnam. The Red dunes are on the way, so is the Red Canyon, so you can visit them all in one day trip. But i have to mention that the Red dunes are more a kid playing place and the Red Canyon became the place to write your name on the hills in all languages you can imagine... By the way i do think that this disrespect behaviour is relevant of what is a "backpacker" is now... The White Dunes are just amazing! Just try to avoid rush hours, sunrise and sunset. Best time to visit is around 8am as the stupid people are still having their buffet breakfast on their hotels or recovering from the night before hangover... It's usually the same people that will ruin your pictures as they are too lazy to walk on the dunes and will rent quads for 30$ per 15minutes!!! BUT BE WARNED... If you go there by your own... Fill your motorbike tank full of gazoline BEFORE TO GO TO THE WHITES DUNES; AND DO NOT BUY SOME ON THE ONLY GAS STATION... Many tourist think that they won't have enough gazoline to go there and come back... Been there many times and had alway's more than enough to come back... The problem is that a that unique gas station is on the way you'll probably stop there to refill your tank. DON'T DO THAT! This place will charge you between 4 or 5 times the real price per liter... Sometimes more... They are too friendly to be honest... And even if i know most of the scams here... I have been scammed by these thieves too... Paying more than the price of a full tank for 2 litres! And unfortunately i heard the same sad story again again by whom have been there before. Actually 1 liter cost 21'000 VND... So how can 2 litres cost 180'000 VND???? Enjoy your trip to the Whites Dunes, have fun BUT DO NOT BUY GASOLINE THERE.

We went here on a morning trip from our hotel. Got there way to early but had the chance to actually sit down and breath before the sun came up. However, the sunrise was kind of destroyed by all the quadbike sounds but a nice view anyway. Had expected it be great but it is kind of small.

The sand dunes are quite impressive but the peace is shattered by very very loud quad bikes! It's still worth a visit and I guess the answer is to hire one of the quad bikes and make some racket yourself!

When you go there then early in the morning for the sun rise. The views are nice but it is to loud because they have a lot of quads and that is a terrible sound in the morning... every tourist jump in and destroy the quitness in the desert. On this place you can see what tourist are for the nature....

Parking Attendent Stole Our Helmets The sand dunes are beautiful (think Lawrence of arabia but on a much smaller scale, with quad tracks everywhere). Cost is 10,000 dong entry. Go early before the tour groups and subsequent ATV use starts. We decided to park on the road outside the dunes, and walked down. When we got to the entrance the parking attendant (an unsavory looking man) told us we had to park with him. We said no thanks, since we didn't feel like paying him, or walking back, and there were other motorbikes parked up there, so we felt safe. He seemed pretty angry and was insistent. When we got back, our helmets were gone. The attendant had marked a "0" on our bike. He was dismissive of us when we asked if he knew about our helmets. I had seen him go up to check on our bike when we were heading back, so I knew he was aware of what was going on. While we can't prove he stole our helmets, I can't think of anyone else who could have (it's on a pretty much empty road) and the way he reacted to us convinces me. I am surprised and saddened by such pettiness. So pay him what he asks for for parking and you should be fine, or carry your helmets with you.

Such a beautiful place spoil by quads We woke up very early (4am) And had a long ride by motorbike to go there, it took almost 1hr to us (but we drive very carefully), we asked the way to some local and finally found it! We arrived on time for the sun rise, we park, and walk through the entrance gate. Nobody was there to charges us the entrance fee at 5h30am (which is 10 000vnd). We had a short walk to reach the dunes, so beautiful, so big. . We thought it is going to be quiet (at 5h30am), but around 15 quads was turning around, down and up the dunes, it was terrible, they spoil everything, and make your walk dangerous ! But we enjoyed it, and recommend you to go by motorbike, much cheaper!

I wouldn't recommend it. We took a jeep tour which cost $9 and was extremely uncomfortable. It was a long bone-jarring drive to the white sand dunes. On arrival we paid a small entrance fee then were invited to pay 600,000 dong for a jeep to go up the sand dunes or take a quad bike. Instead we walked down to a lotus-filled dam and back up to the shop where we had an ice cream then took the long trip back. Some views were very pretty but not worth the long rough ride unless you really want to go quad-biking in the sand dunes. And if you do, wait for the discounted price.

After a very bumpy jeep ride we arrived at the white sand dunes. Quad biking was available to cover these vast dunes. We chose to walk. It was worth a visit, but we didn't feel the need to continue onto the Red dunes.

OK - Nothing to Write Home About I stopped at the White Sand Dunes while travelling from Mui Ne to Da Lat via motorbike. Had I specifically come to the White Sand Dunes (rather than it being conveniently located between the two cities), I would have been severely disappointed. After reading many reports of ATV's not working well and the brakes not working properly, we opted to hike the dunes by foot. The dunes were quite large and beautiful, and we spent a good 30 minutes exploring. Unlike the red dunes, I don't believe anyone was trying to rent "dune sleds". My advice - if it the dunes are convenient, definitely stop to have a look. However, don't go out of your way to come here (it is quite far from Mui Ne).
Bit of a tourist trap, quads are too expensive and in poor shape. Loose sadle, chain came off and you rent it for only 20 minutes for about 30usd, if I remember correctly. The dunes are nice though.

As part of jeep tour we were surprised by the white sands stop. As Australians we are very used to beautiful white beaches and surprised by novelty that others see them as. A little disappointed at bike riding over dunes and I was happy with opportunity to enjoy coconut juice and waterlilies.

Second part of the tour. Had to pay an entrance fee of 10,000 which wasn't explained beforehand by the tour guide however it's really cheap. Arrive at the bottom of the dunes. I have been to the Sahara desert so for me it was anything spectacular, however for others who haven't seen dunes before it's a lovely miniature version. What they also don't tell you is that you can pay between 200,000 to 400,000 to rent a quad bike for 20minutes! Unfortunately I didn't carry that amount with me on that day so couldn't hire one. It did look like lots of fun. I did however walk to the top of the biggest dune which isn't as much of a chore as it looks. Beware of the wind whipping your legs with the sand as it does hurt! Small cafe below for food, drinks and ice cream etc.

The White Sand Dunes are not really worth being seen! If you really want to see the White Sand Dunes, we recommend to book a 4-in-1-tour (for 8 US$ p. P.) combining the following attractions: If you have seen a "real" desert before, the white sand dunes won't impress you. We went there early in the morning to see the sunrise. It was nice, but not more. In addition the beauty of the white sand dunes is diminished by the traces and noise of the quad bikes (which can be hired for 20 US$/ 30Min) and the masses of tourists. 2) Mui Ne Harbor: HIGHLIGHT. Definitely worth being visited with its high amount of small, traditional painted fishing boats. 3) Red Sand Dunes: Smaller than the White Sand Dunes and even more touristy (because there is no entrance fee and the Red Sand Dunes are very close to Mui Ne) 4) Fairy Spring: It is a lot of fun and refreshing to walk barefooted through the river with its scenic riversides. Due to the fact that 3 out of the 4 attractions mentioned above are "entrance free", the 8 US$ is mainly paid for the shuttle service (in a 7-seater-Jeep).

Not really white, but still impressive Lots and lots of sand dunes. And obviously, lots of visitors and very noisy four-wheel drives. Jeep tour to the dunes was painful. Better to book your own than to take some of the cheap group tours which use very old vehicles and squeeze in too many people.

Needed...good fitness level A good bike ride out of Mui Ne, and you arrive at the White Sand Dunes, which are well named. As you arrive the locals swarm to entice you to their stall. You purchase a piece of plastic stuff with a handle or two, walk across the road and begin the very strenuous (for the fit only) walk up to the top of the Sand Dune. Once you arrive at the top you realise that if you decide to ride the plastic to the bottom of this very big pile of sand you then have to trek back up to the top again, and this side is a lot longer than the one you just walked up & its very hot today. The decision you are faced with is quite tormenting. Kids don't care they love it, but have a good degree of fitness if you decide to do it.

As stated, the view was stunning but the journey by jeep was very long. we paid $7 USD per pax for the private jeep tour. compare the prices in mui ne before deciding on which tour to join. the tour we had was with the fairy stream. not recommended to go as there was nothing much to be seen. sunset was very nice at the dunes, do get shades as the sand flying around would most definitely whack u in the face.

paid for a tour of white and red dunes, fishing village and fairy stream - 6$. most of these are easily accessible by moped for 12$ a day apart from the white dunes its a bit further out. Least if you get a moped you have more time, if you go on a tour you have 45 mins. It's ruined by quads too, but is funny watching people get stuck. We got to watch sunrise there, very nice.

The white sandunes is nice, you can have some lovely Pictures, especially before you come to the sandunes (before the lake), and if you haven't visited sandunes or desert before, it could be a nice experience. Where you start walking to the sandunes is a small cafe where you can rent scooters. Unfortunately this place also tied up a monkey for tourist entertainment. I really hate the way they treat animals in this part of Vietnam. All these small monkeys in short chains to attract tourists. It gives me the creeps.

A great place going down Our last visit to the White Sand Dunes a few years ago was a delight. The sand dunes was pristine, no entrance fees and not too crowded. Now they offers sand buggy thrill tours and the sand dunes are littered with debris. It's like having seadoos and motor boats roaring all day on a pristine mountain lake! At least, try to visit very early in the morning before the tour bus crowd and the thrill seekers!

Very scenic, enjoyable ride to get there Dunes let down by money hungry locals. We hired a scooter to the sand dunes for $6 or something. The dunes were massive and I was taken aback by the scenery (probably due to them being the first sand dunes I've visited). Everything was fantastic aside from the loud quad bikes and the local idiots showing off their acceleration skillzzzz. I saw a number of angry tourist couples that had hired the quad bikes (for alot). Only to have the locals come over and kick one of them off to give the other member (usually male) a "thrill ride". It just looked annoying and inconvenient. After driving back to the other member I heard a few them say "why the hell did you do that, that's half our time gone" etc. My advice, travel there by motorbike/scooter and enjoy the surroundings on the way. Avoid the quad bikes, they are an overpriced nuisance run by losers.

spoilt by the dune buggys Coming from Australia I am spoilt for long stretches of white sand dunes so I was a little disappointed with these. Whilst the area is nice it was spoilt by the noise of the dune buggy and the smell of fuel. It is quite easy to walk up and down the dunes which overlook a small man-made lake.

You can definitly do the trip by your own. We rented a bike for 6 $ at phuong nam office and drove there about half an our. It's really no problem and easy to find. So don' t believe the vietnamese layers. One woman from the restaurant song huong told us "oh please don't rent a bike because foreigners aren't allowed to drive in mui ne and especially outside of mui ne the police will stop you and you have to pay more than 100 $ but you can book the trip to the sand dunes with me"..... bla bla bla. So don' t believe any vietnamese it's all about money. We even didn' t see one police man. The sand dunes are nice to see but nothing special if you are there because of the local people which built there some bad looking houses and live there and make money with the entree fees. And please don't rent a "board" they don't work. We rented some and it was just a big joke and it is just to make money we tried them and it didn't work in any way. In total there is to say that except the sand dunes there is nothing special to see im mui ne expect a lot of drunken russians who destroy the vietnamese culture. I would recommend you to rent a room in a hotel which has a private beach because you have no chance to go swimming. The hotels are all close to the sea and there is no public beach.
A small desert in the middle of nowhere. You can take a walk around or rent an ATV an take a drive. Nice to see, but after half an hour it´s time to go home.

We went to the white sand dunes as part of a tour package. We hired the quads however I wish we hadn't bothered. The quads were in poor condition and often got stuck in the sand resulting in me having to pay one of the kids to get it out! I would defo walk up the dunes. It is a beautiful sight but not anything spectacular. It was much cleaner then the red sand dunes!

I'm a 30 years old female solo traveler and after reading the comments here decided to go by myself and avoid the tours. It takes 40 min on motorbike (7$) from Mui Ne tourist center to get there. Went in the morning because was a cloudy day for sunrise and there was nobody. It takes half a tank to get there, 2$. The road is safe and good and you can stop anywhere you want to make pictures! Using google maps tp find it was very easy. Recommend! #traveleca

Not pleasant short trip The place is big. Nature scenes, good. But there are too many "proactive" guys selling for their renting of motorbikes. And the price is high. My bike flipped, with my kid & myself. The driver was riding too dangours. My kid was scared. It was not 100% safe. It depends on the driver u have. We just quickly suspended out ride, and go back to hotel. The driver was trying to say something for me at the end. I thought he was trying to say sorry. But actually he was asking for tips. No, he does not deserve that.

Quite enjoyed this little trip :) nice big pretty dunes, that have been well looked after by the locals, clean and litter free - odd for Vietnam. We decided not to do the buggy option and enjoyed ourselves enough without :) There was a little surprise when we realised there was a man offering ostrich rides – sounds bizarre but it was something I had wanted to do back at Saigon but didn’t have time and wolah! For a little pocket change – I got my wish. He even had saddles for them. Bizarre, uncomfortable and a little scary – but fun. If you do go please remember that sand reflects heat and there us next to no shade – if you can pick a cooler day, take a bottle of water with you (or buy one out there before heading out into the sand, it’s a bit of a walk back to the kiosk) and remember the sunscreen or you will fry yourself.

Shonky and Good at the same time Basically a big sand dune and people have fun going up and down it. You can be driven in a quad bike or jeep and get bumped about on the way. You will pay to access the place and pay again for the ride up the dune and pay again for a nice meal. At least the meal was nice and had good seafood. Prowling kitten at table eating leftovers was a bit off though.Toilets were of usual standard i.e. smelly and dirty. People going up the dune on the back of a Quad bike were having fun but the health and safety standards are lacking.
wasnt what we expected wasnt what we expected, not amazing we woke uo at 4 to see the sunrise, totally just ok .... wouldnt spend money on it,
If you have seen one sand dune you have seen them all. The quad bike operators here have never heard of safety issues

Drove from phan thiet 1 and 1/2 hours to get there. Walked up 15 minutes, Got nice pictures, walked down. The end. If u have seen sand dunes elsewhere you can skip the trip altogether because you would not be impressed. Btw the 'bathroom' at the entrance consists of a stall with a floor toilet and a corrugated iron door that does not lock. You would think due to the large number of tourists they receive they would invest in basic amenities.

Not much to do here unless you want to use the buggies We arrived in mid afternoon with hot overhead sun, and were offered very expensive buggies to go up the dunes (or walk for 2km instead). There was not much else to do there! I ended up sitting by the lake and then just having a fresh coconut waiting for my group to be ready to go.

Nice dunes, good views, fun 4-wheeling - not mind-blowing but worth the time. The White Sand dunes are lovely with several nice spots for photos. There is a cafe at the foot of the dunes and a gang of kids renting 4-wheelers. If you decide to take one for a spin, one of the kids will ride on the back with you and act as a pseudo-guide or get your 4-wheeler out of the sand if you get stuck. The 4-wheelers are in bad shape but it is a decent tour and will get you up the hills. As I have been to desolate dunes in the Sahara, it is hard to appreciate these dunes as there are many tourists around. Be warned, the kid will insist on a tip after the ride. The sublely in tipping is completely lost on these kids and when you are getting off the 4-wheeler, they will start chanting "tip, tip, tip." Yes, I got it, you want a tip. If you have the time, the drive out to the dunes is nice and the dunes are worth seeing.

Went as part of a $7 tour out of Mui Ne backpackers. Rent a bike and follow the jeeps that leave from there around 1:30pm every day. You'll save money and have the freedom to leave without getting stuck in a rust bucket. The dunes are ok but not a must do. Skip the plastic sleds - they don't work. And for 300K dong you can ride a 4 wheeler in the dunes for 30 minutes. They're shoddy but not bad for $15.

Coming from Australia, it wasn't that different to what we have in WA. Maybe a 40 minute drive from Mui Ne and when you get there they will try and charge you 300,000 VND for a 4WD Motorbike to have a look. But we walked around, very hot but now I've seen them.

A welcomed change of scenery You don't really expect this kind of scenery in the middle of the greenish hills... The dunes just appear from nowhere and that's what actually makes them worth the drive. It's a good 45 min drive from Phan Thiet, the road is quite nice, there's a lovely view over the coast, nice, empty beaches and rough coastline. It was a very, very hot day, I guess going there in the middle of the day is not the best, better going at dusk or dawn, the colours must also be amazing with the sun setting over them. Worth the visit, yes!

The dunes were a long ride from Mui Ne main beach where most resorts are. The taxi cost us about US$35 one way to get there and took around 45mins. Once there, we paid US$1 to enterbthe area and opted to walk the 1km from the car park to the foothills of the dune. There is an option to ride a golf buggy there for US$7 one way if it's too hot or far. The dunes are big - if you've never been to the desert or seen big dunes it's worth the effort. We rode plastic sheets down the dunes which was fun too. We declined the offer to ride ostriches - an inhumane money making scheme. If you hire a private taxi you can also stop at the yellow dunes and a beautiful fishing village too. Private taxi is convenient and you can go at your own pace but if you want to save money, book a cheaper tour from your hotel or a provider in Mui Ne.

We had a bad experience with this company. They promise things and don't make it happen. They charge way to much, because it should cost $25 for 4 persons (maximum)in 1 jeep. We ended up with 5 persons in 1 jeep this morning and we payed way to much. They where not able to have a descent conversation about it afterwards. The sanddunes where ok but not spectacular. The sun was already up when we arrived early this morning. But we really liked the fairy stream!

Extremely average. Paid $12 for a trip here (along with the red sand dunes, fishing village etc).. The landscape of just sand dunes is ruined by quad bikes racing around. However if you do go, you will be stopped at the white sand dunes by some men asking for 10,000 dong to enter the park! You do my have to pay this, it is included in your ticket. So when you turn up, simply say your not paying it as you've paid for a ticket. Will take a minute or two for the men to let you through, but it is what our driver told us to do, and it worked!!

Playground for kids ;-) Mui Ne is bloody hot, and thus the sand is hot as well. Make sure you are wearing sneakers and long pants!! Nice attraction, go there by bike and not with a tour, much better! Try to skate of the sand dunes (only possible when your weight is not more than 50-60 kgs ;)). had a very nice morning there!
we went here and just got the sand sledges but next time would get a quad as was tiring walking and sledges barely moved. haggle and get a good quad as they try and rip you off.

Tobogganing on desert sand! Well the view is pretty interesting of these sand dunes in the middle of nowhere... Hard to get to and very rough roads... riding down the steep hill is underwhelming as the speed is quite slow.
We went around the noon time but its better to go during the evening as otherwise the sand will feel intensely hot! It's more or less just like numerous dunes with a flaky whitish color.

You're not going to be blown away, but you may say "oh this is pretty nice". It's probably the top attraction to see on any 'tours' around Mui Ne, but even at that.. its not spectacular; certainly better than the red dunes though. The jeep tours we saw made 5 hours out of going to various sights, but after seeing a couple myself and hearing of others... I'd recommend just getting a motorbike (if you are capable) and just going straight to the white sand dunes and back. Save yourself on the jeep tour cost. The people at the entrance try to hire out quads etc., they told us it was very far to walk... its not at all. It depends on how far you want to go obviously, but anything from a ten minute walk to an hour gets you pretty much everything here.
The white sands dune are very not bad but what's more beautiful are the lakes with lillis in at the foothill of the dunes.

We booked a Jeep Tour through our hotel (Grace Boutique Resort). The owner (Van) does not make any money from the tours and told us he had gone through several operators to make sure his guests got a good experience. The guy that took us was friendly enough and a little English. The dunes are pretty cool. If you bring kids (or like to have fun) rent one of the homemade boards from the kid at the entrance. The right price is 10.000 VND. The walk up in the Dunes is really nice and the view from the top is great. One thing I would warn about is renting the ATVs (Quads) to drive in the Dunes. As an experienced ATV rider and ATV shop owner the ATVs they had were nothing short of dangerous. They had all been flipped several times, had incorrect steering, cracked fenders etc. The very aggressive and rude ATV kids will also tell you that it is OK to ride 2-3 and 4 people on one ATV. Dont even ride one. You can get them down to 10 USD for 30 minutes, which is pretty cheap but I suggest you dont do it unless you want to risk an accident on the cheap Chinese bikes. In addition, your experience will be a lot better without excessive noise and smoke from the badly adjusted engines. Still, the White Dunes are worth the 25 USD trip from Mui Ne. On another note, don't bother too much with the Red Dunes that are closer to Mui Ne. Have a look at the really agressive 8-12 year old kids that want to rent you a board for sliding. They probably make a lot more money than their parents and seem to spend them on smoking cigarettes while yelling in their mobile phones. Not a pretty sight.

Sand dunes nice enough, and worth a look The sand dunes were pretty for a visit and we had a 20 min Quad bike ride. Lots of fun and worth a trip, as there aren't that many touristy things to do in Mui Ne. The advertised rate at the front entrance was $20usd per person for 20 min, but 100 metres through the trees, we got a 20 min ride for 200,000 (about 25% of the cost).
If you've been to other dunes, than White San Dunes are not worth visitng. We rented quads, but spent only 20 minutes using them and local staff was driving with us! No free driving at all.

Spectacular view for sunrise and sunset White Sand Dunes is worth a visit especially to catch the sunrise and sunset picture, but half and hours is all you need if you don't intent to take the 45 minute tour ride around the sand dunes by the 4-wheel bike ride which cost you a fortune. If you want the ride, please make sure you are good in bargain. If possible don't rent the sand surfing board from the kids, I personally find that this board is not safe and certainly you will have fun when surfing down on the high point, but think about it, you have to climb up all the way back to the point. Make sure your belonging is safely keep by your friend and someone you trusted and not by those kids. Admission to this white sand dunes is free, not sure in the future. Sand is fine and and the place is windy. Do equipped yourself with sun lotion, sun glasses and a good pair of shoes or sandals.

We took the time to go 'all the way' out there and where disappointed as it is an activity for the young, not the young at heart. There is little enjoyment in looking at a pile of sand, unless you are going to hire your piece of flex-board, walk a klm or 2, slide down the hill and wlk back again. Sorry not for us and the younger one's said, once done, no need to go back.
The special motorbikes and jeeps which bring the tourists up and down would destroy the dunes with their noise and smoke.

Beautiful spot to visit, and a must if you go to Mui Ne. Grab a guide, and grab a jeep and hit the road and see it. Don't bother with the slide that the locals will try to rent you, doesn't work too well on the sand.

Just a bunch of sand dunes with lots of locals trying to sell you something. Worth a look & a go on a slide, but wouldn't feel I'd missed anything if I hadn't gone. A quick half hour visit is all you need . . . unless you've got children, in which case you'll be there all day !

Over Touted (but good for snaps) Physically, its a beautiful place - but there are just so many people selling so many things...it just loses its charm. They should regulate this place to maintain it - wouldnt mind paying an entry fee if it helps to do that (Right now its free). I used to live in Dubai so the sand duning etc isnt very new - but they sure can learn a few things to implement here.

Much better than the red dunes, but really: dunes are dunes, right? Beyond the very special set of circumstances that permit these dunes to exist amid one of the wettest areas in the region, there is nothing ultimately neat about it on a global level. Yes, the dunes are interesting, but the area is small and it is far less impressive than a full size desert. The dunes are pretty far away (22 miles) from the central strip, I rented a bike and it took me awhile to find the place due to zero signposting, had to keep asking locals where I should be going. I think a jeep tour would be best, it would cost the same price (remember, you might be able to find motorbikes for 6 USD per day, but you still have to pay for gas). You can find jeep tours at 10 USD, cheaper than it is to rent a bike and be responsible for gasoline, and they likely will be more timely given they know how to get there all ready.
Very white sand Dunes on a 45 minutes drive from Mui Ne. Though far away, they are more impressive thatn the red dunes

once and that's enough we rented bike and found themselves.be careful if you want park your bike near the cafe ,local says that they look for your bike for free but after finishing of your excursion will demand money for their looking

We took a jeep with a driver and went to the dunes. On the way there we visited lotus lake (lake with lotus flowers growing around it; good view, but only in a month when lotus blooms; loads of litter around, i.e. plastic bottles, etc) and red canyon (nothing special, 5 minutes is enough). The dunes themselves are nice, the view is great.

I visited the sand dunes as part of a jeep tour which me and my friends pre-booked. It was a 30 minute ride from Mui Ne to the sand dunes to catch the sunrise. The entire experience seemed rather ordinary to me. The sand dunes themselves don't take up a large area and are rather unimpressive. At the dunes, we rented ATV's for 300,000VND for 20 minutes of riding. I guess some might find zooming around a small area in an ATV fun but i felt it was rather boring. Furthermore, you can't go too far as its only 20 minutes and after 10 minutes of riding, the rental shop people will come and find you to ensure you don't wander off too far. I felt the entire experience was overpriced. I recommend to skip the sand dunes entirely, but if you're in Mui Ne already, you might as well make the effort to see the sand dunes since there's nothing else to do in that town (skip the ATV ride though and save yourself the money).

Visited the white sand dunes as part of a jeep tour organised by the resort that I was staying in. The white sand dunes were a good 45 minute drive from the main stretch of resorts along Mui Ne. The sand dunes themselves are not particularly unique (especially if you happen to have travelled to the middle east) but seeing their existence in the tropics is quite interesting. There is a lake surrounded by greenery nearby and this provided for some nice photography opportunities. A few of my friends hired the ATV (but complained thereafter that it had insufficient power to climb up the bigger slopes) while the rest of us walked. The winds were pretty strong and this meant that the hot sand stung us (all of us were unsurprisingly in shorts). Well worth a visit if you're got time on your hands, but otherwise you're probably better off spending your precious hours lazing around at/by the beach.

nice dunes but be aware of children I went there and thought it would be a nice experience. However, some children offered me to play sand sliding with VND50,000. They by turn helped me to do it and also helped u keep ur shoes. So much fun but then when i paid, they told me pay them VND50,000 each. So I ended up paying them VND300,000 or they wouldn't give me back my shoes. Even the dunes is nice with spectacular view but I will never come back. If you go there, just ignore these children.

An interesting experience This is an interesting way to spend a hour or 2. They bill it as Eco tourism but it is anything but, with quad bikes and jeeps charging up the dunes. It is very hot so make sure you cover up / use lots of sun block. We hired boards to surf down the dunes but ended up not using them.

It is nice but nothing really special. Locals offer you a quad for 30 minutes for $20. People who took it got to the top of the nearby dune, then the quads went dead. In about 20 minutes some guys to repair it arrived, quickly started them and the time was over. The sunrise did not present any special view, the photos aftyer it are much better, as with the bright sun the dunes give sharp shades and become more graphical.

Nice to see, but rather long drive Once at the white dunes it was very nice, looking almost surreal. We rented mopeds to get there, which was quite a long (and hot) drive. Before walking up the dunes you'll be asked if you want to rent a 4wheel to get there for an awful lot of money. Don't rent it since it's just a few minutes walk till you get to the dunes and a few more to get up to the top of the nearest one. Nice and surreal to look at, but as mentioned quite a long drive to get there for 45 minutes of walking on the sand.

Nice natural wonder, but spoiled by kids I would have reviewed this spot with a better rating for its deserved natural beauty, but the experience was spoiled by local kids. We arrived by motorbike early the morning to avoid the crowds, payed the guy at the parking lot and ventured into the dunes. The place is very nice, unusual for SE Asia. We shoot nice pics and had fun. However, if it's a big desert you are looking for you might be disappointed. But we weren't disappointed at all since we knew exactly what we where going to see: a little magical sandy wonder in the otherwise green Vietnam. At the arrival we didn't hire the dune-buggy and sleds, this might have upset the kids there, because we had to pay it later: on the way back we almost had an accident because the front tyre was half deflated. We had to find help in the nearby village. We asked the guy who helped to pump up the tyre if repair was necessary and he said the tyre had no problem, it was just been deflated by someone. This was true indeed, since we had no problems at all afterwards. Who did this joke was an irresponsible idiot, probably one of the guys at the white sands parking lot who likes to play pranks (dangerous ones). Check your bike when you leave the place, you might have a surprise.
we made a trip with an old jeep, visiting the white sands! interesting but no so have too see!

White sands: worth the visit I took a motorbike to go there. Not very easy to find the way if you do not have a map! 30kms from Mui Ne beach and average speed can be low as I got strong wind on the way up. Nice to visit as it is like you are at the middle of the desert. 1 hour walk in the dunes is sufficient. Do not talk to the teens trying to sell you their bikes: they can be aggressive if they feel you could be interested in their stuff. Be firm and everything will be right.

Beautiful, but too many peddlers The sand dunes are beautiful, but unfortunately there are a lot of peddlers waiting to fleece you. Sand surfing is absolutely pointless, because its a pain to walk up those huge dunes. I would recommend the ATVs but they are a little steep price wise ($10 for 20 mins...which is a lot in Viet) but they kept yelling at us to stay within course. But from a scenic perspective it was great. The white dunes also have a Lotus Lake. Please do not heed the tour guides who keep talking about how pretty it is. Its basically a small pond with a few lotus flowers. Nothing impressive at all.

Take care of children beggin!! Very Nice nature experience, but children who want your money and want you to rent kiteboardings are very annoying and actually it did ruin my experience very much. They keep following you even though I told Them not to.

40 minute drive from mui ne central beach. Amazing to see. No toilet out there so go before you leave. Take water bottles. Not sure if motorbikes worthwhile as they kept getting stuck. Kids loved it. Best to go on the 2pm trip so you are not there in the full heat of the day. We got there about 4pm after stopping at fairy springs on the way.

Good tour but beware of the children! Myself & 3 friends went in a tour to see the sand dunes. The drive there was interesting as they are in quite a remote area. When we arrived there were lots of children selling blue sheets so you can sledge down the dunes-I wouldn't bother with these as it us virtually impossible to sledge down them, you just get stick & the dunes are pretty small. We did however hire 2quads which cost 200000 dong each...bit pricey for 20 mins but were without a doubt the highlight. Good fun & saved walking! :) the dunes themselves were beautiful & worth going to visit however it was really windy & the sand hitting off every inch of my body was painful. After 20 minutes spinning around on the quad we went to return them and the little boy who hired them to us started demanding more money...he claimed we had been longer then 20 mins. We refused to pay and left on a pretty bad note. The children at the white & orange dunes are extremely pushy & I would recommend that you avoid them if possible or stand your ground when they start demanding money when you know it's unnecessary. The dunes are really lovely & it is unfortunate that these children are spoiling the experience.

Its a big sand dune, windy and okay for a look. My wife and I headed out to the White Sand Dunes which are about 40 minutes ride out from Mui Ne (so NOT the red closer in dunes). We hired a semi-auto motorbike for the day at $5 (they wanted $10 for an auto!!) and took the road north. Easy ride, not too much traffic some pretty great scenery along the way. Once we located the Dunes (not hard, just google maps it) we paid the 5000 dong to the bike parking guy which was no drama. We headed into the cafe area and towards the dunes and spotted Ostirch Riding for $1.25 which looked pretty funny, my wife wimped out though and I would probably have broken it as i weigh 100KG. As we headed to the dunes we spotted quadbike hire, 150,000d for 20 minutes which YES is expensive by Vietnam standards but only $7.50 so not really THAT bad and we had a good time until my wife's quad broke down about 15 minutes in!! We just left it there and I doubled her back. Could not get a refund despite trying, the punk little 13 yo kid was trying to be tough about it. I laughed at him as i am 6'5" and really was not worried about his 2 10yo mates haha. So not a bad day, but there isnt really much to do but look around. I've seen sand in the past so probably would not head back there. BUT as mentioned the ride there was great! Advice : Don't be scared by little brat kids and stand your ground.

We visited as part of a half day jeep tour as a family of 3 - a couple plus 7yr old child. We paid to hire ATV. My husband paid extra to drive himself and then myself and daughter opted for a driver to drive us on the sand dunes. It is a beautiful area and fun to ride on the ATVs. You can also sled on the sand dunes for 50k VND. There are stalls selling goods and refreshments and toilet facilities here.
Scenery is beautiful especially during sunset and not expensive quads make the experience even better. However when it’s windy this open space is pretty extreme😝 Is it a must-do though? I’d say no.

Tour of the "small Sahara" of Vietnam We had booked a guided tour where the White Sand Dunes were also approached. We had the choice there whether to drive the dunes by off-road vehicle or by quad bike. In both variants you do not drive yourself, but you are assigned a driver there. We had decided to go on the tour with the quad bike. The three of us then went over the dunes. In between, our driver stopped again and again so that we could take photos. We had a very nice driver who also questioned how "brave" we are? There are smaller, but sometimes also quite high and steep dunes. The drive through the dunes was really fun, but could have been longer. Automatically translated

Like in the "real desert" If you drive from the north, for example from Ninh Chu towards Mui Ne/Phan Thiet, various dune formations begin about 80 km before Mui Ne. Many of them are not immediately recognizable as such because they are overgrown, in some cases almost completely overgrown. Shortly before Mui Ne they appear in all their glory and are reminiscent of dunes such as those found in the United Arab Emirates. However, a lot has changed since our last visit here, about 25 years ago. Dunes have been leveled to build hotels there.

The white sand dunes are visible from the road with a body of water in the foreground but access to the site requires a fee and then it is better to rent a quad or a Jeep to fully explore the area. Getting there on foot must be exhausting and dangerous with vehicles speeding in all directions and some drivers apparently taking pleasure in scaring their passengers. We had a 4x4 marked Trieu Trang and made 2 stops to take nice photos. The wind was quite violent during our excursion; It is recommended to bring glasses, why not a mask and to wear your headgear. Beautiful views despite the presence of wind turbines on one side. In terms of security it is borderline and I hardly dare imagine the situation in high season with more visitors.
Nice day with white sand dunes.Fun but too short.We will comeback again.The best time to watch sunset here

We had 6 people in our group so we took a 4 runner can fit up to 8 people and it cost per car The drive was fun and exciting but too short of the drive We didn’t see lotus flowers because not in season so we only saw the lake Tip: go early in the morning and not during summer time otherwise it’ll be very hot

Fun to visit and take a ride with quad bikes The coolest thing was riding with the quad bikes up to the dunes and then down a steep slope. However it doesn't seem super safe, but fun. Otherwise sot much else to see, but sand

Had a great visit to the dunes and timed it just right for a 430pm start to avoid the heat and allow us to see the sunset. Next time prob not take our one year old but kids over 4 it is manageable. There is lots of activities at the dunes but one watch out is just keep an eye on all the 4x4s flying around. Had a ball and will be back!!!

Mui Ne is just a 4-hour train ride away from Ho Chi Minh, about 200 Kilometers away, but it feels like you’re exploring altogether a different world. It makes it a famous weekend getaway from Ho Chi Minh City, with a busy streak of hotels, restaurants, and stores. In the last 20 years, Mui Ne has witnessed some significant growth.
Nice view and excited motor riding View is good , a beautiful place ; motor riding will bring you excited experience, but quite expensive.

Nice place, price to get to the top too expensive I went to the white sand dunes with my boyfriend without booking any tour. It was overall a very good experience even though the price to get to the top by quad was too expensive. However, I recommend it

We arrived at the White sand dunes and were surprised at the cost to hire a private jeep or quad bike. (Jeep- 800,000 and quad 1milion vnd). You fo not have to pay to enter and there is a small cafe selling local food and snacks etc. We chose to walk the dunes instead which we could do freely. The time we arrived (11.30) meant we had missed all the organised sunrise and sunset tours so had the dunes almost to ourselves. It was however very hot. We were travelling with 3 children (2x 7 year olds and a 3 year old). The older children made it to the top of the dune which was about a 15 minute walk. Once on top they loved throwing themselves down into the sand and rolling down. The youngest walked for about 10 minutes but loved exploring and feeling the soft sand. We had taken good advice and arrived in trainers which was vital as the sand was incredibly hot. Would highly recommend visiting.
Spotlight of Phan Thiet We realize that white sand dunes is a spotlight of Phan Thiet beside nice beach.You should discover by jeep car

Good but not suitable for kids It has been pretty astonishing to see such a big desert right beside a lake. Ticket is 300,000 VND per person. And sand skiing costs 50,000 VND. On the top, it was very windy. So if you have kids under 3, you might not enjoy yourself very much

If you have never been to the Sahara desert, you should definitely go to see the white sands. I found them truly fascinating! The sands are really white, shining, so gorgeous in the sun :) The sand itself is very delicate and it’s fun to walk in and enjoy all its curves and bumps. We took a jeep to take us around which was fun but walking on foot is also a good option. A beautiful place I am glad we visited :)

Really enjoyed the ATV quad biking over the dunes. They were bigger than I expected. I was disappointed that to ride the bigger quad the guide had to come with us on the quad. I was looking forward to going by myself or just me and my partner. However, we both had turns of driving and the guide had a turn & took us over some super steep sand dunes and offered to take our photo at some nice spots (all for a tip at the end of course). The quad was more expensive than I had read online. It was 800,000 (35USD) for 2 pax on the big quad for 20 minutes. We went around sunset and it was really beautiful. Little bit awkward to find and took us 45 minutes to drive there but worth it for sure.
When visiting a sea beach this is the last thing that you expect to see.The view is amazing and breathtaking.
What a fun place to be if hire quadbikes. Experience but what fun.Self drive is very important. But this place is very busy.

The White Sand Dunes is worth a visit. We rode with a guy on an ATV, which made the trip a little more fun for us. We probably paid him too much ( VND 650 000,-) for about 5 active minutes on the vehicle, so be aware of that when you go there. Other than that we spent a total of 30 minutes there.
Great fun and great views From the 4x4 ride towards the top of the dunes to the climb to the highest point and its views, this is a really fun area to explore.

Worth a visit but quite crowded with jeeps and ATVs We did a early morning trip to the sand dunes. The dunes themselves are quite nice to see, but since all tours show up at roughly the same time, there are a lot of people around. They offer rides by jeeps and ATVs so there’s a lot of noise and tire tracks everywhere - would have been nicer to see untouched sand dunes, but it is what it is...

Be prepared for windy sands! Visited the white sands dune at about 3pm. Atv cost vnd 300,000 for a being pillion rider, sends you 2 locations - to the highest dune and near to the lake. Its windy up in the highest dune whereby the tiny bits of sands will blow along with the wind. I could feel my leg stingy pain as i was wearing shorts, luckily i had my sunglasses on else sand would be in my eyes. I have observed the workers covered their faces with cloth or head gears! Do not wear thinly scarfs or hats that loose, it will be blown away by the strong winds. A jeep costs vnd 600,000. I have uploaded a picture i took riding behind the driver!

Came here as part of our Mui Ne tour from Nha Trang. Once arrived you can choose to rent a jeep (VND 1,500,000 for 4 to 5 persons) or an ATV (VND 1,100,000 for 2 person). You can also walk up there if you want to.
